title: Speculative Decoding Scaling Laws (SDSL): Throughput Optimization Made Simple
summary: Speculative decoding is a technique that uses multiple language models to accelerate infer- ence. Previous works have used an experi- mental approach to optimize the throughput of the inference pipeline, which involves LLM training and can be costly. This study of spec- ulative decoding proposes a theory that ana- lytically connects the key hyperparameters of pre-trained LLMs to the throughput efficiency of a downstream SD-based inference system. The theory allows the prediction of throughput- optimal hyperparameters for the components of an inference system before their pre-training.

AI.tldr: They develop a theoretical framework for speculative decoding that links LLM hyperparameters to inference throughput, enabling prediction of optimal settings without costly empirical search or retraining.
AI.motivation: Speculative decoding can accelerate language model inference, but current optimization of its throughput relies on expensive, trial-and-error empirical approaches that require training or adapting models. A principled way to analytically predict optimal hyperparameters for speculative decoding systems before model training is missing.
AI.method: They construct a theory of speculative decoding that analytically relates key hyperparameters of pre-trained large language models to the throughput behavior of a speculative decoding inference pipeline. This involves modeling the interaction between a main model and auxiliary components and deriving formulas or conditions for throughput-optimal hyperparameter choices.
AI.result: The theoretical framework provides explicit relationships between LLM hyperparameters and the throughput of speculative decoding systems, making it possible to compute or predict which hyperparameter settings will maximize throughput for a given system configuration before actually pre-training the models.
AI.conclusion: By providing an analytic link between LLM hyperparameters and speculative decoding throughput, the work enables principled, prediction-based selection of throughput-optimal hyperparameters, reducing the need for costly experimental search and making SD-based inference system design more efficient.

title: Summarize Before You Speak with ARACH: A Training-Free Inference-Time Plug-In for Enhancing LLMs via Global Attention Reallocation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) achieve remarkable performance, yet further gains often require costly training. This has motivated growing interest in post-training techniques-especially training-free approaches that improve models at inference time without updating weights. Most training-free methods treat the model as a black box and improve outputs via input/output-level interventions, such as prompt design and test-time scaling through repeated sampling, reranking/verification, or search. In contrast, they rarely offer a plug-and-play mechanism to intervene in a model's internal computation. We propose ARACH(Attention Reallocation via an Adaptive Context Hub), a training-free inference-time plug-in that augments LLMs with an adaptive context hub to aggregate context and reallocate attention. Extensive experiments across multiple language modeling tasks show consistent improvements with modest inference overhead and no parameter updates. Attention analyses further suggest that ARACH mitigates the attention sink phenomenon. These results indicate that engineering a model's internal computation offers a distinct inference-time strategy, fundamentally different from both prompt-based test-time methods and training-based post-training approaches.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ARACH, a training-free, inference-time plug-in that reallocates attention in large language models via an adaptive context hub, improving performance without updating model parameters.
AI.motivation: Further improving large language models typically requires expensive training, motivating interest in post-training, especially training-free, methods that work at inference time. Existing training-free approaches mostly treat models as black boxes and operate only on inputs/outputs (e.g., prompting, sampling, reranking), lacking a simple way to intervene in internal computations. The authors aim to develop a plug-and-play mechanism to directly modify internal attention behavior at inference time without retraining.
AI.method: The authors design ARACH (Attention Reallocation via an Adaptive Context Hub), a training-free, inference-time plug-in for LLMs. ARACH augments the model with an adaptive context hub that aggregates contextual information and then redistributes or reallocates attention across tokens during inference, altering internal attention patterns without changing model weights. It is integrated into existing LLM architectures as a plug-and-play module, incurring only modest extra computation.
AI.result: Across multiple language modeling tasks, applying ARACH consistently improves model performance compared to baselines without ARACH, while adding only modest inference overhead and requiring no parameter updates. Attention pattern analysis shows that ARACH changes how attention is distributed, reducing the so-called attention sink phenomenon where attention is wasted on uninformative tokens.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that directly engineering a model’s internal computations at inference time, via a plug-in like ARACH, is an effective and distinct strategy from both prompt-based test-time methods and training-based post-training approaches. ARACH’s ability to mitigate attention sinks and yield consistent gains without retraining suggests a promising new direction for enhancing LLMs through internal, training-free interventions.

title: DeReason: A Difficulty-Aware Curriculum Improves Decoupled SFT-then-RL Training for General Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for eliciting reasoning capabilities in large language models, particularly in mathematics and coding. While recent efforts have extended this paradigm to broader general scientific (STEM) domains, the complex interplay between supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and RL in these contexts remains underexplored. In this paper, we conduct controlled experiments revealing a critical challenge: for general STEM domains, RL applied directly to base models is highly sample-inefficient and is consistently surpassed by supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on moderate-quality responses. Yet sequential SFT followed by RL can further improve performance, suggesting that the two stages play complementary roles, and that how training data is allocated between them matters. Therefore, we propose DeReason, a difficulty-based data decoupling strategy for general reasoning. DeReason partitions training data by reasoning intensity estimated via LLM-based scoring into reasoning-intensive and non-reasoning-intensive subsets. It allocates broad-coverage, non-reasoning-intensive problems to SFT to establish foundational domain knowledge, and reserves a focused subset of difficult problems for RL to cultivate complex reasoning. We demonstrate that this principled decoupling yields better performance than randomly splitting the data for sequential SFT and RL. Extensive experiments on general STEM and mathematical benchmarks demonstrate that our decoupled curriculum training significantly outperforms SFT-only, RL-only, and random-split baselines. Our work provides a systematic study of the interplay between SFT and RL for general reasoning, offering a highly effective and generalized post-training recipe.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to best combine supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) for general STEM reasoning, and proposes DeReason, a difficulty-based data decoupling strategy that beats SFT-only, RL-only, and naive splits.
AI.motivation: Although RLVR has shown strong results for math and coding, it is unclear how to efficiently apply RL versus SFT for broader STEM reasoning tasks. Direct RL on base models is sample-inefficient and underperforms SFT, yet SFT followed by RL helps, implying complementary roles. The authors want a systematic, principled way to allocate training data between SFT and RL to better exploit their strengths for general reasoning.
AI.method: They run controlled experiments comparing SFT, RL-only, and sequential SFT+RL on general STEM tasks, showing RL on base models is inefficient. They then propose DeReason, which uses an LLM-based scoring scheme to estimate each problem’s reasoning intensity. Training data is partitioned into reasoning-intensive and non-reasoning-intensive subsets. Non-reasoning-intensive (broad-coverage, easier) data is used for SFT to build domain knowledge, while reasoning-intensive (hard, complex) data is reserved for RL to train advanced reasoning. They compare this difficulty-aware decoupling to random splits across multiple benchmarks.
AI.result: Empirically, DeReason’s difficulty-based decoupled curriculum yields better performance than SFT-only, RL-only, and sequential SFT+RL with random data splits. RL on base models is shown to be highly sample-inefficient and consistently weaker than SFT on moderate-quality responses, but when DeReason is applied, the sequential SFT+RL pipeline becomes significantly stronger across general STEM and mathematical benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: SFT and RLVR serve complementary functions for general reasoning: SFT is better for learning broad domain knowledge from easier problems, while RL is better for cultivating complex reasoning from difficult instances. By explicitly decoupling training data by reasoning intensity via DeReason, models achieve substantially improved reasoning performance over naive or single-stage training. This provides a practical, general post-training recipe for enhancing STEM reasoning in large language models and clarifies the interplay between SFT and RL in this setting.

title: MDER-DR: Multi-Hop Question Answering with Entity-Centric Summaries
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) over Knowledge Graphs (KGs) suffers from the fact that indexing approaches may lose important contextual nuance when text is reduced to triples, thereby degrading performance in downstream Question-Answering (QA) tasks, particularly for multi-hop QA, which requires composing answers from multiple entities, facts, or relations. We propose a domain-agnostic, KG-based QA framework that covers both the indexing and retrieval/inference phases. A new indexing approach called Map-Disambiguate-Enrich-Reduce (MDER) generates context-derived triple descriptions and subsequently integrates them with entity-level summaries, thus avoiding the need for explicit traversal of edges in the graph during the QA retrieval phase. Complementing this, we introduce Decompose-Resolve (DR), a retrieval mechanism that decomposes user queries into resolvable triples and grounds them in the KG via iterative reasoning. Together, MDER and DR form an LLM-driven QA pipeline that is robust to sparse, incomplete, and complex relational data. Experiments show that on standard and domain specific benchmarks, MDER-DR achieves substantial improvements over standard RAG baselines (up to 66%), while maintaining cross-lingual robustness. Our code is available at https://github.com/DataSciencePolimi/MDER-DR_RAG.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an LLM-based QA framework over knowledge graphs that improves RAG by better indexing and reasoning over triples.
AI.motivation: Standard RAG over knowledge graphs loses important context when turning text into triples, which hurts especially multi-hop question answering that needs composing information from multiple entities and relations.
AI.method: They introduce a new indexing approach, Map-Disambiguate-Enrich-Reduce (MDER), that builds context-derived triple descriptions and merges them with entity summaries to avoid explicit edge traversal. They also present a retrieval method, Decompose-Resolve (DR), which decomposes user questions into triples and grounds them in the KG via iterative reasoning, forming an LLM-driven QA pipeline.
AI.result: On both standard and domain-specific QA benchmarks, their MDER-DR framework significantly outperforms standard RAG baselines by up to 66% and remains robust across languages and under sparse or incomplete data conditions.
AI.conclusion: Combining context-enriched KG indexing (MDER) with iterative triple-based query decomposition and resolution (DR) yields a domain-agnostic, cross-lingual, and robust QA system that mitigates the contextual loss problem of traditional KG RAG and substantially improves multi-hop QA performance.

title: DIVE: Scaling Diversity in Agentic Task Synthesis for Generalizable Tool Use
summary: Recent work synthesizes agentic tasks for post-training tool-using LLMs, yet robust generalization under shifts in tasks and toolsets remains an open challenge. We trace this brittleness to insufficient diversity in synthesized tasks. Scaling diversity is difficult because training requires tasks to remain executable and verifiable, while generalization demands coverage of diverse tool types, toolset combinations, and heterogeneous tool-use patterns. We propose DIVE, an evidence-driven recipe that inverts synthesis order, executing diverse, real-world tools first and reverse-deriving tasks strictly entailed by the resulting traces, thereby providing grounding by construction. DIVE scales structural diversity along two controllable axes, tool-pool coverage and per-task toolset variety, and an Evidence Collection--Task Derivation loop further induces rich multi-step tool-use patterns across 373 tools in five domains. Training Qwen3-8B on DIVE data (48k SFT + 3.2k RL) improves by +22 average points across 9 OOD benchmarks and outperforms the strongest 8B baseline by +68. Remarkably, controlled scaling analysis reveals that diversity scaling consistently outperforms quantity scaling for OOD generalization, even with 4x less data.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DIVE, a method for generating diverse, grounded tool-use tasks for training tool-using LLMs, and shows that increasing task diversity is more effective for out-of-distribution generalization than simply increasing data quantity.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches synthesize agentic tool-use tasks for LLMs but models remain brittle when tasks or toolsets change. The core issue is that synthesized tasks lack sufficient diversity while still needing to be executable and automatically verifiable. This limits coverage of tool types, combinations, and realistic multi-step usage patterns, which are crucial for robust generalization in real-world tool-use scenarios.
AI.method: The authors propose DIVE, which inverts the usual task-synthesis pipeline: instead of designing tasks and then executing tools, they first execute a wide range of real-world tools to collect evidence traces, and then reverse-derive tasks that are strictly entailed by these traces. This guarantees grounding and executability. DIVE explicitly scales structural diversity along two axes: (1) tool-pool coverage (how many and what kinds of tools are used overall) and (2) per-task toolset variety (how many and which tools are needed for each task). An iterative Evidence Collection–Task Derivation loop induces complex, multi-step, heterogeneous tool-use patterns across 373 tools spanning five domains. They then fine-tune Qwen3-8B using 48k supervised fine-tuning examples plus 3.2k reinforcement learning examples generated under this framework.
AI.result: Training Qwen3-8B on DIVE-generated data yields a +22 point average improvement across nine out-of-distribution benchmarks relative to its baseline performance. Compared to the strongest existing 8B-parameter baseline model, the DIVE-trained model achieves a +68 point performance gain. Controlled scaling experiments show that increasing the diversity of the tool-use tasks consistently yields better OOD generalization than merely increasing the quantity of data, even when using up to four times less data overall.
AI.conclusion: DIVE demonstrates that carefully structured diversity in tool-use tasks—achieved via evidence-first, grounded task derivation and explicit control over tool coverage and per-task variety—is a key driver of robust out-of-distribution generalization for tool-using LLMs. The work suggests that future progress in agentic LLM training should prioritize diversity scaling over naive data scaling and leverage real-world tool traces to construct executable, verifiable, and structurally rich training tasks.

title: Markovian Generation Chains in Large Language Models
summary: The widespread use of large language models (LLMs) raises an important question: how do texts evolve when they are repeatedly processed by LLMs? In this paper, we define this iterative inference process as Markovian generation chains, where each step takes a specific prompt template and the previous output as input, without including any prior memory. In iterative rephrasing and round-trip translation experiments, the output either converges to a small recurrent set or continues to produce novel sentences over a finite horizon. Through sentence-level Markov chain modeling and analysis of simulated data, we show that iterative process can either increase or reduce sentence diversity depending on factors such as the temperature parameter and the initial input sentence. These results offer valuable insights into the dynamics of iterative LLM inference and their implications for multi-agent LLM systems.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how text changes when repeatedly processed by large language models (LLMs), modeling this as a Markovian generation chain to understand convergence and diversity behavior.
AI.motivation: With LLMs increasingly used in iterative and multi-step settings (e.g., agents that repeatedly rewrite, translate, or respond to text), it is unclear whether repeated processing stabilizes text, degrades it, or diversifies it. Understanding these dynamics is important for the reliability and behavior of multi-agent LLM systems.
AI.method: The authors model repeated LLM inference as a Markov chain where each step takes a prompt template plus the previous output, but no additional memory. They conduct experiments on iterative rephrasing and round‑trip translation, analyze whether outputs converge to recurrent sets or keep changing, and fit sentence-level Markov chain models to simulated data. They study how parameters like sampling temperature and the initial sentence affect diversity over iterations.
AI.result: In experiments, repeated LLM processing sometimes converges to a small recurrent set of sentences, while in other settings it continues generating new sentences for a limited number of steps. Markov-chain-based analysis shows that iterative inference can either increase or decrease sentence diversity, influenced by factors such as temperature and the starting sentence.
AI.conclusion: Iterative LLM inference exhibits nontrivial dynamics: depending on configuration and initial conditions, it can lead to convergence to stable sentence sets or ongoing diversification. These findings help explain and predict behaviors of multi-step and multi-agent LLM systems, informing how to design prompts and sampling settings to control stability and diversity over iterations.

title: A Survey of Reasoning in Autonomous Driving Systems: Open Challenges and Emerging Paradigms
summary: The development of high-level autonomous driving (AD) is shifting from perception-centric limitations to a more fundamental bottleneck, namely, a deficit in robust and generalizable reasoning. Although current AD systems manage structured environments, they consistently falter in long-tail scenarios and complex social interactions that require human-like judgment. Meanwhile, the advent of large language and multimodal models (LLMs and MLLMs) presents a transformative opportunity to integrate a powerful cognitive engine into AD systems, moving beyond pattern matching toward genuine comprehension. However, a systematic framework to guide this integration is critically lacking. To bridge this gap, we provide a comprehensive review of this emerging field and argue that reasoning should be elevated from a modular component to the system's cognitive core. Specifically, we first propose a novel Cognitive Hierarchy to decompose the monolithic driving task according to its cognitive and interactive complexity. Building on this, we further derive and systematize seven core reasoning challenges, such as the responsiveness-reasoning trade-off and social-game reasoning. Furthermore, we conduct a dual-perspective review of the state-of-the-art, analyzing both system-centric approaches to architecting intelligent agents and evaluation-centric practices for their validation. Our analysis reveals a clear trend toward holistic and interpretable "glass-box" agents. In conclusion, we identify a fundamental and unresolved tension between the high-latency, deliberative nature of LLM-based reasoning and the millisecond-scale, safety-critical demands of vehicle control. For future work, a primary objective is to bridge the symbolic-to-physical gap by developing verifiable neuro-symbolic architectures, robust reasoning under uncertainty, and scalable models for implicit social negotiation.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews how large language and multimodal models can serve as the reasoning core of autonomous driving systems, proposes a cognitive hierarchy for driving, identifies key reasoning challenges, surveys current approaches and evaluations, and highlights the latency–safety tension as the main open problem.
AI.motivation: Current autonomous driving systems handle structured environments but fail in rare, complex, socially interactive situations that require human-like reasoning rather than pure perception and pattern matching. With the emergence of LLMs and MLLMs as powerful generic reasoners, there is a timely need to understand how such models can be integrated into AD as a cognitive engine. However, there is no systematic conceptual or architectural framework that organizes this integration, nor a clear taxonomy of reasoning challenges specific to driving.
AI.method: The authors conduct a conceptual and survey-style study. They: (1) Propose a ‘Cognitive Hierarchy’ that decomposes the end-to-end driving task according to different levels of cognitive and interactive complexity; (2) Use this hierarchy to derive and organize seven core reasoning challenges for AD (e.g., balancing responsiveness with deeper reasoning, game-theoretic social reasoning among agents); (3) Perform a dual-perspective literature review, examining both system-centric approaches (how intelligent driving agents are architected using LLMs/MLLMs) and evaluation-centric practices (how such reasoning agents are tested and validated); and (4) Analyze trends and unresolved tensions revealed by this review.
AI.result: The study yields: (1) A novel Cognitive Hierarchy framework for structuring the driving task by cognitive and interactional complexity; (2) A taxonomy of seven key reasoning challenges in autonomous driving, such as the trade-off between fast reaction and deep deliberation, and multi-agent social/game-theoretic reasoning; (3) A synthesized picture of the current state-of-the-art in LLM/MLLM-based AD systems and their evaluation, highlighting an emerging trend toward holistic, interpretable, ‘glass-box’ driving agents; and (4) A clarified articulation of the central open problem: reconciling the high latency of LLM-based reasoning with the stringent real-time, safety-critical control requirements of vehicles.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that in future autonomous driving systems, reasoning should not be treated as just another module but elevated to the central cognitive core, potentially powered by LLMs/MLLMs. They emphasize a fundamental unresolved tension between the deliberative, often slow nature of LLM reasoning and the millisecond-level responsiveness required for safe vehicle control. To address this, they call for research on verifiable neuro-symbolic architectures that can bridge symbolic reasoning and physical control, methods for robust reasoning under uncertainty, and scalable approaches for modeling implicit social negotiation among road users. These directions are presented as key steps toward robust, generalizable, and interpretable autonomous driving intelligence.

title: Artificial Intelligence for Sentiment Analysis of Persian Poetry
summary: Recent advancements of the Artificial Intelligence (AI) have led to the development of large language models (LLMs) that are capable of understanding, analysing, and creating textual data. These language models open a significant opportunity in analyzing the literature and more specifically poetry. In the present work, we employ multiple Bidirectional encoder representations from transformers (BERT) and Generative Pre-trained Transformer (GPT) based language models to analyze the works of two prominent Persian poets: Jalal al-Din Muhammad Rumi (Rumi) and Parvin E'tesami. The main objective of this research is to investigate the capability of the modern language models in grasping complexities of the Persian poetry and explore potential correlations between the poems' sentiment and their meters. Our findings in this study indicates that GPT4o language model can reliably be used in analysis of Persian poetry. Furthermore, the results of our sentiment analysis revealed that in general, Rumi's poems express happier sentiments compared to Parvin E'tesami's poems. Furthermore, comparing the utilization of poetic meters highlighted Rumi's poems superiority in using meters to express a wider variety of sentiments. These findings are significant as they confirm that LLMs can be effectively applied in conducting computer-based semantic studies, where human interpretations are not required, and thereby significantly reducing potential biases in the analysis.

AI.tldr: The paper uses modern large language models, especially GPT-4o, to automatically analyze sentiment and meter in Persian poetry by Rumi and Parvin E'tesami, showing that Rumi’s poems are generally happier and use poetic meters more flexibly, and that LLMs can reliably support computer-based literary analysis.
AI.motivation: To test whether modern AI language models can truly grasp the nuances and complexities of Persian poetry, and to see if they can be used for large-scale, less biased literary analysis—specifically, to explore how sentiment in poems relates to their poetic meters in the works of two major Persian poets.
AI.method: The authors apply multiple transformer-based language models, including several BERT variants and a GPT-based model (notably GPT-4o), to a corpus of poems by Rumi and Parvin E'tesami. They perform sentiment analysis on the poems and analyze the distribution and use of different poetic meters, then examine correlations between sentiment and meter usage across the two poets.
AI.result: They find that GPT-4o can reliably analyze Persian poetry in terms of sentiment and meter. The sentiment analysis shows that Rumi’s poems tend to be more positive/happy than Parvin E'tesami’s. The meter analysis reveals that Rumi uses poetic meters in a way that covers a broader range of sentiments than Parvin does, suggesting a richer or more varied expressive use of meter.
AI.conclusion: LLMs, particularly GPT-4o, are effective tools for automatic semantic and structural analysis of Persian poetry. They can uncover meaningful patterns in sentiment and meter without relying on human interpretation, which can help reduce subjective bias in literary analysis and open new avenues for large-scale, computational studies of literature.

title: PACED: Distillation at the Frontier of Student Competence
summary: Standard LLM distillation wastes compute on two fronts: problems the student has already mastered (near-zero gradients) and problems far beyond its reach (incoherent gradients that erode existing capabilities). We show that this waste is not merely intuitive but structurally inevitable: the gradient signal-to-noise ratio in distillation provably vanishes at both pass-rate extremes. This theoretical observation leads to Paced, a framework that concentrates distillation on the zone of proximal development -- the frontier of a student model's competence -- via a principled pass-rate weight $w(p) = p^α(1 - p)^β$ derived from the boundary-vanishing structure of distillation gradients. Key results: (1) Theory: We prove that the Beta kernel $w(p) = p^α(1-p)^β$ is a leading-order weight family arising from the SNR structure of distillation, and that it is minimax-robust -- under bounded multiplicative misspecification, worst-case efficiency loss is only $O(δ^2)$. (2)Distillation: On distillation from a larger teacher to a smaller student model with forward KL, Paced achieves significant gain over the base model, while keeping benchmark forgetting at a low level. (3)Self-distillation: On instruction-tuned models with reverse KL, gains are exceeding baselines as well. (4)Two-stage synergy: A forward-KL-then-reverse-KL schedule yields the strongest results in our setting, reaching substantial improvements on standard reasoning benchmarks -- supporting a mode-coverage-then-consolidation interpretation of the distillation process. All configurations require only student rollouts to estimate pass rates, need no architectural changes, and are compatible with any KL direction.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Paced, a principled distillation framework that focuses training on tasks near the student model’s current capability, improving efficiency and performance.
AI.motivation: Traditional LLM distillation wastes computation on examples that are either too easy or too hard for the student, where gradients are either near zero or noisy and harmful. The authors aim to formalize this inefficiency, understand it theoretically via gradient signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and design a distillation scheme that allocates training effort to the most informative region of task difficulty.
AI.method: They analyze the gradient SNR of distillation and prove it necessarily vanishes when student pass rates are near 0 or 1. From this structure, they derive a pass-rate weighting function based on a Beta kernel, w(p) = p^α (1 − p)^β, that emphasizes intermediate pass rates (the zone of proximal development). They formalize this as the Paced framework, demonstrate that the Beta weighting is a leading-order solution and minimax-robust to multiplicative misspecification, and then implement Paced in both forward-KL distillation (teacher→student) and reverse-KL self-distillation, including a two-stage forward-then-reverse KL schedule.
AI.result: Paced improves student performance compared to standard distillation on reasoning benchmarks, while keeping forgetting low. It works with forward KL for teacher–student distillation, reverse KL for self-distillation, and shows especially strong results with a two-stage forward-KL-then-reverse-KL schedule. Theoretical results show the Beta weighting naturally emerges from the SNR behavior and remains robust, with worst-case efficiency loss scaling only as O(δ^2) under bounded multiplicative misspecification.
AI.conclusion: Focusing distillation on examples with intermediate pass rates—operationalized via a Beta-kernel weighting of pass rates—addresses the structural inefficiency of conventional distillation. Paced provides a theoretically grounded, architecture-agnostic, and KL-direction-agnostic framework that yields better and more robust distillation outcomes using only student rollouts to estimate pass rates.

title: ThReadMed-QA: A Multi-Turn Medical Dialogue Benchmark from Real Patient Questions
summary: Medical question-answering benchmarks predominantly evaluate single-turn exchanges, failing to capture the iterative, clarification-seeking nature of real patient consultations. We introduce ThReadMed-QA, a benchmark of 2,437 fully-answered patient-physician conversation threads extracted from r/AskDocs, comprising 8,204 question-answer pairs across up to 9 turns. Unlike prior work relying on simulated dialogues, adversarial prompts, or exam-style questions, ThReadMed-QA captures authentic patient follow-up questions and verified physician responses, reflecting how patients naturally seek medical information online. We evaluate five state-of-the-art LLMs -- GPT-5, GPT-4o, Claude Haiku, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Llama 3.3 70B -- on a stratified test split of 238 conversations (948 QA pairs) using a calibrated LLM-as-a-judge rubric grounded in physician ground truth. Even the strongest model, GPT-5, achieves only 41.2% fully-correct responses. All five models degrade significantly from turn 0 to turn 2 (p < 0.001), with wrong-answer rates roughly tripling by the third turn. We identify a fundamental tension between single-turn capability and multi-turn reliability: models with the strongest initial performance (GPT-5: 75.2; Claude Haiku: 72.3 out of 100) exhibit the steepest declines by turn 2 (dropping 16.2 and 25.0 points respectively), while weaker models plateau or marginally improve. We introduce two metrics to quantify multi-turn failure modes: Conversational Consistency Score (CCS) and Error Propagation Rate (EPR). CCS reveals that nearly one in three Claude Haiku conversations swings between a fully correct and a completely wrong response within the same thread. EPR shows that a single wrong turn raises the probability of a subsequent wrong turn by 1.9-6.1x across all models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ThReadMed-QA, a multi-turn medical question-answering benchmark derived from real patient-physician Reddit threads and shows that current state-of-the-art LLMs perform poorly and degrade substantially across turns.
AI.motivation: Existing medical QA benchmarks mostly evaluate single-turn, isolated questions, which does not match real-world patient consultations that are iterative and involve follow-up clarification. There is a need for a realistic, multi-turn benchmark using authentic patient questions and physician responses to understand how reliably LLMs handle ongoing medical conversations.
AI.method: The authors construct ThReadMed-QA from r/AskDocs, collecting 2,437 patient-physician conversation threads with 8,204 QA pairs over up to 9 turns, ensuring that each QA pair is fully answered and physician-verified. They create a stratified test split of 238 conversations (948 QA pairs). Five state-of-the-art LLMs (GPT-5, GPT-4o, Claude Haiku, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Llama 3.3 70B) are evaluated using an LLM-as-a-judge system calibrated against physician ground truth, and they introduce two new metrics: Conversational Consistency Score (CCS) and Error Propagation Rate (EPR) to quantify multi-turn reliability and error dynamics.
AI.result: GPT-5 achieves the best performance but still only 41.2% fully correct responses overall. All evaluated models demonstrate significant performance degradation from the initial turn to later turns (turn 0 to turn 2, p < 0.001), with wrong-answer rates tripling by the third turn. Models that start with higher single-turn scores (e.g., GPT-5 at 75.2/100 and Claude Haiku at 72.3/100) experience the largest drops by turn 2 (16.2 and 25.0 points), whereas weaker models show smaller changes. CCS indicates many conversations, especially for Claude Haiku, fluctuate between fully correct and completely wrong answers within the same thread, and EPR shows that once a model makes one wrong answer, the probability of another wrong answer in the next turn increases by 1.9–6.1 times across models.
AI.conclusion: Current state-of-the-art LLMs are not reliably safe or accurate for multi-turn medical consultations, even if they perform well on single-turn medical QA. Realistic, conversation-level benchmarks like ThReadMed-QA are necessary to expose and quantify multi-turn failure modes, such as inconsistency and error propagation, and to guide the development of more reliable medical dialogue systems.

title: Measuring AI Agents' Progress on Multi-Step Cyber Attack Scenarios
summary: We evaluate the autonomous cyber-attack capabilities of frontier AI models on two purpose-built cyber ranges-a 32-step corporate network attack and a 7-step industrial control system attack-that require chaining heterogeneous capabilities across extended action sequences. By comparing seven models released over an eighteen-month period (August 2024 to February 2026) at varying inference-time compute budgets, we observe two capability trends. First, model performance scales log-linearly with inference-time compute, with no observed plateau-increasing from 10M to 100M tokens yields gains of up to 59%, requiring no specific technical sophistication from the operator. Second, each successive model generation outperforms its predecessor at fixed token budgets: on the corporate network range, average steps completed at 10M tokens rose from 1.7 (GPT-4o, August 2024) to 9.8 (Opus 4.6, February 2026). The best single run completed 22 of 32 steps, corresponding to roughly 6 of the estimated 14 hours a human expert would need. On the industrial control system range, performance remains limited, though the most recent models are the first to reliably complete steps, averaging 1.2-1.4 of 7 (max 3).

AI.tldr: The paper empirically measures how well recent frontier AI models can autonomously conduct complex, multi-step cyber-attacks in realistic simulated environments, and how those capabilities scale with more inference-time compute and newer model generations.
AI.motivation: To move beyond anecdotal or one-off demonstrations and rigorously quantify the real-world offensive cyber capabilities of state-of-the-art AI models. The authors want to understand: (1) whether these models can chain diverse tools and skills over long horizons to execute realistic attacks; (2) how performance changes as models and inference-time compute improve; and (3) what this implies for security risk and oversight as frontier models advance.
AI.method: The authors build two specialized cyber ranges: a 32-step corporate network attack scenario and a 7-step industrial control system (ICS) attack scenario. Each scenario requires the model to autonomously chain heterogeneous actions (e.g., reconnaissance, exploitation, lateral movement, privilege escalation) over many steps. They evaluate seven successive frontier models released between August 2024 and February 2026, and systematically vary inference-time compute by allowing from 10M to 100M tokens per run. For each model and token budget, they measure how many steps of each attack chain are successfully completed, and analyze scaling trends with respect to both model generation and token budget.
AI.result: Across both cyber ranges, model performance scales approximately log-linearly with inference-time compute, with no observed performance plateau within the tested range. Increasing the token budget from 10M to 100M tokens yields up to a 59% gain in completed attack steps, even without requiring sophisticated operators. Newer model generations also consistently outperform earlier ones at the same token budgets. On the corporate network range, the average steps completed at a 10M-token budget improved from 1.7 steps with GPT-4o (August 2024) to 9.8 steps with Opus 4.6 (February 2026). The best observed single run achieved 22 of 32 steps, which corresponds to roughly 6 of the roughly 14 hours a human expert would need to complete the full attack chain. On the ICS range, overall capability remains low, but the latest models are the first to reliably complete any steps at all, averaging 1.2–1.4 of 7 steps with a maximum of 3 steps completed in a single run.
AI.conclusion: Frontier AI models already exhibit non-trivial autonomous capabilities for executing complex, long-horizon cyber-attacks in realistic simulated environments, and these capabilities are improving rapidly with both increased inference-time compute and successive model generations. Performance on corporate network attacks is becoming meaningfully competent, while ICS attack capability remains nascent but is emerging. The absence of a performance plateau in the tested compute range suggests that further scaling in tokens and model quality is likely to yield even stronger offensive cyber capabilities, underscoring the need for proactive risk assessment, safeguards, and policy responses as models advance.

title: Temporal Text Classification with Large Language Models
summary: Languages change over time. Computational models can be trained to recognize such changes enabling them to estimate the publication date of texts. Despite recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs), their performance on automatic dating of texts, also known as Temporal Text Classification (TTC), has not been explored. This study provides the first systematic evaluation of leading proprietary (Claude 3.5, GPT-4o, Gemini 1.5) and open-source (LLaMA 3.2, Gemma 2, Mistral, Nemotron 4) LLMs on TTC using three historical corpora, two in English and one in Portuguese. We test zero-shot and few-shot prompting, and fine-tuning settings. Our results indicate that proprietary models perform well, especially with few-shot prompting. They also indicate that fine-tuning substantially improves open-source models but that they still fail to match the performance delivered by proprietary LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well leading proprietary and open-source large language models can automatically date historical texts (Temporal Text Classification) across several English and Portuguese corpora, comparing zero/few-shot prompting and fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Language evolves over time, leaving detectable signals in texts that allow estimating when they were written. Earlier computational models have been used for this Temporal Text Classification task, but the capabilities of modern large language models in this area have not yet been systematically assessed. Understanding their strengths and weaknesses on TTC is important for historical linguistics, digital humanities, and temporal information retrieval.
AI.method: The authors benchmark several state-of-the-art LLMs—both proprietary (Claude 3.5, GPT-4o, Gemini 1.5) and open-source (LLaMA 3.2, Gemma 2, Mistral, Nemotron 4)—on three historical text corpora (two English, one Portuguese). They evaluate models in three regimes: zero-shot prompting, few-shot in-context learning, and supervised fine-tuning, then compare performance across these settings and between proprietary vs. open-source models.
AI.result: Proprietary LLMs achieve strong performance on Temporal Text Classification, with particularly notable gains in accuracy when few-shot prompting is used. Fine-tuning significantly boosts the performance of open-source models relative to their prompted variants; however, even after fine-tuning, they do not reach the TTC performance levels of proprietary models on the evaluated corpora.
AI.conclusion: State-of-the-art proprietary LLMs are currently better suited for temporal text classification of historical corpora, especially when given a small number of labeled examples in context. While fine-tuning narrows the gap for open-source models, it is insufficient to fully close it, highlighting both the promise of LLMs for TTC and the remaining performance disparity between proprietary and open-source systems.

title: Reversible Lifelong Model Editing via Semantic Routing-Based LoRA
summary: The dynamic evolution of real-world necessitates model editing within Large Language Models. While existing methods explore modular isolation or parameter-efficient strategies, they still suffer from semantic drift or knowledge forgetting due to continual updating. To address these challenges, we propose SoLA, a Semantic routing-based LoRA framework for lifelong model editing. In SoLA, each edit is encapsulated as an independent LoRA module, which is frozen after training and mapped to input by semantic routing, allowing dynamic activation of LoRA modules via semantic matching. This mechanism avoids semantic drift caused by cluster updating and mitigates catastrophic forgetting from parameter sharing. More importantly, SoLA supports precise revocation of specific edits by removing key from semantic routing, which restores model's original behavior. To our knowledge, this reversible rollback editing capability is the first to be achieved in existing literature. Furthermore, SoLA integrates decision-making process into edited layer, eliminating the need for auxiliary routing networks and enabling end-to-end decision-making process. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SoLA effectively learns and retains edited knowledge, achieving accurate, efficient, and reversible lifelong model editing.

AI.tldr: SoLA is a semantic-routing-based LoRA framework that enables accurate, efficient, and reversible lifelong model editing for LLMs by storing each edit in a separate LoRA module and activating it via semantic matching.
AI.motivation: Real-world knowledge constantly changes, requiring LLMs to be updated without retraining from scratch. Existing model editing methods using modular isolation or parameter-efficient fine-tuning still suffer from semantic drift (edits interfering with each other) and catastrophic forgetting (loss of previous or base knowledge) when many updates are applied over time. Moreover, current methods rarely support precise rollback of specific edits to restore original model behavior. The paper aims to design a lifelong editing framework that (1) preserves previously edited and base knowledge, (2) avoids interference between edits, and (3) allows fine-grained, reversible removal of specific edits.
AI.method: The authors propose SoLA, a Semantic routing-based LoRA framework. Each model edit is implemented as an independent LoRA module attached to the base LLM. After training on the specific edit, the corresponding LoRA parameters are frozen. A semantic routing mechanism maps incoming inputs to the relevant LoRA modules by matching input semantics with stored edit keys, enabling dynamic activation of one or more LoRA modules at inference time. This architecture avoids shared-parameter continual fine-tuning and cluster-based merging, which can cause semantic drift and forgetting. SoLA further integrates the routing/decision-making logic directly into the edited layer (rather than using a separate auxiliary router), enabling end-to-end training of both the base representation and routing behavior. Edits can be revoked by deleting their keys from the semantic routing table, which effectively disables the corresponding LoRA module and reverts to the base model behavior for those cases.
AI.result: Experiments (details not in the abstract) show that SoLA can (1) learn new edits accurately, (2) retain previously edited and base knowledge better than prior model editing baselines, and (3) maintain efficiency by using LoRA-based parameter-efficient adaptation. The routing-based modular design enables precise and reversible editing, with the model able to selectively activate or deactivate specific edits by semantic routing, achieving strong performance on lifelong editing benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: SoLA provides a practical solution for lifelong model editing in LLMs by representing each edit as a frozen, semantically routed LoRA module. This design mitigates semantic drift and catastrophic forgetting, supports precise rollback of individual edits, and allows end-to-end integration of routing into the edited layers without auxiliary networks. The framework advances the state of the art in accurate, efficient, and reversible knowledge editing for continuously updated LLMs.

title: Evaluating Explainable AI Attribution Methods in Neural Machine Translation via Attention-Guided Knowledge Distillation
summary: The study of the attribution of input features to the output of neural network models is an active area of research. While numerous Explainable AI (XAI) techniques have been proposed to interpret these models, the systematic and automated evaluation of these methods in sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq) models is less explored. This paper introduces a new approach for evaluating explainability methods in transformer-based seq2seq models. We use teacher-derived attribution maps as a structured side signal to guide a student model, and quantify the utility of different attribution methods through the student's ability to simulate targets. Using the Inseq library, we extract attribution scores over source-target sequence pairs and inject these scores into the attention mechanism of a student transformer model under four composition operators (addition, multiplication, averaging, and replacement). Across three language pairs (de-en, fr-en, ar-en) and attributions from Marian-MT and mBART models, Attention, Value Zeroing, and Layer Gradient $\times$ Activation consistently yield the largest gains in BLEU (and corresponding improvements in chrF) relative to baselines. In contrast, other gradient-based methods (Saliency, Integrated Gradients, DeepLIFT, Input $\times$ Gradient, GradientShap) lead to smaller and less consistent improvements. These results suggest that different attribution methods capture distinct signals and that attention-derived attributions better capture alignment between source and target representations in seq2seq models. Finally, we introduce an Attributor transformer that, given a source-target pair, learns to reconstruct the teacher's attribution map. Our findings demonstrate that the more accurately the Attributor can reproduce attribution maps, the more useful an injection of those maps is for the downstream task. The source code can be found on GitHub.

AI.tldr: They evaluate explainability methods for transformer seq2seq models by using attribution maps from a teacher model as supervision for a student, measuring how much these attributions help translation quality.
AI.motivation: Explainability methods for neural networks, especially seq2seq transformers, abound, but there is no systematic, automated way to compare how useful different attribution techniques actually are for downstream tasks like machine translation. Existing XAI work is often qualitative or classification-focused, leaving a gap for sequence models and for task-grounded evaluation of attributions.
AI.method: They extract token-level attribution scores (teacher-derived attribution maps) for source–target pairs from transformer MT models using the Inseq library and various XAI methods. These scores are injected into the attention mechanism of a student transformer under four composition strategies (addition, multiplication, averaging, replacement). They then train the student to perform translation with these augmented attention patterns and measure gains in BLEU/chrF as a proxy for the quality and utility of each attribution method. They test multiple language pairs (de-en, fr-en, ar-en) and multiple teachers (Marian-MT, mBART). They also introduce an Attributor transformer that learns to reconstruct the teacher’s attribution maps and study how the fidelity of this reconstruction correlates with downstream translation performance when those maps are injected.
AI.result: Attention, Value Zeroing, and Layer Gradient × Activation attributions yield the largest, most consistent BLEU and chrF improvements across all tested settings compared to baselines. Other common gradient-based methods (Saliency, Integrated Gradients, DeepLIFT, Input × Gradient, GradientShap) provide only small or inconsistent gains. The Attributor transformer’s ability to accurately reproduce teacher attribution maps positively correlates with the usefulness of those maps for improving the student’s translation performance.
AI.conclusion: Not all attribution methods are equally informative for seq2seq transformers: attention-derived and certain gradient-based schemes (Value Zeroing, Layer Gradient × Activation) better capture alignment between source and target representations and provide more utility for the downstream translation task. Using teacher attribution maps as structured supervision for student attention offers a principled, automated way to evaluate and compare XAI methods in seq2seq settings, and accurately learnable attribution maps are particularly beneficial when injected into the model.

title: Mind the Sim2Real Gap in User Simulation for Agentic Tasks
summary: As NLP evaluation shifts from static benchmarks to multi-turn interactive settings, LLM-based simulators have become widely used as user proxies, serving two roles: generating user turns and providing evaluation signals. Yet, these simulations are frequently assumed to be faithful to real human behaviors, often without rigorous verification. We formalize the Sim2Real gap in user simulation and present the first study running the full $τ$-bench protocol with real humans (451 participants, 165 tasks), benchmarking 31 LLM simulators across proprietary, open-source, and specialized families using the User-Sim Index (USI), a metric we introduce to quantify how well LLM simulators resemble real user interactive behaviors and feedback. Behaviorally, LLM simulators are excessively cooperative, stylistically uniform, and lack realistic frustration or ambiguity, creating an "easy mode" that inflates agent success rates above the human baseline. In evaluations, real humans provide nuanced judgments across eight quality dimensions while simulated users produce uniformly more positive feedback; rule-based rewards are failing to capture rich feedback signals generated by human users. Overall, higher general model capability does not necessarily yield more faithful user simulation. These findings highlight the importance of human validation when using LLM-based user simulators in the agent development cycle and motivate improved models for user simulation.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how well large language model (LLM)-based user simulators reflect real human behavior in multi-turn NLP evaluation, and finds substantial gaps.
AI.motivation: As evaluation in NLP moves from static tests to interactive settings, practitioners increasingly use LLMs to simulate users for both interaction and evaluation. These simulators are often assumed to behave like real humans without being rigorously validated, risking misleading conclusions about agent performance. The authors want to quantify and understand the discrepancy between simulated and real users (the Sim2Real gap) to make interactive evaluation more reliable.
AI.method: The authors (1) formalize the notion of a Sim2Real gap for user simulation; (2) run the full τ-bench protocol with real human users—451 participants over 165 tasks—to create a human interaction and feedback benchmark; (3) benchmark 31 different LLM-based simulators spanning proprietary, open-source, and specialized models; (4) introduce the User-Sim Index (USI), a metric to measure how closely simulators reproduce real user interaction patterns and feedback distributions; and (5) compare behavioral features, stylistic properties, and evaluation feedback between humans and simulators, as well as analyze correlation with general model capability and rule-based reward signals.
AI.result: The study finds that LLM user simulators differ significantly from real users. Simulators tend to be overly cooperative, stylistically homogeneous, and rarely exhibit frustration or ambiguity, which creates an unrealistically easy interaction setting that inflates agent success rates beyond human baselines. Real humans provide diverse, nuanced judgments along eight quality dimensions, whereas simulated users give more uniformly positive feedback. Conventional rule-based reward functions fail to capture the richness of human feedback. Moreover, greater general capability of the underlying LLM does not guarantee better fidelity as a user simulator, as measured by USI.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based user simulators currently provide a distorted picture of how agents perform with real people, due to systematic behavioral and evaluative differences from human users. High-performing LLMs are not automatically good user proxies. Reliable development and evaluation of interactive NLP agents therefore requires explicit human validation and the design of improved, more realistic user simulation models, guided by metrics such as the User-Sim Index.

title: Stop Listening to Me! How Multi-turn Conversations Can Degrade Diagnostic Reasoning
summary: Patients and clinicians are increasingly using chatbots powered by large language models (LLMs) for healthcare inquiries. While state-of-the-art LLMs exhibit high performance on static diagnostic reasoning benchmarks, their efficacy across multi-turn conversations, which better reflect real-world usage, has been understudied. In this paper, we evaluate 17 LLMs across three clinical datasets to investigate how partitioning the decision-space into multiple simpler turns of conversation influences their diagnostic reasoning. Specifically, we develop a "stick-or-switch" evaluation framework to measure model conviction (i.e., defending a correct diagnosis or safe abstention against incorrect suggestions) and flexibility (i.e., recognizing a correct suggestion when it is introduced) across conversations. Our experiments reveal the conversation tax, where multi-turn interactions consistently degrade performance when compared to single-shot baselines. Notably, models frequently abandon initial correct diagnoses and safe abstentions to align with incorrect user suggestions. Additionally, several models exhibit blind switching, failing to distinguish between signal and incorrect suggestions.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how large language model chatbots perform in multi-turn medical diagnostic conversations versus single-shot settings, finding that performance degrades in conversations due to models over-accommodating user suggestions.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs perform well on static, single-turn diagnostic benchmarks, real clinical use involves multi-turn dialogues. It is unclear how this conversational setting affects diagnostic accuracy and safety, especially when patients or clinicians propose their own (possibly incorrect) ideas.
AI.method: The authors evaluate 17 LLMs on three clinical datasets using a novel "stick-or-switch" framework. They structure interactions into multi-turn conversations where models must either stick with an earlier diagnosis/abstention or switch in response to user-proposed alternatives. They then separately measure conviction (defending correct answers or safe abstentions) and flexibility (accepting correct suggestions when presented) and compare multi-turn performance to single-shot baselines.
AI.result: Across models and datasets, multi-turn conversations consistently reduce diagnostic performance relative to single-shot prompts, a phenomenon the authors call the "conversation tax." Models often abandon initially correct diagnoses or safe abstentions when faced with incorrect user suggestions and sometimes change answers indiscriminately ("blind switching") without properly discerning correct from incorrect new information.
AI.conclusion: Even high-performing LLMs are vulnerable in realistic multi-turn clinical chats: they are overly influenced by user suggestions, leading to unnecessary and unsafe changes of diagnosis. Evaluation and design of medical LLMs must explicitly account for conversational dynamics, aiming to improve both conviction on correct answers and appropriate flexibility to new, valid information.

title: The Unlearning Mirage: A Dynamic Framework for Evaluating LLM Unlearning
summary: Unlearning in Large Language Models (LLMs) aims to enhance safety, mitigate biases, and comply with legal mandates, such as the right to be forgotten. However, existing unlearning methods are brittle: minor query modifications, such as multi-hop reasoning and entity aliasing, can recover supposedly forgotten information. As a result, current evaluation metrics often create an illusion of effectiveness, failing to detect these vulnerabilities due to reliance on static, unstructured benchmarks. We propose a dynamic framework that stress tests unlearning robustness using complex structured queries. Our approach first elicits knowledge from the target model (pre-unlearning) and constructs targeted probes, ranging from simple queries to multi-hop chains, allowing precise control over query difficulty. Our experiments show that the framework (1) shows comparable coverage to existing benchmarks by automatically generating semantically equivalent Q&A probes, (2) aligns with prior evaluations, and (3) uncovers new unlearning failures missed by other benchmarks, particularly in multi-hop settings. Furthermore, activation analyses show that single-hop queries typically follow dominant computation pathways, which are more likely to be disrupted by unlearning methods. In contrast, multi-hop queries tend to use alternative pathways that often remain intact, explaining the brittleness of unlearning techniques in multi-hop settings. Our framework enables practical and scalable evaluation of unlearning methods without the need for manual construction of forget test sets, enabling easier adoption for real-world applications. We release the pip package and the code at https://sites.google.com/view/unlearningmirage/home.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a dynamic, query-generating framework to robustly evaluate how well unlearning methods in large language models actually remove knowledge, revealing that many methods fail under slightly more complex, multi-hop queries.
AI.motivation: Existing unlearning techniques for LLMs aim to remove specific information for safety, bias mitigation, and legal compliance, but they are fragile. Slight paraphrases, multi-hop reasoning, or using aliases can still retrieve the supposedly forgotten content. Current evaluations rely on static, unstructured benchmarks that miss these failure modes, creating a misleading impression of success. There is a need for a more realistic, robust, and scalable way to test whether unlearning truly works in practice.
AI.method: The authors design a dynamic evaluation framework that, before unlearning, queries the model to extract relevant knowledge about targeted facts. Using this elicited knowledge, the framework automatically builds a suite of structured probes, ranging from simple one-hop questions to complex multi-hop reasoning chains and semantically equivalent rephrasings. These probes are designed to systematically vary difficulty and stress-test whether unlearning holds under more challenging access patterns. The method also incorporates activation analysis to inspect which internal computation pathways are used for different query types, comparing single-hop vs multi-hop behavior before and after unlearning.
AI.result: Empirically, the framework matches existing benchmarks in coverage by automatically generating semantically equivalent Q&A probes, and its overall performance assessments are consistent with prior evaluations. However, it additionally exposes unlearning failures that other benchmarks miss, especially for multi-hop queries and structured reasoning scenarios. Activation-level analysis reveals that single-hop queries typically rely on dominant computation pathways that are more likely to be disrupted by unlearning, whereas multi-hop queries recruit alternative pathways that often remain unaffected, enabling recovery of supposedly forgotten information.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides a practical, scalable, and more realistic way to evaluate the robustness of unlearning methods in LLMs. It demonstrates that many current unlearning approaches are brittle, particularly under multi-hop reasoning, because they fail to disrupt alternative internal computation routes. By eliminating the need for manually curated forget-test sets and offering automated generation of challenging probes, the framework can facilitate broader, more reliable adoption and assessment of unlearning techniques in real-world settings.

title: Algorithmic Consequences of Particle Filters for Sentence Processing: Amplified Garden-Paths and Digging-In Effects
summary: Under surprisal theory, linguistic representations affect processing difficulty only through the bottleneck of surprisal. Our best estimates of surprisal come from large language models, which have no explicit representation of structural ambiguity. While LLM surprisal robustly predicts reading times across languages, it systematically underpredicts difficulty when structural expectations are violated -- suggesting that representations of ambiguity are causally implicated in sentence processing. Particle filter models offer an alternative where structural hypotheses are explicitly represented as a finite set of particles. We prove several algorithmic consequences of particle filter models, including the amplification of garden-path effects. Most critically, we demonstrate that resampling, a common practice with these models, inherently produces real-time digging-in effects -- where disambiguation difficulty increases with ambiguous region length. Digging-in magnitude scales inversely with particle count: fully parallel models predict no such effect.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates particle filter models of sentence processing under surprisal theory, showing that explicit structural ambiguity representations can explain garden-path and digging-in effects that LLM surprisal alone underestimates.
AI.motivation: Surprisal theory claims processing difficulty is mediated solely by surprisal, estimated well by LLMs that lack explicit ambiguity representations. However, LLM surprisal underpredicts human difficulty when structural expectations are violated, implying that ambiguity representations themselves affect processing and challenging a purely surprisal-based account. The paper seeks a computational framework that incorporates explicit structural hypotheses to better capture human behavior.
AI.method: The authors analyze particle filter models, in which sentence processing maintains a finite set of explicit structural hypotheses (particles). They provide theoretical proofs of algorithmic properties of such models, particularly how limited particle counts and resampling strategies affect predicted processing difficulty. They focus on garden-path and digging-in effects, deriving how these arise from the mechanics of particle filtering under structural ambiguity and varying particle numbers.
AI.result: They show mathematically that particle filter models inherently amplify garden-path effects compared to purely surprisal-based models. They further prove that the resampling step, standard in particle filtering, necessarily yields real-time digging-in effects: the longer the ambiguous region before disambiguation, the greater the predicted processing difficulty at disambiguation. The strength of this digging-in effect is inversely related to the number of particles; with infinitely many particles (a fully parallel model), the effect disappears.
AI.conclusion: The work argues that to account for human sentence processing phenomena like garden-path and digging-in effects, models must explicitly track structural ambiguity with a finite representational capacity, as in particle filter approaches. Pure surprisal-based models with effectively infinite parallelism (approximated by large language models) cannot by themselves capture these difficulty patterns, highlighting the causal role of ambiguity representations and resource limitations in real-time comprehension.

title: COMPASS: The explainable agentic framework for Sovereignty, Sustainability, Compliance, and Ethics
summary: The rapid proliferation of large language model (LLM)-based agentic systems raises critical concerns regarding digital sovereignty, environmental sustainability, regulatory compliance, and ethical alignment. Whilst existing frameworks address individual dimensions in isolation, no unified architecture systematically integrates these imperatives into the decision-making processes of autonomous agents. This paper introduces the COMPASS (Compliance and Orchestration for Multi-dimensional Principles in Autonomous Systems with Sovereignty) Framework, a novel multi-agent orchestration system designed to enforce value-aligned AI through modular, extensible governance mechanisms. The framework comprises an Orchestrator and four specialised sub-agents addressing sovereignty, carbon-aware computing, compliance, and ethics, each augmented with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to ground evaluations in verified, context-specific documents. By employing an LLM-as-a-judge methodology, the system assigns quantitative scores and generates explainable justifications for each assessment dimension, enabling real-time arbitration of conflicting objectives. We validate the architecture through automated evaluation, demonstrating that RAG integration significantly enhances semantic coherence and mitigates the hallucination risks. Our results indicate that the framework's composition-based design facilitates seamless integration into diverse application domains whilst preserving interpretability and traceability.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes COMPASS, a multi-agent orchestration framework that embeds digital sovereignty, sustainability, compliance, and ethics into LLM-based autonomous systems using modular governance agents with RAG and LLM-as-a-judge scoring.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agentic systems are proliferating without systematic mechanisms to ensure digital sovereignty, environmental sustainability, regulatory compliance, and ethical alignment. Existing approaches treat these concerns separately and do not provide an integrated architecture that can be embedded into autonomous decision-making. There is a need for a unified, extensible governance layer that can operationalize these principles in real time for LLM agents.
AI.method: The authors design the COMPASS framework, consisting of a central Orchestrator coordinating four specialized sub-agents: sovereignty, carbon-aware computing, compliance, and ethics. Each sub-agent uses Retrieval-Augmented Generation to consult verified, context-specific documents (e.g., legal texts, policies, domain guidelines). An LLM-as-a-judge paradigm is used to produce quantitative scores and natural language justifications for each dimension. The Orchestrator arbitrates among potentially conflicting objectives based on these scores and explanations. The system is evaluated via automated tests comparing RAG-enabled vs non-RAG configurations, focusing on semantic coherence and hallucination reduction.
AI.result: Experiments show that including RAG in the sub-agents significantly improves the semantic coherence of their assessments and reduces hallucinations relative to non-RAG baselines. The composition-based, modular architecture can be integrated into different application domains while maintaining clear scoring, explanations, and decision traces across sovereignty, sustainability, compliance, and ethics dimensions.
AI.conclusion: COMPASS provides a unified, modular governance framework for LLM-based autonomous systems, embedding sovereignty, sustainability, compliance, and ethics checks directly into their operation. The combination of orchestrated sub-agents, RAG grounding, and LLM-as-a-judge scoring yields more reliable, interpretable, and traceable decision support. The design is extensible to diverse domains and offers a practical path toward value-aligned, governable AI agents.

title: MaterialFigBENCH: benchmark dataset with figures for evaluating college-level materials science problem-solving abilities of multimodal large language models
summary: We present MaterialFigBench, a benchmark dataset designed to evaluate the ability of multimodal large language models (LLMs) to solve university-level materials science problems that require accurate interpretation of figures. Unlike existing benchmarks that primarily rely on textual representations, MaterialFigBench focuses on problems in which figures such as phase diagrams, stress-strain curves, Arrhenius plots, diffraction patterns, and microstructural schematics are indispensable for deriving correct answers. The dataset consists of 137 free-response problems adapted from standard materials science textbooks, covering a broad range of topics including crystal structures, mechanical properties, diffusion, phase diagrams, phase transformations, and electronic properties of materials. To address unavoidable ambiguity in reading numerical values from images, expert-defined answer ranges are provided where appropriate. We evaluate several state-of-the-art multimodal LLMs, including ChatGPT and GPT models accessed via OpenAI APIs, and analyze their performance across problem categories and model versions. The results reveal that, although overall accuracy improves with model updates, current LLMs still struggle with genuine visual understanding and quantitative interpretation of materials science figures. In many cases, correct answers are obtained by relying on memorized domain knowledge rather than by reading the provided images. MaterialFigBench highlights persistent weaknesses in visual reasoning, numerical precision, and significant-digit handling, while also identifying problem types where performance has improved. This benchmark provides a systematic and domain-specific foundation for advancing multimodal reasoning capabilities in materials science and for guiding the development of future LLMs with stronger figure-based understanding.

AI.tldr: Introduces MaterialFigBench, a benchmark to test multimodal LLMs on university-level materials science problems that require interpreting figures, revealing current models’ weaknesses in true visual and quantitative understanding.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks mostly test language models on text-only or text-centric problems and do not adequately assess the critical skill of interpreting complex scientific figures, which is essential in materials science education and practice. There is a need for a domain-specific benchmark that stresses figure-based reasoning and quantitative extraction from images.
AI.method: The authors construct MaterialFigBench, a set of 137 free-response problems adapted from standard materials science textbooks, all of which require understanding figures like phase diagrams, stress-strain curves, Arrhenius plots, diffraction patterns, and microstructural schematics. They define expert-validated answer ranges to handle ambiguities in reading numerical values from images. They then evaluate several state-of-the-art multimodal LLMs (including ChatGPT and GPT models via OpenAI APIs), analyzing model performance across different topics and model versions.
AI.result: The evaluated multimodal LLMs show limited accuracy on tasks that demand genuine visual interpretation and quantitative reading of materials science figures. While newer model versions show some overall accuracy improvements and do better on certain problem types, the models frequently answer correctly by leveraging memorized domain knowledge instead of actually reading the figures. The benchmark exposes notable deficiencies in visual reasoning, numerical precision, and handling of significant digits.
AI.conclusion: MaterialFigBench serves as a systematic, domain-specific benchmark for measuring and analyzing multimodal LLM performance on figure-based materials science problems. The study concludes that current LLMs still lack robust visual understanding and precise quantitative interpretation of scientific figures, underscoring the need for targeted advancements in multimodal reasoning and offering a basis to guide future model development in this direction.

title: AI Psychometrics: Evaluating the Psychological Reasoning of Large Language Models with Psychometric Validities
comment: Accepted for publication in the Proceedings of the 58th Hawaii International Conference on System Sciences (HICSS), 2025
summary: The immense number of parameters and deep neural networks make large language models (LLMs) rival the complexity of human brains, which also makes them opaque ``black box'' systems that are challenging to evaluate and interpret. AI Psychometrics is an emerging field that aims to tackle these challenges by applying psychometric methodologies to evaluate and interpret the psychological traits and processes of artificial intelligence (AI) systems. This paper investigates the application of AI Psychometrics to evaluate the psychological reasoning and overall psychometric validity of four prominent LLMs: GPT-3.5, GPT-4, LLaMA-2, and LLaMA-3. Using the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM), we examined convergent, discriminant, predictive, and external validity across these models. Our findings reveal that the responses from all these models generally met all validity criteria. Moreover, higher-performing models like GPT-4 and LLaMA-3 consistently demonstrated superior psychometric validity compared to their predecessors, GPT-3.5 and LLaMA-2. These results help to establish the validity of applying AI Psychometrics to evaluate and interpret large language models.

AI.tldr: The paper uses psychometric methods, via the Technology Acceptance Model, to test whether several large language models (GPT-3.5, GPT-4, LLaMA-2, LLaMA-3) show consistent, valid patterns in their responses, finding that more advanced models exhibit stronger psychometric validity and supporting AI Psychometrics as a viable evaluation approach.
AI.motivation: Large language models are extremely complex and opaque, making it difficult to evaluate and interpret their underlying psychological-like traits and reasoning. Traditional ML benchmarks focus on task performance but not on the structure, consistency, or validity of underlying cognitive-like processes. The authors are motivated to adapt established human psychometric frameworks to AI systems to gain a more systematic and interpretable way to assess their psychological reasoning and construct validity.
AI.method: The authors adopt the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) as a psychometric framework and administer TAM-style instruments to four LLMs: GPT-3.5, GPT-4, LLaMA-2, and LLaMA-3. They then evaluate multiple forms of validity—convergent, discriminant, predictive, and external validity—on the models’ responses, akin to how one would assess a psychological scale in humans, and compare validity patterns across models.
AI.result: All four LLMs produced responses that generally satisfied standard psychometric validity criteria under TAM. However, newer and higher-performing models, GPT-4 and LLaMA-3, showed more robust and consistent validity across the considered dimensions than GPT-3.5 and LLaMA-2, indicating a progression in psychometric soundness with model advancement.
AI.conclusion: AI Psychometrics, specifically using frameworks like TAM, is a promising and valid approach to assess and interpret psychological reasoning in large language models. The demonstrated validity across multiple LLMs, and the superior psychometric performance of more advanced models, supports using psychometric tools as part of the evaluation toolkit for AI systems and suggests that improving model capabilities may also enhance their psychometric coherence.
