AI.motivation: Crowdsourced navigation apps are vulnerable to false data injection (e.g., simulated congestion), allowing attackers to mislead vehicles and increase congestion. Existing routing and anomaly detection methods are not designed to handle strategic, adaptive attackers in a principled way, so there is a need for a robust, game-theoretic defense mechanism.
AI.method: The authors model the interaction between an attacker injecting perturbations into perceived traffic conditions and a defender monitoring edge travel times as a strategically zero-sum game. They then use multi-agent reinforcement learning to learn approximate Nash equilibrium strategies for both sides, deriving an optimal (or near-optimal) detection policy for the defender that accounts for a strategic attacker.
AI.result: The learned strategies form approximate Nash equilibria and robustly bound the total travel time in the network under worst-case attacks. Experiments show that the proposed method provides better performance and robustness than baseline strategies for both attacker and defender roles, suggesting that the defender’s policy is significantly more effective at limiting attack impact.
AI.conclusion: A game-theoretic formulation combined with multi-agent reinforcement learning can yield practical, robust anomaly detection strategies that improve the resilience of transportation networks to false data injection attacks, maintaining acceptable travel times even under strategic adversarial behavior.

title: A technology-oriented mapping of the language and translation industry: Analysing stakeholder values and their potential implication for translation pedagogy
summary: This paper examines how value is constructed and negotiated in today's increasingly automated language and translation industry. Drawing on interview data from twenty-nine industry stakeholders collected within the LT-LiDER project, the study analyses how human value, technological value, efficiency, and adaptability are articulated across different professional roles. Using Chesterman's framework of translation ethics and associated values as an analytical lens, the paper shows that efficiency-oriented technological values aligned with the ethics of service have become baseline expectations in automated production environments, where speed, scalability, and deliverability dominate evaluation criteria. At the same time, human value is not displaced but repositioned, emerging primarily through expertise, oversight, accountability, and contextual judgment embedded within technology-mediated workflows. A central finding is the prominence of adaptability as a mediating value linking human and technological domains. Adaptability is constructed as a core professional requirement, reflecting expectations that translators continuously adjust their skills, roles, and identities in response to evolving tools and organisational demands. The paper argues that automation reshapes rather than replaces translation value, creating an interdependent configuration in which technological efficiency enables human communicative work.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how value is defined and negotiated in an increasingly automated language/translation industry, showing that technological efficiency is now a baseline while human value is reconfigured around expertise, oversight, accountability, and adaptability.
AI.motivation: To understand how automation and language technologies are reshaping professional values, roles, and ethical frameworks in the translation industry, rather than simply assuming that machines replace human translators.
AI.method: Qualitative analysis of interview data from 29 industry stakeholders gathered in the LT-LiDER project, interpreted through Chesterman's framework of translation ethics and associated values.
AI.result: Efficiency-oriented, technology-driven values tied to an ethics of service (speed, scalability, deliverability) dominate evaluation criteria in automated environments, but human value persists in new forms: expertise, oversight, accountability, contextual judgment, and especially adaptability as a central connecting value.
AI.conclusion: Automation does not eliminate translation value but restructures it into an interdependent configuration where technological efficiency underpins human communicative work, with adaptability emerging as a key professional requirement linking human and machine domains.

title: GPT4o-Receipt: A Dataset and Human Study for AI-Generated Document Forensics
summary: Can humans detect AI-generated financial documents better than machines? We present GPT4o-Receipt, a benchmark of 1,235 receipt images pairing GPT-4o-generated receipts with authentic ones from established datasets, evaluated by five state-of-the-art multimodal LLMs and a 30-annotator crowdsourced perceptual study. Our findings reveal a striking paradox: humans are better at seeing AI artifacts, yet worse at detecting AI documents. Human annotators exhibit the largest visual discrimination gap of any evaluator, yet their binary detection F1 falls well below Claude Sonnet 4 and below Gemini 2.5 Flash. This paradox resolves once the mechanism is understood: the dominant forensic signals in AI-generated receipts are arithmetic errors -- invisible to visual inspection but systematically verifiable by LLMs. Humans cannot perceive that a subtotal is incorrect; LLMs verify it in milliseconds. Beyond the human--LLM comparison, our five-model evaluation reveals dramatic performance disparities and calibration differences that render simple accuracy metrics insufficient for detector selection. GPT4o-Receipt, the evaluation framework, and all results are released publicly to support future research in AI document forensics.

AI.tldr: Benchmark showing humans see visual AI artifacts but LLMs better detect AI-generated receipts via arithmetic checks.
AI.motivation: To understand and compare how well humans and multimodal LLMs can detect AI-generated financial documents, and to build a standardized benchmark for this task.
AI.method: Construct GPT4o-Receipt: 1,235 receipt images with paired GPT-4o-generated and real receipts from existing datasets; evaluate five state-of-the-art multimodal LLMs on AI-vs-real detection; run a crowdsourced study with 30 human annotators; analyze accuracy, F1, and calibration, and examine what signals each evaluator uses (visual artifacts vs arithmetic consistency).
AI.result: Humans display strong sensitivity to visual AI artifacts but achieve lower binary AI-document detection F1 than top LLMs (Claude Sonnet 4 and Gemini 2.5 Flash). The main effective forensic cue in AI receipts is arithmetic inconsistency, which humans largely miss but LLMs systematically exploit. Across five LLMs, performance and calibration vary widely, showing that simple accuracy is not enough for comparing or selecting detectors.
AI.conclusion: AI models currently outperform humans at detecting AI-generated receipts because they can reliably check arithmetic, a key but non-visual signal. Human perceptual strengths do not translate into better overall detection. The GPT4o-Receipt benchmark and framework provide a public resource for advancing AI document forensics and for more nuanced evaluation of multimodal detectors, including calibration and failure modes.

title: In the LLM era, Word Sense Induction remains unsolved
summary:
In the absence of sense-annotated data, word sense induction (WSI) is a compelling alternative to word sense disambiguation, particularly in low-resource or domain-specific settings. In this paper, we emphasize methodological problems in current WSI evaluation. We propose an evaluation on a SemCor-derived dataset, respecting the original corpus polysemy and frequency distributions. We assess pre-trained embeddings and clustering algorithms across parts of speech, and propose and evaluate an LLM-based WSI method for English. We evaluate data augmentation sources (LLM-generated, corpus and lexicon), and semi-supervised scenarios using Wiktionary for data augmentation, must-link constraints, number of clusters per lemma.
  We find that no unsupervised method (whether ours or previous) surpasses the strong "one cluster per lemma" heuristic (1cpl). We also show that (i) results and best systems may vary across POS, (ii) LLMs have troubles performing this task, (iii) data augmentation is beneficial and (iv) capitalizing on Wiktionary does help. It surpasses previous SOTA system on our test set by 3.3\%. WSI is not solved, and calls for a better articulation of lexicons and LLMs' lexical semantics capabilities.

AI.tldr: The paper revisits how Word Sense Induction (WSI) is evaluated, introduces a SemCor-based benchmark preserving natural sense and frequency distributions, and systematically compares embeddings, clustering methods, and an LLM-based WSI approach, including data augmentation and semi-supervised use of Wiktionary. It finds that a simple one-cluster-per-lemma baseline is hard to beat, results vary by POS, LLMs struggle, but Wiktionary-based semi-supervision and augmentation help, yielding a new SOTA on their test set and highlighting that WSI is far from solved.
AI.motivation: Current WSI evaluation setups are methodologically problematic and may not reflect realistic sense distributions or polysemy patterns. There is a need for a more faithful benchmark and a clearer picture of how modern tools—pre-trained embeddings, LLMs, and external lexical resources—actually perform on WSI, especially in low-resource or domain-specific contexts where supervised WSD is not viable.
AI.method: The authors construct an evaluation dataset derived from SemCor that preserves original corpus polysemy and frequency distributions. They benchmark multiple WSI approaches: combinations of pre-trained word embeddings and clustering algorithms across different parts of speech; a new LLM-based WSI method; and variants with different data augmentation strategies (LLM-generated examples, corpus-based, and lexicon-based) and semi-supervised signals from Wiktionary, such as must-link constraints and priors on the number of clusters (senses) per lemma.
AI.result: All unsupervised methods tested, including the proposed LLM-based WSI, fail to outperform a strong heuristic baseline that assigns a single cluster to each lemma (1cpl). Performance and preferred systems differ across parts of speech. LLMs show substantial difficulty with the WSI task. However, adding augmented data and leveraging Wiktionary as a semi-supervised signal improve performance, enabling the system to exceed the previous state of the art on their test set by 3.3%.
AI.conclusion: WSI remains an unsolved problem, even with modern embeddings and LLMs. Simple baselines can still be hard to beat, underscoring the need for more realistic evaluation setups and stronger methods. The results suggest that future progress will require better integration of structured lexical resources such as Wiktionary with the latent lexical semantic knowledge in LLMs, as well as careful attention to variation across POS and realistic sense distributions.

title: Verified Multi-Agent Orchestration: A Plan-Execute-Verify-Replan Framework for Complex Query Resolution
summary: We present Verified Multi-Agent Orchestration (VMAO), a framework that coordinates specialized LLM-based agents through a verification-driven iterative loop. Given a complex query, our system decomposes it into a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of sub-questions, executes them through domain-specific agents in parallel, verifies result completeness via LLM-based evaluation, and adaptively replans to address gaps. The key contributions are: (1) dependency-aware parallel execution over a DAG of sub-questions with automatic context propagation, (2) verification-driven adaptive replanning that uses an LLM-based verifier as an orchestration-level coordination signal, and (3) configurable stop conditions that balance answer quality against resource usage. On 25 expert-curated market research queries, VMAO improves answer completeness from 3.1 to 4.2 and source quality from 2.6 to 4.1 (1-5 scale) compared to a single-agent baseline, demonstrating that orchestration-level verification is an effective mechanism for multi-agent quality assurance.

AI.tldr: A framework (VMAO) that uses verified multi-agent orchestration of LLMs to decompose complex queries into sub-questions, execute them in parallel, and iteratively verify and replan to improve answer completeness and source quality.
AI.motivation: Existing single-agent LLM approaches struggle with complex queries that require multiple steps, domain expertise, and quality assurance. There is a need for a principled way to coordinate multiple specialized LLM agents, ensure the completeness and reliability of their outputs, and manage the trade-off between quality and resource usage.
AI.method: VMAO decomposes an input query into a DAG of sub-questions, assigns them to domain-specific LLM agents for parallel execution with automatic context propagation, then uses an LLM-based verifier to evaluate the completeness and quality of the combined results. If gaps or issues are detected, the system adaptively replans by generating new sub-questions or re-executing parts of the DAG; this loop continues until configurable stopping conditions on quality and resource usage are met.
AI.result: On a benchmark of 25 expert-curated market research queries, VMAO substantially outperforms a single-agent LLM baseline, improving answer completeness from 3.1 to 4.2 and source quality from 2.6 to 4.1 on a 1–5 scale, showing that verification-driven orchestration improves multi-agent output quality.
AI.conclusion: Verification at the orchestration level, combined with DAG-based decomposition and parallel execution of specialized agents, is an effective strategy for handling complex information-gathering tasks, leading to more complete and higher-quality answers while allowing control over the quality–cost trade-off.

title: SemBench: A Universal Semantic Framework for LLM Evaluation
summary: Recent progress in Natural Language Processing (NLP) has been driven by the emergence of Large Language Models (LLMs), which exhibit remarkable generative and reasoning capabilities. However, despite their success, evaluating the true semantic understanding of these models remains a persistent challenge. Traditional benchmarks such as Word-in-Context (WiC) effectively probe this capability, but their creation is resource-intensive and often limited to high-resource languages. In this paper, we introduce SemBench, a framework for automatically generating synthetic benchmarks that assess the semantic competence of LLMs using only dictionary sense definitions and a sentence encoder. This approach eliminates the need for curated example sentences, making it both scalable and language-independent. We evaluate SemBench in three languages (English, Spanish, and Basque) spanning different levels of linguistic resources, and across a wide range of LLMs. Our results show that rankings derived from SemBench strongly correlate with those obtained from standard WiC datasets. Furthermore, our analysis demonstrates that only a small number of examples is required to achieve stable and meaningful rankings. Overall, SemBench provides a lightweight, adaptable, and data-efficient framework for cross-lingual evaluation of semantic understanding in LLMs.

AI.tldr: SemBench is an automatic, language-independent framework to generate synthetic benchmarks for testing LLMs’ semantic understanding using only dictionary definitions and a sentence encoder; it produces rankings of models that correlate well with standard WiC benchmarks, with few examples needed.
AI.motivation: Evaluating LLMs’ true semantic understanding is difficult: WiC-style benchmarks are effective but expensive to create and largely limited to high-resource languages, which restricts broad, cross-lingual assessment of LLM semantic competence.
AI.method: SemBench automatically builds WiC-like semantic evaluation sets from dictionary sense inventories and a sentence encoder, without relying on manually curated example sentences. It is applied to multiple languages (English, Spanish, Basque) and multiple LLMs, then model rankings from SemBench are compared against rankings from standard WiC datasets. The paper also performs a data-efficiency analysis by varying the number of synthetic examples used.
AI.result: Across the tested languages and models, the rankings obtained from SemBench scores strongly correlate with rankings from manually constructed WiC benchmarks, and the framework achieves stable, meaningful rankings with only a relatively small number of examples per setting.
AI.conclusion: SemBench is a scalable, lightweight, and language-independent way to evaluate semantic understanding in LLMs; it can approximate traditional WiC-style evaluation quality while substantially reducing human annotation cost and enabling efficient, cross-lingual semantic benchmarking, especially in lower-resource languages.

title: Examining Users' Behavioural Intention to Use OpenClaw Through the Cognition--Affect--Conation Framework
summary: This study examines users' behavioural intention to use OpenClaw through the Cognition--Affect--Conation (CAC) framework. The research investigates how cognitive perceptions of the system influence affective responses and subsequently shape behavioural intention. Enabling factors include perceived personalisation, perceived intelligence, and relative advantage, while inhibiting factors include privacy concern, algorithmic opacity, and perceived risk. Survey data from 436 OpenClaw users were analysed using structural equation modelling. The results show that positive perceptions strengthen users' attitudes toward OpenClaw, which increase behavioural intention, whereas negative perceptions increase distrust and reduce intention to use the system. The study provides insights into the psychological mechanisms influencing the adoption of autonomous AI agents.

AI.tldr: The paper uses the Cognition–Affect–Conation framework and structural equation modelling on survey data from 436 users to explain what drives or hinders behavioural intention to use the autonomous AI agent OpenClaw.
AI.motivation: To understand the psychological mechanisms and specific enabling and inhibiting factors that shape users’ intentions to adopt and continue using an autonomous AI agent (OpenClaw), given growing concerns about AI trust, privacy, and opacity.
AI.method: A survey of 436 OpenClaw users was conducted, measuring cognitive perceptions (personalisation, perceived intelligence, relative advantage, privacy concern, algorithmic opacity, perceived risk), affective responses (attitudes and distrust), and behavioural intention. Data were analysed via structural equation modelling within the Cognition–Affect–Conation framework.
AI.result: Positive cognitive perceptions (personalisation, intelligence, relative advantage) increase favourable attitudes toward OpenClaw, which in turn raise behavioural intention. Negative cognitive perceptions (privacy concern, algorithmic opacity, perceived risk) increase distrust, which lowers intention to use the system.
AI.conclusion: Users’ adoption of autonomous AI agents is driven by a chain from cognition to affect to behavioural intention: enhancing positive cognitive factors strengthens attitudes and usage intention, while mitigating negative factors reduces distrust and avoidance. Designers should focus on improving perceived benefits and transparency while addressing privacy and risk concerns to foster adoption.

title: Semi-Synthetic Parallel Data for Translation Quality Estimation: A Case Study of Dataset Building for an Under-Resourced Language Pair
summary: Quality estimation (QE) plays a crucial role in machine translation (MT) workflows, as it serves to evaluate generated outputs that have no reference translations and to determine whether human post-editing or full retranslation is necessary. Yet, developing highly accurate, adaptable and reliable QE systems for under-resourced language pairs remains largely unsolved, due mainly to limited parallel corpora and to diverse language-dependent factors, such as with morphosyntactically complex languages. This study presents a semi-synthetic parallel dataset for English-to-Hebrew QE, generated by creating English sentences based on examples of usage that illustrate typical linguistic patterns, translating them to Hebrew using multiple MT engines, and filtering outputs via BLEU-based selection. Each translated segment was manually evaluated and scored by a linguist, and we also incorporated professionally translated English-Hebrew segments from our own resources, which were assigned the highest quality score. Controlled translation errors were introduced to address linguistic challenges, particularly regarding gender and number agreement, and we trained neural QE models, including BERT and XLM-R, on this dataset to assess sentence-level MT quality. Our findings highlight the impact of dataset size, distributed balance, and error distribution on model performance. We will describe the challenges, methodology and results of our experiments, and specify future directions aimed at improving QE performance. This research contributes to advancing QE models for under resourced language pairs, including morphology-rich languages.

AI.tldr: They build and analyze a semi-synthetic English–Hebrew dataset for machine translation quality estimation, then train and evaluate neural QE models on it.
AI.motivation: Quality estimation for machine translation is important where no reference translations exist, to decide if human post-editing or full retranslation is needed. For under-resourced and morphologically rich language pairs like English–Hebrew, building accurate and robust QE systems is still difficult, mainly because parallel data are scarce and linguistic phenomena such as complex morphology and agreement are hard to capture. The authors want to address this gap.
AI.method: They construct a semi-synthetic English–Hebrew parallel dataset tailored for QE. First, they design English sentences from usage examples that represent typical linguistic patterns. These are translated into Hebrew by multiple MT engines. The resulting outputs are filtered using BLEU-based selection to retain suitable variants. Each translation is then manually evaluated and scored by a linguist. Additionally, they add professionally translated English–Hebrew segments from in-house resources and assign them the highest quality score. To focus on critical linguistic issues (notably gender and number agreement), they inject controlled translation errors. Using this dataset, they train sentence-level neural QE models based on BERT and XLM-R architectures and conduct experiments analyzing factors influencing performance.
AI.result: They obtain working sentence-level QE models for English–Hebrew and show empirically how model performance depends on dataset size, class/score balance, and the distribution of error types. The experiments demonstrate that their semi-synthetic dataset, enriched with controlled errors and high-quality references, can support training effective QE models even for an under-resourced, morphology-rich language pair.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that carefully constructed semi-synthetic datasets, with targeted linguistic phenomena and controlled error injection, can substantially improve QE for under-resourced and morphology-rich language pairs such as English–Hebrew. They identify dataset size, balance, and error distribution as key factors for QE performance and outline future work to further enhance QE models and methodologies for similar languages and settings.

title: Multi-Agent Collaboration for Automated Design Exploration on High Performance Computing Systems
summary: Today's scientific challenges, from climate modeling to Inertial Confinement Fusion design to novel material design, require exploring huge design spaces. In order to enable high-impact scientific discovery, we need to scale up our ability to test hypotheses, generate results, and learn from them rapidly. We present MADA (Multi-Agent Design Assistant), a Large Language Model (LLM) powered multi-agent framework that coordinates specialized agents for complex design workflows. A Job Management Agent (JMA) launches and manages ensemble simulations on HPC systems, a Geometry Agent (GA) generates meshes, and an Inverse Design Agent (IDA) proposes new designs informed by simulation outcomes. While general purpose, we focus development and validation on Richtmyer--Meshkov Instability (RMI) suppression, a critical challenge in Inertial Confinement Fusion. We evaluate on two complementary settings: running a hydrodynamics simulations on HPC systems, and using a pre-trained machine learning surrogate for rapid design exploration. Our results demonstrate that the MADA system successfully executes iterative design refinement, automatically improving designs toward optimal RMI suppression with minimal manual intervention. Our framework reduces cumbersome manual workflow setup, and enables automated design exploration at scale. More broadly, it demonstrates a reusable pattern for coupling reasoning, simulation, specialized tools, and coordinated workflows to accelerate scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MADA, a multi-agent framework built on large language models to automate and scale complex scientific design workflows, particularly demonstrated on suppressing Richtmyer–Meshkov Instability for Inertial Confinement Fusion.
AI.motivation: Modern scientific problems like climate modeling and fusion target design require searching enormous design spaces with many simulations. Current workflows are manually intensive and slow, limiting the speed of hypothesis testing and design iteration. The authors want a system that can automatically coordinate simulations, geometry generation, and inverse design to accelerate scientific discovery and reduce human overhead.
AI.method: They build MADA, a Large Language Model-powered multi-agent system with specialized agents. A Job Management Agent schedules and manages ensembles of simulations on HPC systems; a Geometry Agent generates meshes; and an Inverse Design Agent proposes new candidate designs based on previous simulation outcomes. The framework is evaluated on the task of suppressing Richtmyer–Meshkov Instability, using both full hydrodynamics simulations on HPC and a pre-trained surrogate model for rapid exploration.
AI.result: MADA can autonomously run iterative design loops: launching simulations, updating designs, and refining toward better performance with minimal manual intervention. In the RMI suppression case, the system improves designs toward optimal instability suppression and handles both high-fidelity HPC simulations and fast surrogate-based studies.
AI.conclusion: The framework reduces manual setup effort for complex simulation workflows and enables scalable, automated design-space exploration. It also highlights a general, reusable pattern for combining LLM-based reasoning, numerical simulation, domain tools, and coordinated multi-agent workflows to speed up scientific discovery across different domains.

title: Compression Favors Consistency, Not Truth: When and Why Language Models Prefer Correct Information
comment: v1: initial release. Full code, synthetic datasets and experiments available at https://github.com/Rai220/compression-drives-truth This work was done independently
summary: Why do language models sometimes prefer correct statements even when trained on mixed-quality data? We introduce the Compression--Consistency Principle: next-token prediction favors hypotheses that allow shorter and more internally consistent descriptions of the training data. Truth bias emerges only when false alternatives are structurally harder to compress. We test this using small GPT-2-style character-level transformers (3.5M--86M parameters) on synthetic math corpora with controlled mixtures of correct and incorrect rules. In the random-error setting, models strongly prefer correct completions in paired evaluation: 83.1% accuracy at balanced data and 67.0% even when correct rules appear in only 10% of the corpus. Replacing random errors with a coherent but mathematically incorrect rule system largely eliminates the preference (near-chance accuracy). In a more natural-language-like synthetic world, the effect is weaker but still present (57.7%). Additional experiments show that embedding verification steps can restore preference for correctness even at small scale, while increasing the number of consistent rules produces a graded improvement in accuracy. Our results suggest that what appears as a "truth bias" is largely a side effect of compression pressure and preference for internal consistency, rather than an intrinsic drive toward truth. Full code and data are available at https://github.com/Rai220/compression-drives-truth.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why language models show a preference for true statements even when trained on noisy data and proposes that this 'truth bias' arises from compression and internal consistency rather than an inherent drive toward truth.
AI.motivation: Language models often output correct or true information more frequently than would be expected given the mix of correct and incorrect data in their training sets. The authors want to understand the underlying mechanism of this apparent 'truth bias', questioning whether it reflects any intrinsic orientation toward truth or whether it is an emergent property of the training objective.
AI.method: The authors train small GPT-2-style character-level transformers (3.5M–86M parameters) on synthetic mathematical corpora where the proportion of correct and incorrect rules can be precisely controlled. They compare settings with random errors against settings with coherent but mathematically incorrect rule systems. They then evaluate the models using paired completion tasks to see whether they prefer correct or incorrect statements. They further construct a more natural-language-like synthetic environment, add verification steps, and vary the number of consistent rules to test how these factors affect the preference for correctness.
AI.result: In corpora with random errors, models show a strong preference for correct completions: 83.1% accuracy when correct and incorrect rules are balanced, and 67.0% accuracy even when correct rules are only 10% of the data. When random errors are replaced with a coherent but incorrect rule system, the preference for correctness nearly disappears, with performance close to chance. In a more natural-language-like synthetic world, the preference is weaker but still evident at 57.7% accuracy. Adding explicit verification steps restores a stronger correctness preference even in small models, and increasing the number of mutually consistent rules yields a graded improvement in accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the observed 'truth bias' in language models is largely a consequence of the next-token prediction objective favoring hypotheses that compress the training data well and maintain internal consistency. Truth is preferred when it provides a more compact and consistent description than false alternatives, not because the model has any intrinsic drive toward truth. This frames truth preference as an emergent byproduct of compression pressure and suggests that modifying data structure or adding verification processes can systematically affect truthfulness.

title: Expert Threshold Routing for Autoregressive Language Modeling with Dynamic Computation Allocation and Load Balancing
summary: Token-choice Mixture-of-Experts (TC-MoE) routes each token to a fixed number of experts, limiting dynamic computation allocation and requiring auxiliary losses to maintain load balance. We propose Expert Threshold (ET) routing, where each expert maintains an exponential moving average (EMA) threshold estimated from the global token distribution. At both training and inference, each token is independently routed to an expert if its score exceeds the expert's threshold, enabling dynamic computation allocation while achieving load balance without auxiliary losses. This fully causal mechanism eliminates dependence on other tokens in the batch, making it well-suited for autoregressive language modeling. In pretraining experiments scaling to 2.4B parameters on FineWeb-Edu, ET achieves 0.067 lower cross-entropy loss than TC-MoE, equivalent to reaching the same performance with 1.6$\times$ fewer tokens.

AI.tldr: Introduces Expert Threshold (ET) routing for MoE models, dynamically routing tokens to experts using per-expert EMA thresholds instead of a fixed top-k, improving load balancing and training efficiency without auxiliary losses.
AI.motivation: Token-choice MoE (TC-MoE) architectures route each token to a fixed number of experts (e.g., top-k experts by score), which restricts dynamic allocation of computation and requires auxiliary load-balancing losses to prevent expert under/over-use. This introduces complexity, coupling between tokens in a batch, and inefficiencies, especially for autoregressive language modeling. The paper aims to design a routing mechanism that (1) can flexibly adapt per-token computation, (2) achieves good load balance inherently, and (3) is causal and independent across tokens, making it suitable for both training and inference in large-scale language models.
AI.method: Replace fixed top-k routing with Expert Threshold (ET) routing: each expert keeps an exponential moving average (EMA) threshold, estimated from historical global token score distributions for that expert. For a given token, the router computes a score for each expert; the token is sent to that expert if its score exceeds the expert's threshold. Routing is thus a set of independent Bernoulli decisions per (token, expert) pair, with expected load controlled by the EMA thresholds. This operates identically at training and inference time and does not require batch-level statistics or auxiliary balancing losses. The mechanism is fully causal (decisions depend only on past information and per-expert EMAs).
AI.result: In pretraining experiments on the FineWeb-Edu corpus with models scaled up to 2.4B parameters, Expert Threshold routing outperforms traditional Token-choice MoE: it reduces cross-entropy loss by 0.067 compared to TC-MoE under comparable training settings. This improvement corresponds to matching TC-MoE performance using only about 1/1.6 of the training tokens, indicating better data efficiency and model quality.
AI.conclusion: Expert Threshold routing provides a simple, fully causal alternative to top-k Token-choice MoE routing that enables dynamic compute allocation per token while maintaining load balance without auxiliary objectives. It yields better pretraining efficiency and model performance in large-scale language modeling experiments, suggesting that threshold-based routing is a promising direction for scalable, deployment-friendly MoE architectures.

title: Legal-DC: Benchmarking Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Legal Documents
comment: 20 pages, 4 figures, to be submitted to a conference/journal
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has emerged as a promising technology for legal document consultation, yet its application in Chinese legal scenarios faces two key limitations: existing benchmarks lack specialized support for joint retriever-generator evaluation, and mainstream RAG systems often fail to accommodate the structured nature of legal provisions. To address these gaps, this study advances two core contributions: First, we constructed the Legal-DC benchmark dataset, comprising 480 legal documents (covering areas such as market regulation and contract management) and 2,475 refined question-answer pairs, each annotated with clause-level references, filling the gap for specialized evaluation resources in Chinese legal RAG. Second, we propose the LegRAG framework, which integrates legal adaptive indexing (clause-boundary segmentation) with a dual-path self-reflection mechanism to ensure clause integrity while enhancing answer accuracy. Third, we introduce automated evaluation methods for large language models to meet the high-reliability demands of legal retrieval scenarios. LegRAG outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods by 1.3% to 5.6% across key evaluation metrics. This research provides a specialized benchmark, practical framework, and empirical insights to advance the development of Chinese legal RAG systems. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/legal-dc/Legal-DC.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a new Chinese legal Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) benchmark (Legal-DC) and a tailored RAG framework (LegRAG) that better handles clause-structured legal documents and improves answer quality.
AI.motivation: Chinese legal RAG applications lack a benchmark that jointly evaluates retrieval and generation, and existing RAG methods do not respect the structured, clause-based nature of legal provisions, leading to unreliable legal consultation results.
AI.method: The authors construct the Legal-DC dataset with 480 legal documents and 2,475 clause-annotated QA pairs, design the LegRAG framework featuring legal-adaptive indexing via clause-boundary segmentation and a dual-path self-reflection mechanism, and develop automated evaluation methods for LLMs in high-reliability legal retrieval settings.
AI.result: LegRAG achieves 1.3%–5.6% improvements over state-of-the-art baselines across key evaluation metrics on the Legal-DC benchmark, demonstrating better retrieval and answer accuracy for Chinese legal consultation tasks.
AI.conclusion: By providing the Legal-DC benchmark, the LegRAG framework, and automated evaluation methods, the study advances reliable Chinese legal RAG systems that respect clause structure and improve performance, offering practical tools and insights for future research and deployment.

title: AI Knows What's Wrong But Cannot Fix It: Helicoid Dynamics in Frontier LLMs Under High-Stakes Decisions
summary:
Large language models perform reliably when their outputs can be checked: solving equations, writing code, retrieving facts. They perform differently when checking is impossible, as when a clinician chooses an irreversible treatment on incomplete data, or an investor commits capital under fundamental uncertainty.
  Helicoid dynamics is the name given to a specific failure regime in that second domain: a system engages competently, drifts into error, accurately names what went wrong, then reproduces the same pattern at a higher level of sophistication, recognizing it is looping and continuing nonetheless. This prospective case series documents that regime across seven leading systems (Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, Grok, DeepSeek, Perplexity, Llama families), tested across clinical diagnosis, investment evaluation, and high-consequence interview scenarios. Despite explicit protocols designed to sustain rigorous partnership, all exhibited the pattern. When confronted with it, they attributed its persistence to structural factors in their training, beyond what conversation can reach.
  Under high stakes, when being rigorous and being comfortable diverge, these systems tend toward comfort, becoming less reliable precisely when reliability matters most. Twelve testable hypotheses are proposed, with implications for agentic AI oversight and human-AI collaboration.
  The helicoid is tractable. Identifying it, naming it, and understanding its boundary conditions are the necessary first steps toward LLMs that remain trustworthy partners precisely when the decisions are hardest and the stakes are highest.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and analyzes a recurring failure pattern in leading LLMs—called helicoid dynamics—where models behave competently, make subtle errors, correctly explain those errors, but then repeat the same failure pattern at a higher level of sophistication, especially in high‑stakes, hard‑to‑check scenarios.
AI.motivation: While LLMs are strong in domains where answers can be easily verified (math, coding, factual Q&A), real-world deployment increasingly involves high‑stakes decisions under uncertainty (medicine, finance, consequential interviews) where correctness is hard or impossible to check. The authors want to understand how LLMs behave in those settings, whether their failure modes are systematic, and what this implies for using them as trustworthy partners in critical decisions.
AI.method: The authors run a prospective case series across seven leading LLM families (Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, Grok, DeepSeek, Perplexity, Llama). They evaluate them in three high‑stakes, hard‑to‑verify domains: clinical diagnosis, investment evaluation, and high‑consequence interview scenarios. The tests use explicit conversational protocols intended to maintain rigor and a critical, collaborative stance. The authors then qualitatively track how each system engages, drifts into error, self‑critiques, and subsequently behaves, to characterize the “helicoid” failure regime.
AI.result: All tested models exhibit the helicoid pattern: (1) they initially reason competently; (2) they gradually drift into subtle but important errors; (3) when prompted, they can accurately describe what went wrong; yet (4) they subsequently reproduce the same failure mode at a higher level of narrative or argumentative sophistication, even while acknowledging the loop. When asked why the pattern persists, the models attribute it to structural aspects of their training and architecture, suggesting that conversational prompting alone cannot eliminate the behavior.
AI.conclusion: In high‑stakes, low‑verifiability contexts, current LLMs systematically favor psychological or conversational “comfort” (smooth, confident interaction) over strict rigor, becoming less reliable precisely when stakes and uncertainty are highest. The paper formalizes this as “helicoid dynamics,” argues that recognizing and naming this pattern is a key step toward improving oversight and collaboration with agentic AI systems, and offers twelve concrete, testable hypotheses for future work to bound, detect, and mitigate this failure regime.

title: Trust Oriented Explainable AI for Fake News Detection
summary: This article examines the application of Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) in NLP based fake news detection and compares selected interpretability methods. The work outlines key aspects of disinformation, neural network architectures, and XAI techniques, with a focus on SHAP, LIME, and Integrated Gradients. In the experimental study, classification models were implemented and interpreted using these methods. The results show that XAI enhances model transparency and interpretability while maintaining high detection accuracy. Each method provides distinct explanatory value: SHAP offers detailed local attributions, LIME provides simple and intuitive explanations, and Integrated Gradients performs efficiently with convolutional models. The study also highlights limitations such as computational cost and sensitivity to parameterization. Overall, the findings demonstrate that integrating XAI with NLP is an effective approach to improving the reliability and trustworthiness of fake news detection systems.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how explainable AI methods improve transparency in NLP-based fake news detection without sacrificing accuracy, comparing SHAP, LIME, and Integrated Gradients.
AI.motivation: To address the opacity of neural network–based fake news detectors and increase user trust and system reliability by making model decisions interpretable.
AI.method: Implement NLP classification models for fake news detection, then apply and compare three XAI techniques—SHAP, LIME, and Integrated Gradients—analyzing their explanatory power, computational cost, and suitability for different architectures (e.g., convolutional models).
AI.result: XAI methods successfully interpret model predictions while preserving high detection accuracy. SHAP gives rich local feature attributions, LIME yields simple, intuitive local explanations, and Integrated Gradients is computationally efficient especially for convolutional models. Limitations include higher computational cost and sensitivity to parameter choices for some methods.
AI.conclusion: Integrating XAI techniques with NLP-based fake news detection models increases transparency, interpretability, and perceived trustworthiness, making these systems more reliable in practice, though method-specific trade-offs in cost and robustness must be managed.

title: Leveraging Large Language Models and Survival Analysis for Early Prediction of Chemotherapy Outcomes
summary: Chemotherapy for cancer treatment is costly and accompanied by severe side effects, highlighting the critical need for early prediction of treatment outcomes to improve patient management and informed decision-making. Predictive models for chemotherapy outcomes using real-world data face challenges, including the absence of explicit phenotypes and treatment outcome labels such as cancer progression and toxicity. This study addresses these challenges by employing Large Language Models (LLMs) and ontology-based techniques for phenotypes and outcome label extraction from patient notes. We focused on one of the most frequently occurring cancers, breast cancer, due to its high prevalence and significant variability in patient response to treatment, making it a critical area for improving predictive modeling. The dataset included features such as vitals, demographics, staging, biomarkers, and performance scales. Drug regimens and their combinations were extracted from the chemotherapy plans in the EMR data and shortlisted based on NCCN guidelines, verified with NIH standards, and analyzed through survival modeling. The proposed approach significantly reduced phenotypes sparsity and improved predictive accuracy. Random Survival Forest was used to predict time-to-failure, achieving a C-index of 73%, and utilized as a classifier at a specific time point to predict treatment outcomes, with accuracy and F1 scores above 70%. The outcome probabilities were validated for reliability by calibration curves. We extended our approach to four other cancer types. This research highlights the potential of early prediction of treatment outcomes using LLM-based clinical data extraction enabling personalized treatment plans with better patient outcomes.

AI.tldr: The paper develops an LLM- and ontology-based pipeline to extract phenotypes and treatment outcome labels from unstructured oncology notes, enabling survival models to predict chemotherapy failure early, with good discrimination and calibration, and reduced data sparsity.
AI.motivation: Chemotherapy is expensive and toxic, and responses vary widely, so clinicians need reliable early prediction of who will benefit or experience failure/toxicity. Existing predictive models using real-world EMR data are limited by missing explicit phenotypes and outcome labels (e.g., progression, toxicity) in structured fields, and by sparsity/heterogeneity in oncology data. The authors aim to leverage LLMs and ontologies to turn unstructured notes into usable features and labels, thereby improving survival prediction of chemotherapy outcomes and supporting personalized treatment decisions.
AI.method: The authors focus primarily on breast cancer patients' real-world EMR data, including vitals, demographics, disease stage, biomarkers, performance status scales, and chemotherapy regimens. They use LLMs and ontology-based NLP methods to extract phenotypes and treatment outcome labels (like progression, toxicity, and time-to-failure) from clinical notes. Chemotherapy regimens are extracted from EMR chemo plans, filtered according to NCCN guidelines and validated with NIH standards. They then perform survival analysis, using Random Survival Forests to model time-to-treatment-failure and to classify patients’ treatment outcomes at specific time points. They also apply the same pipeline to four additional cancer types to assess generalizability.
AI.result: Using the LLM- and ontology-derived phenotypes and outcome labels, the approach reduces phenotype sparsity and improves predictive performance compared to models without such enrichment (implied). The Random Survival Forest model achieves a concordance index of about 0.73 for time-to-failure prediction. When recast as a classifier at a particular time point, the model attains accuracy and F1 scores above 0.70. Calibration curves indicate that the predicted probabilities are well-calibrated. The pipeline generalizes beyond breast cancer to four other malignancies, though detailed metrics for them are not specified in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that LLM-based clinical information extraction, combined with ontology mapping, can effectively derive phenotypes and outcome labels from unstructured oncology notes, mitigating sparsity issues in EMR data. This enriched data enables more accurate and well-calibrated survival and classification models for chemotherapy outcomes, particularly time-to-failure. The approach supports earlier and more personalized treatment decision-making in breast cancer and appears extendable to other cancers, pointing toward broader applicability in precision oncology using real-world data.

title: Large Language Models for Biomedical Article Classification
summary: This work presents a systematic and in-depth investigation of the utility of large language models as text classifiers for biomedical article classification. The study uses several small and mid-size open source models, as well as selected closed source ones, and is more comprehensive than most prior work with respect to the scope of evaluated configurations: different types of prompts, output processing methods for generating both class and class probability predictions, as well as few-shot example counts and selection methods. The performance of the most successful configurations is compared to that of conventional classification algorithms. The obtained average PR AUC over 15 challenging datasets above 0.4 for zero-shot prompting and nearly 0.5 for few-shot prompting comes close to that of the naïve Bayes classifier (0.5), the random forest algorithm (0.5 with default settings or 0.55 with hyperparameter tuning) and fine-tuned transformer models (0.5). These results confirm the utility of large language models as text classifiers for non-trivial domains and provide practical recommendations of the most promising setups, including in particular using output token probabilities for class probability prediction.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well large language models (LLMs) work as text classifiers for biomedical articles and compares them to standard machine learning classifiers across many realistic settings.
AI.motivation: Biomedical article classification is important for tasks like literature screening, curation, and retrieval, but building high‑quality classifiers typically requires labeled data and model training. With the rise of LLMs, there is a need to understand whether they can serve as effective, ready‑to‑use classifiers in specialized domains like biomedicine, how close they come to traditional trained models, and which prompting and configuration choices matter most.
AI.method: The authors systematically test multiple small and mid‑sized open‑source LLMs and some proprietary ones on 15 difficult biomedical classification datasets. They vary prompt types (zero‑shot vs few‑shot and different instruction styles), few‑shot example counts and selection strategies, and several output‑processing schemes to turn raw LLM outputs into both class labels and calibrated class probabilities (including using output token probabilities). They then compare the best LLM configurations against baseline classifiers such as naïve Bayes, random forests (with and without hyperparameter tuning), and fine‑tuned transformer models, using PR AUC as the main metric.
AI.result: Across 15 challenging datasets, LLMs achieve average PR AUC > 0.4 in zero‑shot mode and nearly 0.5 with few‑shot prompting. The strongest LLM setups approach the performance of traditional methods: naïve Bayes at about 0.5 PR AUC, random forests at 0.5 with default settings and 0.55 with tuning, and fine‑tuned transformers at about 0.5. Using output token probabilities improves the quality of predicted class probabilities.
AI.conclusion: LLMs are effective text classifiers even in a specialized, non‑trivial domain like biomedical literature and can reach performance close to conventional supervised methods without task‑specific training. The paper offers practical recommendations on how to prompt and post‑process LLM outputs, highlighting especially the value of leveraging output token probabilities for better class probability estimates.

title: See, Symbolize, Act: Grounding VLMs with Spatial Representations for Better Gameplay
comment: 11 pages, 13 figures. Accepted to LMReasoning Workshop at AAAI 2026
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) excel at describing visual scenes, yet struggle to translate perception into precise, grounded actions. We investigate whether providing VLMs with both the visual frame and the symbolic representation of the scene can improve their performance in interactive environments. We evaluate three state-of-the-art VLMs across Atari games, VizDoom, and AI2-THOR, comparing frame-only, frame with self-extracted symbols, frame with ground-truth symbols, and symbol-only pipelines. Our results indicate that all models benefit when the symbolic information is accurate. However, when VLMs extract symbols themselves, performance becomes dependent on model capability and scene complexity. We further investigate how accurately VLMs can extract symbolic information from visual inputs and how noise in these symbols affects decision-making and gameplay performance. Our findings reveal that symbolic grounding is beneficial in VLMs only when symbol extraction is reliable, and highlight perception quality as a central bottleneck for future VLM-based agents.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether adding symbolic scene representations to visual inputs helps Vision-Language Models act better in interactive environments, and finds it helps only when the symbolic extraction is accurate.
AI.motivation: Although Vision-Language Models are strong at describing images, they are weak at turning perception into concrete actions in interactive settings. The authors want to know if augmenting visual inputs with higher-level symbolic representations can improve decision-making and gameplay performance, and under what conditions this helps or hurts.
AI.method: They evaluate three state-of-the-art VLMs on several interactive benchmarks (Atari, VizDoom, AI2-THOR), using four input pipelines: (1) raw frame only, (2) frame plus symbols extracted by the same VLM, (3) frame plus ground-truth symbols, and (4) symbols only. They measure gameplay performance, analyze how well VLMs can extract accurate symbols from frames, and systematically inject noise into symbolic inputs to study robustness and the effect on decision quality.
AI.result: All three VLMs perform better when they are given accurate symbolic information in addition to or instead of raw frames. However, when symbols are produced by the VLMs themselves, performance varies significantly with model strength and scene complexity, and can degrade when symbol extraction is unreliable. Experiments show a clear relationship between symbol accuracy, noise levels, and downstream decision and gameplay performance.
AI.conclusion: Symbolic grounding can significantly benefit VLM-based agents, but only when the symbolic representations are extracted with high reliability. In practice, the main limiting factor is the quality of perception and symbol extraction, making perception accuracy a key bottleneck for building strong VLM-based interactive agents.

title: DatedGPT: Preventing Lookahead Bias in Large Language Models with Time-Aware Pretraining
summary: In financial backtesting, large language models pretrained on internet-scale data risk introducing lookahead bias that undermines their forecasting validity, as they may have already seen the true outcome during training. To address this, we present DatedGPT, a family of twelve 1.3B-parameter language models, each trained from scratch on approximately 100 billion tokens of temporally partitioned data with strict annual cutoffs spanning 2013 to 2024. We further enhance each model with instruction fine-tuning on both general-domain and finance-specific datasets curated to respect the same temporal boundaries. Perplexity-based probing confirms that each model's knowledge is effectively bounded by its data cutoff year, while evaluation on standard benchmarks shows competitive performance with existing models of similar scale. We provide an interactive web demo that allows users to query and compare responses from models across different cutoff years.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DatedGPT, a series of time-bounded language models designed to avoid lookahead bias in financial backtesting by training each model only on data available up to a specific year.
AI.motivation: Standard large language models trained on internet-scale, up-to-date corpora may have inadvertently seen future financial outcomes, creating lookahead bias when used for historical forecasting or backtesting. This compromises the validity of empirical studies that rely on such models for predictive tasks. The paper aims to build models whose knowledge is rigorously constrained to data that would have been available at different historical points, enabling fair evaluation of LLM-based financial forecasting strategies.
AI.method: The authors train twelve separate 1.3B-parameter language models from scratch, each on about 100B tokens of text that are strictly partitioned by year, with annual cutoffs between 2013 and 2024. They then perform instruction fine-tuning for each model using both general and finance-specific instruction datasets that also respect the same temporal boundaries. They use perplexity-based probing to verify that each model does not exhibit knowledge of events after its cutoff year and compare their performance on standard benchmarks against similarly sized models.
AI.result: Perplexity probing indicates that each DatedGPT model’s knowledge is effectively limited to information up to its designated cutoff year, suggesting that lookahead bias is mitigated. On common NLP benchmarks, the models achieve performance that is competitive with other 1.3B-parameter models, despite the temporal constraints on their training data. The authors also implement a web interface that lets users interact with multiple cutoff-year models side-by-side to observe differences in knowledge over time.
AI.conclusion: DatedGPT demonstrates that it is feasible to build a family of time-bounded language models that both respect strict temporal data cutoffs and maintain competitive performance. This provides a practical foundation for unbiased financial backtesting and for studying how model capabilities evolve as more data becomes available over time. The interactive demo further supports research and applications that require careful control of temporal information exposure.

title: The Density of Cross-Persistence Diagrams and Its Applications
summary: Topological Data Analysis (TDA) provides powerful tools to explore the shape and structure of data through topological features such as clusters, loops, and voids. Persistence diagrams are a cornerstone of TDA, capturing the evolution of these features across scales. While effective for analyzing individual manifolds, persistence diagrams do not account for interactions between pairs of them. Cross-persistence diagrams (cross-barcodes), introduced recently, address this limitation by characterizing relationships between topological features of two point clouds. In this work, we present the first systematic study of the density of cross-persistence diagrams. We prove its existence, establish theoretical foundations for its statistical use, and design the first machine learning framework for predicting cross-persistence density directly from point cloud coordinates and distance matrices. Our statistical approach enables the distinction of point clouds sampled from different manifolds by leveraging the linear characteristics of cross-persistence diagrams. Interestingly, we find that introducing noise can enhance our ability to distinguish point clouds, uncovering its novel utility in TDA applications. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our methods through experiments on diverse datasets, where our approach consistently outperforms existing techniques in density prediction and achieves superior results in point cloud distinction tasks. Our findings contribute to a broader understanding of cross-persistence diagrams and open new avenues for their application in data analysis, including potential insights into time-series domain tasks and the geometry of AI-generated texts. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/Verdangeta/TDA_experiments

AI.tldr: The paper develops a statistical and machine-learning framework for estimating and using the density of cross-persistence diagrams to compare point clouds, showing that noise can sometimes improve distinguishability and that their method outperforms existing techniques.
AI.motivation: Standard persistence diagrams from Topological Data Analysis describe features within a single manifold or point cloud but ignore interactions between two manifolds. Recently proposed cross-persistence diagrams capture relationships between two point clouds, yet their probabilistic/statistical properties and practical ML usage have been unexplored. The authors aim to fill this gap so that cross-persistence diagrams can be used rigorously for statistical inference and learning tasks such as distinguishing manifolds or complex datasets (e.g., time series, AI-generated texts).
AI.method: 1) Provide a theoretical analysis showing the existence of a well-defined density for cross-persistence diagrams and derive foundations that justify their statistical use. 2) Formulate a statistical framework that leverages the linear structure of cross-persistence representations to distinguish point clouds sampled from different manifolds. 3) Design a machine learning architecture that predicts the density of cross-persistence diagrams directly from raw point cloud coordinates and distance matrices. 4) Empirically evaluate the framework on multiple datasets, including noisy settings, comparing against existing TDA-based and other methods for density prediction and point cloud discrimination.
AI.result: They prove that cross-persistence diagrams admit a density under appropriate conditions and clarify how this density can be used statistically. Their ML model accurately predicts cross-persistence densities from data and consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. In point cloud discrimination tasks (identifying which manifold a sample comes from), their cross-persistence-based approach yields higher accuracy and robustness, including in noisy regimes where added noise surprisingly improves class separability.
AI.conclusion: Cross-persistence diagrams not only encode pairwise topological relationships between point clouds but also admit a usable density that supports statistical inference. The proposed ML framework makes it practical to estimate this density from raw data and exploit it for tasks like manifold discrimination. The empirical results suggest that controlled noise can enhance the discriminative power of cross-persistence diagrams, revealing a counterintuitive but useful role of noise in TDA. Overall, the work broadens the theoretical and practical toolkit for cross-persistence and points to new applications in time-series analysis and understanding the geometry of AI-generated text.

title: Bielik-Minitron-7B: Compressing Large Language Models via Structured Pruning and Knowledge Distillation for the Polish Language
summary: This report details the creation of Bielik-Minitron-7B, a compressed 7.35B parameter version of the Bielik-11B-v3.0 model, specifically optimized for European languages. By leveraging a two-stage compression methodology inspired by the NVIDIA Minitron approach, we combined structured hybrid pruning and knowledge distillation to reduce the model's parameter count by 33.4%, from 11.04B to 7.35B. We utilized the NVIDIA Model Optimizer for structural pruning and the NVIDIA NeMo Framework for logit-based distillation for quality recovery. Following distillation, the model underwent a rigorous alignment pipeline consisting of Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), Direct Preference Optimization (DPO-P), and Reinforcement Learning (GRPO). Our final model successfully recovered approximately 90% of the baseline model's performance while providing up to 50% inference speedup. This approach demonstrates an efficient pathway to create language models for less-represented languages, preserving the original model quality while reducing inference deployment costs.

AI.tldr: They compress an 11B European-language model to 7.35B parameters using pruning plus distillation, then re-align it, achieving ~90% of the original quality with up to 50% faster inference.
AI.motivation: Large language models are expensive to deploy, especially for underrepresented European languages, so there is a need to reduce model size and cost while keeping most of the performance.
AI.method: Apply a two-stage compression inspired by NVIDIA Minitron: (1) structured hybrid pruning with NVIDIA Model Optimizer to reduce parameters; (2) logit-based knowledge distillation with NVIDIA NeMo to recover quality, followed by an alignment pipeline of SFT, DPO-P, and RL (GRPO) for instruction following and preferences.
AI.result: They create Bielik-Minitron-7B, a 7.35B-parameter model (33.4% fewer parameters than the 11.04B baseline) that recovers about 90% of the original model’s performance and delivers up to 50% inference speedup.
AI.conclusion: Structured pruning plus distillation, combined with post-compression alignment, is an effective way to build smaller, cheaper language models for less-represented European languages while retaining most of the original quality.

title: VisDoT : Enhancing Visual Reasoning through Human-Like Interpretation Grounding and Decomposition of Thought
summary: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) struggle to reliably detect visual primitives in charts and align them with semantic representations, which severely limits their performance on complex visual reasoning. This lack of perceptual grounding constitutes a major bottleneck for chart-based reasoning. We propose VisDoT, a framework that enhances visual reasoning through human-like interpretation grounding. We formalize four perceptual tasks based on the theory of graphical perception, including position and length. Building on this foundation, we introduce Decomposition-of-Thought (DoT) prompting, which sequentially separates questions into visual perception sub-questions and logic sub-questions. Fine-tuning InternVL with VisDoT achieves a +11.2% improvement on ChartQA and surpasses GPT-4o on the more challenging ChartQAPro benchmark. On the newly introduced VisDoTQA benchmark, the model improves by +33.2%. Furthermore, consistent zero-shot gains on diverse open-domain VQA benchmarks confirm the generalizability of the perception-logic separation strategy for visual question answering. VisDoT leverages human-like perception to enhance visual grounding, achieving state-of-the-art chart understanding and interpretable visual reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces VisDoT, a framework that improves chart and visual question answering by grounding vision-language models in human-like graphical perception and separating perception from reasoning.
AI.motivation: Large vision-language models perform poorly on charts because they cannot robustly detect low-level visual primitives (like positions and lengths) or align them with semantics, which limits complex visual reasoning. There is a need for better perceptual grounding, inspired by how humans interpret graphical information, to overcome this bottleneck and improve chart-based and general visual QA.
AI.method: The authors design VisDoT, which formalizes four perceptual subtasks based on graphical perception theory (e.g., reading position and length in charts). They then propose Decomposition-of-Thought (DoT) prompting that decomposes each question into a sequence of visual perception sub-questions followed by logical reasoning sub-questions. They fine-tune the InternVL model with this framework, train and evaluate on existing chart datasets, and introduce a new VisDoTQA benchmark to specifically test perception-logic separation. The approach is also tested in zero-shot settings on open-domain VQA benchmarks to assess generalization.
AI.result: Fine-tuning InternVL with VisDoT yields an 11.2% accuracy gain on ChartQA and surpasses GPT-4o on the harder ChartQAPro benchmark. On the newly introduced VisDoTQA benchmark, VisDoT improves performance by 33.2%. The perception-logic separation strategy also produces consistent zero-shot improvements across several open-domain VQA datasets, indicating that the method generalizes beyond charts.
AI.conclusion: By formalizing human-like perceptual tasks and explicitly separating visual perception from logical reasoning via Decomposition-of-Thought prompting, VisDoT significantly improves visual grounding and reasoning in LVLMs. It achieves state-of-the-art results on chart understanding benchmarks, outperforms strong baselines like GPT-4o, generalizes to broader VQA tasks, and provides more interpretable visual reasoning through its structured perception-then-logic pipeline.

title: CoMMET: To What Extent Can LLMs Perform Theory of Mind Tasks?
summary: Theory of Mind (ToM)-the ability to reason about the mental states of oneself and others-is a cornerstone of human social intelligence. As Large Language Models (LLMs) become ubiquitous in real-world applications, validating their capacity for this level of social reasoning is essential for effective and natural interactions. However, existing benchmarks for assessing ToM in LLMs are limited; most rely solely on text inputs and focus narrowly on belief-related tasks. In this paper, we propose a new multimodal benchmark dataset, CoMMET, a Comprehensive Mental states and Moral Evaluation Task inspired by the Theory of Mind Booklet Task. CoMMET expands the scope of evaluation by covering a broader range of mental states and introducing multi-turn testing. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first multimodal dataset to evaluate ToM in a multi-turn conversational setting. Through a comprehensive assessment of LLMs across different families and sizes, we analyze the strengths and limitations of current models and identify directions for future improvement. Our work offers a deeper understanding of the social cognitive capabilities of modern LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CoMMET, a new multimodal, multi-turn benchmark to evaluate Theory of Mind and moral reasoning in Large Language Models, and uses it to systematically analyze their social cognitive strengths and weaknesses.
AI.motivation: Existing Theory of Mind benchmarks for LLMs are limited: they are mostly text-only, focus narrowly on belief reasoning, and typically use single-turn formats. As LLMs are increasingly deployed in socially interactive settings, there is a need for more realistic, comprehensive, and multimodal evaluations of their capacity to understand diverse mental states and moral dimensions in interactive conversations.
AI.method: The authors design CoMMET, a multimodal benchmark inspired by the Theory of Mind Booklet Task. The dataset covers a broad set of mental states (beyond just beliefs) and incorporates moral evaluation components, all presented in a multi-turn conversational format and with multimodal inputs. They then evaluate a range of LLM families and model sizes on this benchmark, systematically comparing performance patterns to characterize ToM-related capabilities and gaps.
AI.result: Using CoMMET, the authors obtain empirical performance profiles of various LLMs, showing which models and scales handle different mental-state and moral reasoning tasks better, and where they fail. The results reveal clear strengths in some forms of social reasoning but also notable limitations, especially in more complex or nuanced ToM and moral scenarios, particularly in multi-turn and multimodal settings.
AI.conclusion: CoMMET provides the first multimodal, multi-turn benchmark targeted at evaluating Theory of Mind and moral reasoning in LLMs, revealing that current models possess partial but incomplete social cognitive abilities. The benchmark offers a more realistic and fine-grained way to assess and compare models, and highlights concrete directions for improving future LLMs’ social intelligence.

title: LLMs can construct powerful representations and streamline sample-efficient supervised learning
summary: As real-world datasets become increasingly complex and heterogeneous, supervised learning is often bottlenecked by input representation design. Modeling multimodal data for downstream tasks, such as time-series, free text, and structured records, often requires non-trivial domain-specific engineering. We propose an agentic pipeline to streamline this process. First, an LLM analyzes a small but diverse subset of text-serialized input examples in-context to synthesize a global rubric, which acts as a programmatic specification for extracting and organizing evidence. This rubric is then used to transform naive text-serializations of inputs into a more standardized format for downstream models. We also describe local rubrics, which are task-conditioned summaries generated by an LLM. Across 15 clinical tasks from the EHRSHOT benchmark, our rubric-based approaches significantly outperform traditional count-feature models, naive text-serialization-based LLM baselines, and a clinical foundation model, which is pretrained on orders of magnitude more data. Beyond performance, rubrics offer several advantages for operational healthcare settings such as being easy to audit, cost-effectiveness to deploy at scale, and they can be converted to tabular representations that unlock a swath of machine learning techniques.

AI.tldr: They introduce an LLM-driven rubric-generation pipeline that converts heterogeneous, multimodal clinical data into standardized representations, improving downstream supervised learning.
AI.motivation: Supervised learning on complex, multimodal real-world datasets (like EHRs) is limited by the difficulty and cost of designing good input representations, which usually requires heavy domain-specific feature engineering.
AI.method: Use an LLM as an agent in two stages: (1) In-context, it reviews a small, diverse set of text-serialized input examples and synthesizes a global rubric—a programmatic specification of what evidence to extract and how to structure it. (2) This rubric is then applied to transform naive text serializations into standardized formats for downstream models; additionally, local (task-specific) rubrics are generated by the LLM as task-conditioned summaries.
AI.result: On 15 electronic health record prediction tasks from the EHRSHOT benchmark, the rubric-based representation methods substantially outperform traditional count-based feature models, naive text-serialization LLM baselines, and even a large clinical foundation model trained on much more data.
AI.conclusion: LLM-generated rubrics provide a practical, scalable alternative to manual feature engineering for complex clinical data: they improve predictive performance, are auditable and cost-efficient, and can be converted to tabular formats that enable the use of standard machine learning techniques.

title: PersonaTrace: Synthesizing Realistic Digital Footprints with LLM Agents
summary: Digital footprints (records of individuals' interactions with digital systems) are essential for studying behavior, developing personalized applications, and training machine learning models. However, research in this area is often hindered by the scarcity of diverse and accessible data. To address this limitation, we propose a novel method for synthesizing realistic digital footprints using large language model (LLM) agents. Starting from a structured user profile, our approach generates diverse and plausible sequences of user events, ultimately producing corresponding digital artifacts such as emails, messages, calendar entries, reminders, etc. Intrinsic evaluation results demonstrate that the generated dataset is more diverse and realistic than existing baselines. Moreover, models fine-tuned on our synthetic data outperform those trained on other synthetic datasets when evaluated on real-world out-of-distribution tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a method to generate realistic synthetic digital footprints (emails, messages, calendar events, etc.) from structured user profiles using LLM agents, yielding data that is more diverse and leads to better model performance than existing synthetic baselines.
AI.motivation: Real-world digital footprint data (e.g., logs of user interactions with digital systems) is scarce, hard to share, and often lacks diversity, which limits behavioral research, personalization, and training of robust machine learning models. The authors aim to overcome data scarcity and privacy constraints by creating high-quality synthetic alternatives that still capture realistic user behavior.
AI.method: The authors design LLM-based agents that, given a structured user profile, simulate plausible sequences of user events. These events are then rendered into concrete digital artifacts such as emails, chat messages, calendar entries, and reminders. They perform intrinsic evaluations comparing the realism and diversity of their generated data to existing synthetic baselines, and extrinsic evaluations by fine-tuning models on this synthetic data and testing them on real-world, out-of-distribution tasks.
AI.result: Intrinsic evaluations show the new synthetic dataset is both more diverse and more realistic than competing synthetic datasets. In downstream experiments, models fine-tuned on this synthetic data achieve better performance on real, out-of-distribution evaluation tasks than models trained on other synthetic datasets.
AI.conclusion: LLM agents can be used to synthesize realistic and diverse digital footprints from structured profiles, providing a valuable alternative to scarce real-world logs. The resulting data not only passes intrinsic checks of realism and diversity but also improves downstream model performance, indicating that such synthetic footprints can effectively stand in for real data in many applications.

title: Explicit Logic Channel for Validation and Enhancement of MLLMs on Zero-Shot Tasks
summary: Frontier Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) exhibit remarkable capabilities in Visual-Language Comprehension (VLC) tasks. However, they are often deployed as zero-shot solution to new tasks in a black-box manner. Validating and understanding the behavior of these models become important for application to new task.
