We propose an Explicit Logic Channel, in parallel with the black-box model channel, to perform explicit logical reasoning for model validation, selection and enhancement. The frontier MLLM, encapsulating latent vision-language knowledge, can be considered as an Implicit Logic Channel. The proposed Explicit Logic Channel, mimicking human logical reasoning, incorporates a LLM, a VFM, and logical reasoning with probabilistic inference for factual, counterfactual, and relational reasoning over the explicit visual evidence. A Consistency Rate (CR) is proposed for cross-channel validation and model selection, even without ground-truth annotations. Additionally, cross-channel integration further improves performance in zero-shot tasks over MLLMs, grounded with explicit visual evidence to enhance trustworthiness. Comprehensive experiments conducted for two representative VLC tasks, i.e., MC-VQA and HC-REC, on three challenging benchmarks, with 11 recent open-source MLLMs from 4 frontier families. Our systematic evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness of proposed ELC and CR for model validation, selection and improvement on MLLMs with enhanced explainability and trustworthiness.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an Explicit Logic Channel (ELC) that runs alongside existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) to explicitly reason over visual evidence, enabling model validation, selection, and performance gains without needing ground-truth labels.
AI.motivation: Frontier multimodal LLMs perform strongly on visual-language tasks but are typically used as black-box zero-shot solvers. This makes it hard to validate, understand, and trust their behavior on new tasks, especially when ground-truth annotations are not available. The authors want a principled way to inspect and compare models, improve reliability, and enhance explainability and trustworthiness.
AI.method: They treat the MLLM itself as an Implicit Logic Channel that encodes latent vision-language reasoning. In parallel, they build an Explicit Logic Channel that mimics human reasoning: it combines a (text) LLM, a visual foundation model (VFM), and a logical reasoning module with probabilistic inference. This channel operates directly on explicit visual evidence to perform factual, counterfactual, and relational reasoning. They then define a Consistency Rate (CR), measuring agreement between the implicit (MLLM) and explicit channels. CR is used for cross-channel validation, model comparison, and selection, even without labels. They also propose cross-channel integration, where outputs from both channels are fused to improve zero-shot task performance.
AI.result: On two representative visual-language comprehension tasks—multiple-choice VQA (MC-VQA) and holistic concept recognition (HC-REC)—and three challenging benchmarks, they evaluate 11 open-source MLLMs from four major families. The experiments show that: (1) ELC can systematically validate and compare MLLMs via the Consistency Rate; (2) CR correlates with model quality, enabling model selection without ground-truth; and (3) integrating the explicit and implicit channels improves zero-shot performance over using the MLLMs alone, while providing more interpretable reasoning traces.
AI.conclusion: The Explicit Logic Channel and the Consistency Rate provide an effective framework to validate, select, and enhance multimodal LLMs in a principled way, even in label-scarce, zero-shot settings. By grounding reasoning in explicit visual evidence and making the logical process transparent, the approach improves explainability and trustworthiness, and achieves better performance on challenging visual-language comprehension tasks.

title: CHiL(L)Grader: Calibrated Human-in-the-Loop Short-Answer Grading
summary: Scaling educational assessment with large language models requires not just accuracy, but the ability to recognize when predictions are trustworthy. Instruction-tuned models tend to be overconfident, and their reliability deteriorates as curricula evolve, making fully autonomous deployment unsafe in high-stakes settings. We introduce CHiL(L)Grader, the first automated grading framework that incorporates calibrated confidence estimation into a human-in-the-loop workflow. Using post-hoc temperature scaling, confidence-based selective prediction, and continual learning, CHiL(L)Grader automates only high-confidence predictions while routing uncertain cases to human graders, and adapts to evolving rubrics and unseen questions. Across three short-answer grading datasets, CHiL(L)Grader automatically scores 35-65% of responses at expert-level quality (QWK >= 0.80). A QWK gap of 0.347 between accepted and rejected predictions confirms the effectiveness of the confidence-based routing. Each correction cycle strengthens the model's grading capability as it learns from teacher feedback. These results show that uncertainty quantification is key for reliable AI-assisted grading.

AI.tldr: The paper presents CHiL(L)Grader, an automated short-answer grading framework that integrates calibrated confidence estimation into a human-in-the-loop workflow, enabling safe partial automation in evolving educational settings.
AI.motivation: In educational assessment, large language models can grade short answers accurately, but they are often overconfident and unreliable when curricula, rubrics, or question types change. This makes fully autonomous grading risky in high-stakes environments like formal education, where errors can impact students significantly. There is a need for systems that not only predict grades but also know when they can be trusted and when human intervention is necessary, while continuously improving from teacher feedback.
AI.method: The authors propose CHiL(L)Grader, a grading framework that combines large language models with uncertainty quantification and continual learning. They apply post-hoc temperature scaling to calibrate model confidence, then use confidence-based selective prediction to automatically accept only high-confidence grading decisions and route low-confidence or uncertain cases to human graders. The system incorporates continual learning so that model parameters are updated over time using human corrections, allowing adaptation to evolving rubrics and unseen question types. The approach is evaluated on three short-answer grading datasets using Quadratic Weighted Kappa (QWK) as the primary metric.
AI.result: On three short-answer grading datasets, CHiL(L)Grader succeeds in automatically grading between 35% and 65% of student responses at expert-level agreement with human graders, defined as QWK ≥ 0.80. The framework achieves a large quality separation between accepted (high-confidence) and rejected (low-confidence) predictions, with a QWK gap of 0.347, demonstrating that the confidence estimates are effective for routing decisions. As the system undergoes multiple correction cycles where it learns from teacher feedback, its grading performance improves over time.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that incorporating calibrated uncertainty estimates into LLM-based grading is crucial for safe and reliable deployment in educational assessment. By combining confidence calibration, selective prediction, and continual learning within a human-in-the-loop setup, CHiL(L)Grader can partially automate grading at expert-level quality while deferring challenging cases to humans and adapting to changing rubrics and novel questions. This demonstrates a practical path toward trustworthy AI-assisted grading rather than fully autonomous, static models.

title: Just Use XML: Revisiting Joint Translation and Label Projection
summary: Label projection is an effective technique for cross-lingual transfer, extending span-annotated datasets from a high-resource language to low-resource ones. Most approaches perform label projection as a separate step after machine translation, and prior work that combines the two reports degraded translation quality. We re-evaluate this claim with LabelPigeon, a novel framework that jointly performs translation and label projection via XML tags. We design a direct evaluation scheme for label projection, and find that LabelPigeon outperforms baselines and actively improves translation quality in 11 languages. We further assess translation quality across 203 languages and varying annotation complexity, finding consistent improvement attributed to additional fine-tuning. Finally, across 27 languages and three downstream tasks, we report substantial gains in cross-lingual transfer over comparable work, up to +39.9 F1 on NER. Overall, our results demonstrate that XML-tagged label projection provides effective and efficient label transfer without compromising translation quality.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LabelPigeon, a framework that jointly performs machine translation and label projection using XML tags, improving both translation quality and cross-lingual transfer performance over strong baselines.
AI.motivation: Cross-lingual transfer relies on projecting labels from high-resource to low-resource languages, often via machine translation. Existing methods typically separate translation and label projection, and prior attempts to combine them harmed translation quality. There is a need for a unified approach that can simultaneously translate and project labels without degrading, and ideally improving, translation quality while scaling to many languages and tasks.
AI.method: The authors propose LabelPigeon, which embeds span labels into the source text using XML tags and trains/fine-tunes machine translation models to jointly produce translated text and projected XML-tagged spans. They design a direct evaluation scheme for label projection quality, and systematically compare LabelPigeon to separate-step baselines across many languages, varying annotation complexities, and multiple downstream tasks. The approach relies on additional fine-tuning of MT models using XML-tagged data to make them robust to structured inputs and outputs.
AI.result: LabelPigeon outperforms traditional label projection baselines on direct projection evaluation, and, contrary to prior claims, it improves translation quality in 11 languages. When evaluated on 203 languages with different levels of annotation complexity, the method yields consistent gains in translation attributed to the extra fine-tuning. On 27 languages and three downstream tasks such as NER, the approach achieves substantial gains in cross-lingual transfer performance, with improvements up to +39.9 F1 on named entity recognition compared to comparable methods.
AI.conclusion: Joint translation and label projection via XML tagging is an effective and efficient strategy for cross-lingual label transfer. It not only avoids the previously observed degradation in translation quality but actually improves it across many languages, while delivering large gains on downstream tasks. XML-tagged label projection thus provides a practical way to scale span-annotated resources from high-resource to low-resource languages without sacrificing translation performance.

title: When OpenClaw Meets Hospital: Toward an Agentic Operating System for Dynamic Clinical Workflows
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents extend conventional generative models by integrating reasoning, tool invocation, and persistent memory. Recent studies suggest that such agents may significantly improve clinical workflows by automating documentation, coordinating care processes, and assisting medical decision making. However, despite rapid progress, deploying autonomous agents in healthcare environments remains difficult due to reliability limitations, security risks, and insufficient long-term memory mechanisms. This work proposes an architecture that adapts LLM agents for hospital environments. The design introduces four core components: a restricted execution environment inspired by Linux multi-user systems, a document-centric interaction paradigm connecting patient and clinician agents, a page-indexed memory architecture designed for long-term clinical context management, and a curated medical skills library enabling ad-hoc composition of clinical task sequences. Rather than granting agents unrestricted system access, the architecture constrains actions through predefined skill interfaces and resource isolation. We argue that such a system forms the basis of an Agentic Operating System for Hospital, a computing layer capable of coordinating clinical workflows while maintaining safety, transparency, and auditability. This work grounds the design in OpenClaw, an open-source autonomous agent framework that structures agent capabilities as a curated library of discrete skills, and extends it with the infrastructure-level constraints required for safe clinical deployment.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an architecture for safely deploying LLM-based autonomous agents in hospitals by constraining their actions, structuring interactions, and providing robust long-term memory and skills libraries.
AI.motivation: LLM agents could greatly improve clinical workflows (documentation, care coordination, decision support) but current systems are hard to deploy safely in real hospitals due to reliability, security, and long-term memory limitations. There is a need for an architecture that adapts agent capabilities to hospital constraints while maintaining safety, transparency, and auditability.
AI.method: Design and proposal of an Agentic Operating System for Hospitals. The architecture has four main components: (1) a restricted execution environment modeled after Linux multi-user systems to isolate and control agent actions; (2) a document-centric interaction model that mediates communication between patient and clinician agents through clinical documents; (3) a page-indexed memory structure to manage long-term clinical context; and (4) a curated medical skills library that exposes clinical tools as discrete, composable skills, grounded in and extending the existing OpenClaw framework.
AI.result: The work defines a structured, constrained architecture for hospital-deployed LLM agents that uses skills and resource isolation instead of unrestricted system access, and shows how OpenClaw can be extended to support this environment. Concrete empirical results are not described in the abstract; the main result is the architectural design and its safety properties in principle.
AI.conclusion: By constraining LLM agents via predefined skill interfaces, sandboxed execution, document-centric workflows, and page-indexed memory, the proposed Agentic Operating System for Hospital can coordinate clinical workflows while improving safety, transparency, and auditability. The extended OpenClaw framework serves as a practical foundation for implementing such an agentic OS in healthcare settings.

title: Translationese as a Rational Response to Translation Task Difficulty
summary: Translations systematically diverge from texts originally produced in the target language, a phenomenon widely referred to as translationese. Translationese has been attributed to production tendencies (e.g. interference, simplification), socio-cultural variables, and language-pair effects, yet a unified explanatory account is still lacking. We propose that translationese reflects cognitive load inherent in the translation task itself. We test whether observable translationese can be predicted from quantifiable measures of translation task difficulty. Translationese is operationalised as a segment-level translatedness score produced by an automatic classifier. Translation task difficulty is conceptualised as comprising source-text and cross-lingual transfer components, operationalised mainly through information-theoretic metrics based on LLM surprisal, complemented by established syntactic and semantic alternatives. We use a bidirectional English-German corpus comprising written and spoken subcorpora. Results indicate that translationese can be partly explained by translation task difficulty, especially in English-to-German. For most experiments, cross-lingual transfer difficulty contributes more than source-text complexity. Information-theoretic indicators match or outperform traditional features in written mode, but offer no advantage in spoken mode. Source-text syntactic complexity and translation-solution entropy emerged as the strongest predictors of translationese across language pairs and modes.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that translationese partly arises from cognitive load in translation and shows that measures of translation task difficulty, especially cross-lingual transfer difficulty, predict how translationese a segment is.
AI.motivation: Although translationese is a well-known phenomenon, it lacks a unified explanation. Existing accounts mention interference, simplification, socio-cultural factors, and language-pair specifics, but they do not tie these together in a predictive framework. The authors aim to test whether translationese can be systematically explained and predicted by quantifiable measures of cognitive difficulty inherent in the translation task itself.
AI.method: They operationalise translationese as a segment-level translatedness score output by an automatic classifier. Translation task difficulty is divided into (1) source-text complexity and (2) cross-lingual transfer difficulty. These are mainly measured using information-theoretic metrics derived from LLM surprisal, along with established syntactic and semantic complexity features. Using a bidirectional English–German corpus with written and spoken data, they run regression-style experiments to see which difficulty measures predict translationese scores and how well information-theoretic features compare with traditional ones.
AI.result: Translationese is partially predictable from translation task difficulty, with a stronger effect in English→German than German→English. In most settings, cross-lingual transfer difficulty is a better predictor than source-text complexity. Information-theoretic indicators based on LLM surprisal perform as well as or better than traditional features in written texts but do not provide an advantage for spoken texts. Across languages and modes, source-text syntactic complexity and translation-solution entropy are consistently among the strongest predictors of translationese.
AI.conclusion: The findings support the view that translationese is not only a matter of stylistic convention or interference but is partly a direct consequence of cognitive load during translation. Difficult segments, especially in terms of cross-lingual transfer and syntactic complexity, are more likely to exhibit translationese features. Information-theoretic measures are promising tools for modeling this difficulty in written translation, though their benefits are less clear for spoken mode. Overall, the study moves toward a unified, cognitively grounded explanation of translationese.

title: Gender Bias in Generative AI-assisted Recruitment Processes
summary: In recent years, generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) systems have assumed increasingly crucial roles in selection processes, personnel recruitment and analysis of candidates' profiles. However, the employment of large language models (LLMs) risks reproducing, and in some cases amplifying, gender stereotypes and bias already present in the labour market. The objective of this paper is to evaluate and measure this phenomenon, analysing how a state-of-the-art generative model (GPT-5) suggests occupations based on gender and work experience background, focusing on under-35-year-old Italian graduates. The model has been prompted to suggest jobs to 24 simulated candidate profiles, which are balanced in terms of gender, age, experience and professional field. Although no significant differences emerged in job titles and industry, gendered linguistic patterns emerged in the adjectives attributed to female and male candidates, indicating a tendency of the model to associate women with emotional and empathetic traits, while men with strategic and analytical ones. The research raises an ethical question regarding the use of these models in sensitive processes, highlighting the need for transparency and fairness in future digital labour markets.

AI.tldr: The paper examines whether a state-of-the-art generative AI model (GPT‑5) exhibits gender bias when recommending jobs to young Italian graduates, finding gendered language patterns in how male and female candidates are described.
AI.motivation: Generative AI and large language models are increasingly used in recruitment and candidate evaluation. Because labour markets already contain gender stereotypes and inequalities, there is concern that these systems may replicate or intensify such biases when suggesting jobs or describing candidates, with real consequences for fairness and equal opportunity. The authors aim to empirically test whether an advanced LLM shows gender-related bias in career recommendations for young graduates in Italy.
AI.method: The authors construct 24 simulated candidate profiles representing under‑35 Italian graduates, balanced across gender, age, experience, and professional field. They prompt a state-of-the-art generative model (GPT‑5) to propose occupations for each profile. They then comparatively analyze the outputs, focusing on both the job titles/industries suggested and the descriptive language (notably adjectives) used for male vs. female candidates, to detect systematic gendered patterns.
AI.result: The model’s suggested job titles and industries do not show major gender differences across the matched male and female profiles. However, a clear difference emerges in the adjectives and descriptive language: the model tends to associate female candidates with emotional, relational, and empathetic traits, and male candidates with strategic, analytical, and more agentic traits. This indicates a subtle but systematic gender stereotyping at the level of linguistic framing rather than explicit job allocation.
AI.conclusion: Even when overt job recommendations appear gender‑neutral, advanced LLMs can still encode and express gender stereotypes through the way they describe candidates. This raises ethical concerns about deploying such systems in recruitment and selection, where linguistic framing can influence perceptions and decisions. The authors argue for stronger transparency, bias detection, and fairness safeguards when using generative AI in digital labour markets, especially for sensitive processes affecting individuals’ careers.

title: To Words and Beyond: Probing Large Language Models for Sentence-Level Psycholinguistic Norms of Memorability and Reading Times
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently been shown to produce estimates of psycholinguistic norms, such as valence, arousal, or concreteness, for words and multiword expressions, that correlate with human judgments. These estimates are obtained by prompting an LLM, in zero-shot fashion, with a question similar to those used in human studies. Meanwhile, for other norms such as lexical decision time or age of acquisition, LLMs require supervised fine-tuning to obtain results that align with ground-truth values. In this paper, we extend this approach to the previously unstudied features of sentence memorability and reading times, which involve the relationship between multiple words in a sentence-level context. Our results show that via fine-tuning, models can provide estimates that correlate with human-derived norms and exceed the predictive power of interpretable baseline predictors, demonstrating that LLMs contain useful information about sentence-level features. At the same time, our results show very mixed zero-shot and few-shot performance, providing further evidence that care is needed when using LLM-prompting as a proxy for human cognitive measures.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether large language models can predict human-like sentence memorability and reading times, and finds they can when fine-tuned but not reliably in zero/few-shot prompting.
AI.motivation: Prior work showed that LLMs can approximate some word-level psycholinguistic norms (e.g., valence, arousal, concreteness) with simple prompts, but other norms (e.g., lexical decision time, age of acquisition) need supervised fine-tuning. However, it was unknown whether LLMs also implicitly encode more complex sentence-level cognitive properties, such as how memorable a sentence is or how long it takes to read. The authors aim to test whether LLMs contain usable information about these sentence-level features and to what extent zero-shot prompting is a valid proxy for human cognitive measures.
AI.method: The authors treat sentence memorability and reading times as psycholinguistic norms and evaluate LLMs on their ability to estimate them. They compare two general approaches: (1) zero-shot and few-shot prompting, where the LLM is directly asked for estimates in a way that mimics human rating tasks; and (2) supervised fine-tuning, where model parameters are trained on datasets with ground-truth human-derived norms. They then correlate the models’ predictions with human norms and benchmark them against interpretable baseline predictors to assess added value from the LLMs’ internal representations.
AI.result: Fine-tuned LLMs produce sentence memorability and reading time estimates that correlate well with human-derived norms and outperform interpretable baseline predictors, indicating that they encode useful sentence-level information. In contrast, zero-shot and few-shot prompting yields highly mixed performance, failing to consistently match human norms across conditions.
AI.conclusion: LLMs do contain informative representations of sentence-level cognitive properties like memorability and reading times, but this information is not reliably accessible through naïve prompting alone. Instead, supervised fine-tuning is necessary to harness these representations effectively. Consequently, researchers and practitioners should be cautious about using raw LLM prompts as stand-ins for human cognitive measures, especially at the sentence level.

title: CINDI: Conditional Imputation and Noisy Data Integrity with Flows in Power Grid Data
summary: Real-world multivariate time series, particularly in critical infrastructure such as electrical power grids, are often corrupted by noise and anomalies that degrade the performance of downstream tasks. Standard data cleaning approaches often rely on disjoint strategies, which involve detecting errors with one model and imputing them with another. Such approaches can fail to capture the full joint distribution of the data and ignore prediction uncertainty. This work introduces Conditional Imputation and Noisy Data Integrity (CINDI), an unsupervised probabilistic framework designed to restore data integrity in complex time series. Unlike fragmented approaches, CINDI unifies anomaly detection and imputation into a single end-to-end system built on conditional normalizing flows. By modeling the exact conditional likelihood of the data, the framework identifies low-probability segments and iteratively samples statistically consistent replacements. This allows CINDI to efficiently reuse learned information while preserving the underlying physical and statistical properties of the system. We evaluate the framework using real-world grid loss data from a Norwegian power distribution operator, though the methodology is designed to generalize to any multivariate time series domain. The results demonstrate that CINDI yields robust performance compared to competitive baselines, offering a scalable solution for maintaining reliability in noisy environments.

AI.tldr: CINDI is an unsupervised probabilistic framework that jointly detects anomalies and imputes corrupted values in multivariate time series using conditional normalizing flows, improving data integrity and downstream reliability in noisy real-world settings like power grids.
AI.motivation: Real-world multivariate time series, especially in critical infrastructures such as power grids, are often noisy and contain anomalies. Existing data cleaning solutions typically separate anomaly detection and imputation into different models, which can fail to capture the joint data distribution and ignore predictive uncertainty. There is a need for a unified framework that can both detect and repair corrupted data while respecting the underlying physical and statistical structure of the system.
AI.method: The paper proposes CINDI, a unified unsupervised probabilistic framework based on conditional normalizing flows. CINDI models the exact conditional likelihood of multivariate time series segments. Anomalous or corrupted segments are identified as low-probability regions under this learned distribution. The method then iteratively samples statistically consistent replacements for these segments, effectively imputing missing or corrupted values while leveraging the learned joint structure of the data. This end-to-end design combines anomaly detection and imputation within a single conditional generative model.
AI.result: Applied to real-world grid loss data from a Norwegian power distribution operator, CINDI achieves robust performance relative to strong baseline methods for anomaly detection and data imputation. The experiments indicate that the framework can scale to complex, noisy multivariate time series and maintain reliable behavior, outperforming or matching baselines across relevant evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: CINDI provides an effective, scalable, and domain-agnostic approach for restoring data integrity in noisy multivariate time series. By unifying anomaly detection and imputation within a conditional normalizing flow framework that respects the joint data distribution, it better preserves physical and statistical properties of the system and enhances reliability for downstream tasks in critical infrastructure and other domains.

title: SommBench: Assessing Sommelier Expertise of Language Models
summary: With the rapid advances of large language models, it becomes increasingly important to systematically evaluate their multilingual and multicultural capabilities. Previous cultural evaluation benchmarks focus mainly on basic cultural knowledge that can be encoded in linguistic form. Here, we propose SommBench, a multilingual benchmark to assess sommelier expertise, a domain deeply grounded in the senses of smell and taste. While language models learn about sensory properties exclusively through textual descriptions, SommBench tests whether this textual grounding is sufficient to emulate expert-level sensory judgment. SommBench comprises three main tasks: Wine Theory Question Answering (WTQA), Wine Feature Completion (WFC), and Food-Wine Pairing (FWP). SommBench is available in multiple languages: English, Slovak, Swedish, Finnish, German, Danish, Italian, and Spanish. This helps separate a language model's wine expertise from its language skills. The benchmark datasets were developed in close collaboration with a professional sommelier and native speakers of the respective languages, resulting in 1,024 wine theory question-answering questions, 1,000 wine feature-completion examples, and 1,000 food-wine pairing examples. We provide results for the most popular language models, including closed-weights models such as Gemini 2.5, and open-weights models, such as GPT-OSS and Qwen 3. Our results show that the most capable models perform well on wine theory question answering (up to 97% correct with a closed-weights model), yet feature completion (peaking at 65%) and food-wine pairing show (MCC ranging between 0 and 0.39) turn out to be more challenging. These results position SommBench as an interesting and challenging benchmark for evaluating the sommelier expertise of language models. The benchmark is publicly available at https://github.com/sommify/sommbench.

AI.tldr: Introduces SommBench, a multilingual benchmark to test LLMs’ sommelier (wine) expertise, going beyond basic cultural knowledge to sensory-grounded reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing cultural benchmarks mostly assess factual or linguistic cultural knowledge, ignoring domains where expertise is rooted in non-linguistic sensory experience (smell, taste). The authors want to know whether language-only training can approximate expert sensory judgment, and to have a more realistic, multicultural testbed for LLMs’ domain expertise in wine across several languages.
AI.method: They design SommBench, a benchmark developed with a professional sommelier and native speakers in eight languages (EN, SK, SV, FI, DE, DA, IT, ES). It contains three tasks: (1) Wine Theory Question Answering (WTQA) to test factual/theoretical knowledge about wine; (2) Wine Feature Completion (WFC) where models infer missing sensory or descriptive attributes of wines; and (3) Food-Wine Pairing (FWP) evaluating models’ ability to choose appropriate wine pairings. The dataset includes 1,024 WTQA items, 1,000 WFC examples, and 1,000 FWP examples. They evaluate multiple popular LLMs (both closed- and open-weights, e.g., Gemini 2.5, GPT-OSS, Qwen 3) on all tasks and languages, measuring accuracy for QA and standard metrics (e.g., MCC) for more complex tasks such as pairing.
AI.result: Closed-weight frontier models achieve high performance on the theoretical wine QA task (up to 97% accuracy), but performance is markedly lower on the more sensory-grounded tasks: feature completion peaks at about 65% accuracy, and food-wine pairing yields low-to-moderate Matthews Correlation Coefficients (0 to 0.39), indicating substantial difficulty. This pattern suggests that while LLMs are strong at text-encoded theoretical knowledge, they struggle more when the task implicitly relies on grounded sensory expertise and nuanced judgment.
AI.conclusion: SommBench is a challenging, multilingual benchmark that separates language proficiency from wine-specific expertise and exposes the limits of current LLMs in approximating sommelier-level sensory reasoning. High scores on theory but weaker performance on feature completion and pairing show that textual training alone does not yet fully substitute grounded sensory experience. The benchmark, released publicly, provides a standardized, cross-lingual tool for future work on improving and evaluating LLMs’ performance in sensory-rich, expert domains.

title: Anomaly detection in time-series via inductive biases in the latent space of conditional normalizing flows
summary: Deep generative models for anomaly detection in multivariate time-series are typically trained by maximizing data likelihood. However, likelihood in observation space measures marginal density rather than conformity to structured temporal dynamics, and therefore can assign high probability to anomalous or out-of-distribution samples. We address this structural limitation by relocating the notion of anomaly to a prescribed latent space. We introduce explicit inductive biases in conditional normalizing flows, modeling time-series observations within a discrete-time state-space framework that constrains latent representations to evolve according to prescribed temporal dynamics. Under this formulation, expected behavior corresponds to compliance with a specified distribution over latent trajectories, while anomalies are defined as violations of these dynamics. Anomaly detection is consequently reduced to a statistically grounded compliance test, such that observations are mapped to latent space and evaluated via goodness-of-fit tests against the prescribed latent evolution. This yields a principled decision rule that remains effective even in regions of high observation likelihood. Experiments on synthetic and real-world time-series demonstrate reliable detection of anomalies in frequency, amplitude, and observation noise, while providing interpretable diagnostics of model compliance.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a deep generative anomaly detection method for multivariate time-series that defines anomalies in a structured latent space instead of raw observation space, using conditional normalizing flows embedded in a state-space model and detecting anomalies via goodness-of-fit tests on latent dynamics.
AI.motivation: Existing deep generative models for time-series anomaly detection typically rely on maximizing data likelihood in the observation space. However, likelihood mainly captures marginal density and does not necessarily reflect whether a sequence conforms to realistic temporal dynamics. As a result, such models can assign high probability to anomalous or out-of-distribution sequences, especially in complex, high-dimensional series. There is a need for methods that explicitly encode temporal structure and define anomalies based on violations of expected dynamical behavior rather than just low likelihood in observation space.
AI.method: The authors introduce a framework that shifts the definition of anomalies to a latent space with prescribed temporal dynamics. They design conditional normalizing flows whose latent variables follow a discrete-time state-space model. The normalizing flow maps observations to latent states, while the state-space prior enforces specific temporal evolution patterns. Expected behavior is formulated as adherence to a chosen distribution over latent trajectories. Anomaly detection is carried out by mapping observed time-series to latent space and performing statistically principled goodness-of-fit or compliance tests on whether the latent trajectories are consistent with the prescribed dynamics, rather than thresholding observation likelihoods.
AI.result: In experiments on both synthetic and real-world multivariate time-series, the proposed approach reliably detects anomalies that manifest as changes in frequency, amplitude, or observation noise. Compared to likelihood-based baselines, it remains effective in regions where raw observation likelihood is high but the temporal evolution is abnormal. The method also yields interpretable diagnostic information, indicating which aspects of the latent dynamics are violated when an anomaly occurs.
AI.conclusion: By embedding normalizing flows within a state-space framework and relocating anomaly definition to a structured latent space, the paper provides a statistically grounded and robust approach to time-series anomaly detection. This approach overcomes limitations of pure likelihood-based methods in observation space, offers interpretability via latent compliance checks, and effectively detects diverse types of temporal anomalies even when they reside in high-likelihood regions of the observation space.

title: Cross-Context Review: Improving LLM Output Quality by Separating Production and Review Sessions
summary: Large language models struggle to catch errors in their own outputs when the review happens in the same session that produced them. This paper introduces Cross-Context Review (CCR), a straightforward method where the review is conducted in a fresh session with no access to the production conversation history. We ran a controlled experiment: 30 artifacts (code, technical documents, presentation scripts) with 150 injected errors, tested under four review conditions -- same-session Self-Review (SR), repeated Self-Review (SR2), context-aware Subagent Review (SA), and Cross-Context Review (CCR). Over 360 reviews, CCR reached an F1 of 28.6%, outperforming SR (24.6%, p=0.008, d=0.52), SR2 (21.7%, p<0.001, d=0.72), and SA (23.8%, p=0.004, d=0.57). The SR2 result matters most for interpretation: reviewing twice in the same session did not beat reviewing once (p=0.11), which rules out repetition as an explanation for CCR's advantage. The benefit comes from context separation itself. CCR works with any model, needs no infrastructure, and costs only one extra session.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Cross-Context Review (CCR), where a model reviews outputs in a fresh session without prior conversation context, significantly improving error detection over standard same-session self-review methods.
AI.motivation: Large language models often miss errors in their own outputs, especially when reviewing them within the same conversational context that produced them. There is a need for simple, model-agnostic methods to improve AI self-review and error detection, without additional training or infrastructure.
AI.method: The authors design a controlled experiment using 30 artifacts (code, technical documents, presentation scripts) into which they inject 150 known errors. They compare four review conditions: (1) Self-Review in the same session (SR), (2) repeated Self-Review in the same session (SR2), (3) context-aware Subagent Review that has access to production history (SA), and (4) Cross-Context Review (CCR), where the reviewer is in a fresh session without access to the original conversation. They conduct 360 review trials, measuring error detection performance with F1 scores and using statistical tests (p-values, effect sizes) to compare conditions.
AI.result: CCR achieves the best error-detection performance with an F1 score of 28.6%, significantly outperforming SR (24.6%, p=0.008, d=0.52), SR2 (21.7%, p<0.001, d=0.72), and SA (23.8%, p=0.004, d=0.57). Importantly, repeating self-review within the same session (SR2) does not improve performance over a single self-review (p=0.11), suggesting that simply reviewing more is not the cause of CCR's improvement.
AI.conclusion: The improvements in error detection are due to context separation rather than repetition or additional context. Cross-Context Review, by isolating the reviewer from the original production context, helps language models be more critical of outputs and catch more errors. CCR is simple, model-agnostic, requires no special infrastructure, and only adds the cost of one additional session, making it a practical method to enhance AI self-review in real-world workflows.

title: Understanding Wikidata Qualifiers: An Analysis and Taxonomy
summary: This paper presents an in-depth analysis of Wikidata qualifiers, focusing on their semantics and actual usage, with the aim of developing a taxonomy that addresses the challenges of selecting appropriate qualifiers, querying the graph, and making logical inferences. The study evaluates qualifier importance based on frequency and diversity, using a modified Shannon entropy index to account for the "long tail" phenomenon. By analyzing a Wikidata dump, the top 300 qualifiers were selected and categorized into a refined taxonomy that includes contextual, epistemic/uncertainty, structural, and additional qualifiers. The taxonomy aims to guide contributors in creating and querying statements, improve qualifier recommendation systems, and enhance knowledge graph design methodologies. The results show that the taxonomy effectively covers the most important qualifiers and provides a structured approach to understanding and utilizing qualifiers in Wikidata.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how qualifiers are used in Wikidata and proposes a taxonomy to better understand and use them.
AI.motivation: To address difficulties in choosing appropriate qualifiers, querying the knowledge graph, and making logical inferences due to the complex and varied use of qualifiers in Wikidata.
AI.method: The authors analyzed a Wikidata dump, evaluated qualifier importance using frequency and a modified Shannon entropy to handle long-tail distributions, selected the top 300 qualifiers, and manually categorized them into a refined taxonomy (contextual, epistemic/uncertainty, structural, and additional).
AI.result: They identified the 300 most important qualifiers and successfully classified them into a clear taxonomy, demonstrating that these categories cover the qualifiers that matter most in practice.
AI.conclusion: The proposed taxonomy offers practical guidance for contributors, improves qualifier recommendation and querying, and supports better knowledge graph design by providing a structured understanding of Wikidata qualifiers.

title: LifeSim: Long-Horizon User Life Simulator for Personalized Assistant Evaluation
summary: The rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs) has accelerated progress toward universal AI assistants. However, existing benchmarks for personalized assistants remain misaligned with real-world user-assistant interactions, failing to capture the complexity of external contexts and users' cognitive states. To bridge this gap, we propose LifeSim, a user simulator that models user cognition through the Belief-Desire-Intention (BDI) model within physical environments for coherent life trajectories generation, and simulates intention-driven user interactive behaviors. Based on LifeSim, we introduce LifeSim-Eval, a comprehensive benchmark for multi-scenario, long-horizon personalized assistance. LifeSim-Eval covers 8 life domains and 1,200 diverse scenarios, and adopts a multi-turn interactive method to assess models' abilities to complete explicit and implicit intentions, recover user profiles, and produce high-quality responses. Under both single-scenario and long-horizon settings, our experiments reveal that current LLMs face significant limitations in handling implicit intention and long-term user preference modeling.

AI.tldr: LifeSim introduces a simulated user environment to better benchmark personalized AI assistants.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for personalized AI assistants fail to match real-world interactions because they ignore rich external contexts and users' internal cognitive states. There is a need for an evaluation framework that captures complex, long-term, multi-scenario user behavior and intentions.
AI.method: The authors design LifeSim, a user simulator grounded in the Belief-Desire-Intention (BDI) cognitive model and embedded in physical environments, enabling coherent life trajectory generation and intention-driven interactive behaviors. On top of this simulator, they build LifeSim-Eval, a benchmark spanning 8 life domains and 1,200 scenarios, using multi-turn interactions to test models on explicit/implicit intention completion, user profile recovery, and response quality.
AI.result: Using LifeSim-Eval under single-scenario and long-horizon settings, the authors evaluate current LLMs and find they struggle notably with understanding and addressing implicit user intentions and with modeling user preferences over long time horizons.
AI.conclusion: LifeSim and LifeSim-Eval offer a more realistic, cognitively grounded and long-horizon framework for evaluating personalized AI assistants, and experimental results indicate significant remaining gaps in current LLMs' abilities to handle implicit intentions and long-term personalization.

title: Governing Evolving Memory in LLM Agents: Risks, Mechanisms, and the Stability and Safety Governed Memory (SSGM) Framework
summary: Long-term memory has emerged as a foundational component of autonomous Large Language Model (LLM) agents, enabling continuous adaptation, lifelong multimodal learning, and sophisticated reasoning. However, as memory systems transition from static retrieval databases to dynamic, agentic mechanisms, critical concerns regarding memory governance, semantic drift, and privacy vulnerabilities have surfaced. While recent surveys have focused extensively on memory retrieval efficiency, they largely overlook the emergent risks of memory corruption in highly dynamic environments. To address these emerging challenges, we propose the Stability and Safety-Governed Memory (SSGM) framework, a conceptual governance architecture. SSGM decouples memory evolution from execution by enforcing consistency verification, temporal decay modeling, and dynamic access control prior to any memory consolidation. Through formal analysis and architectural decomposition, we show how SSGM can mitigate topology-induced knowledge leakage where sensitive contexts are solidified into long-term storage, and help prevent semantic drift where knowledge degrades through iterative summarization. Ultimately, this work provides a comprehensive taxonomy of memory corruption risks and establishes a robust governance paradigm for deploying safe, persistent, and reliable agentic memory systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a governance framework (SSGM) to make long-term memory in LLM agents stable, safe, and reliable by managing how memories are stored, updated, and accessed.
AI.motivation: As LLM agents gain long-term, dynamic memory, they face new risks: privacy leaks when sensitive information is stored, semantic drift from repeated summarization, and general memory corruption in constantly changing environments. Existing work mainly optimizes retrieval efficiency and ignores these governance and safety issues, creating a gap that this paper aims to fill.
AI.method: The authors introduce the Stability and Safety-Governed Memory (SSGM) framework. Conceptually, it separates (decouples) memory evolution from the agent’s execution pipeline and adds governance steps before memory is consolidated: consistency verification, temporal decay modeling, and dynamic access control. They perform formal analysis and architectural decomposition to show how these components interact and how they mitigate specific risks.
AI.result: The framework yields a structured way to reduce topology-induced knowledge leakage and semantic drift in long-term memory systems. It produces a taxonomy of memory corruption risks and demonstrates, at a conceptual and architectural level, how SSGM components can limit unsafe or unstable memory updates in LLM agents.
AI.conclusion: SSGM provides a principled governance paradigm for long-term memory in LLM agents, emphasizing safety, stability, and reliability. By controlling how memories are verified, decayed, and accessed before consolidation, the framework aims to prevent corruption, leakage, and degradation of knowledge, supporting safer deployment of autonomous, memory-augmented agents.

title: QAQ: Bidirectional Semantic Coherence for Selecting High-Quality Synthetic Code Instructions
comment: 12 pages, 5 figures. Under review at ACL 2026
summary: Synthetic data has become essential for training code generation models, yet it introduces significant noise and hallucinations that are difficult to detect with current metrics. Existing data selection methods like Instruction-Following Difficulty (IFD) typically assess how hard a model generates an answer given a query ($A|Q$). However, this metric is ambiguous on noisy synthetic data, where low probability can distinguish between intrinsic task complexity and model-generated hallucinations. Here, we propose QAQ, a novel data selection framework that evaluates data quality from the reverse direction: how well can the answer predict the query ($Q|A$)? We define Reverse Mutual Information (RMI) to quantify the information gain about the query conditioned on the answer. Our analyses reveal that both extremes of RMI signal quality issues: low RMI indicates semantic misalignment, while excessively high RMI may contain defect patterns that LLMs easily recognize. Furthermore, we introduce a selection strategy based on the disagreement between strong and weak models to identify samples that are valid yet challenging. Experiments on the WarriorCoder dataset demonstrate that selecting just 25% of data using stratified RMI achieves comparable performance to full-data training, significantly outperforming existing data selection methods. Our approach highlights the importance of bidirectional semantic coherence in synthetic data curation, offering a scalable pathway to reduce computational costs without sacrificing model capability.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces QAQ, a reverse-direction data selection framework for synthetic code-generation datasets that measures how informative answers are about their queries using Reverse Mutual Information (RMI), enabling more precise filtering of noisy synthetic data and reducing training cost without hurting performance.
AI.motivation: Synthetic data is critical for scaling code generation models but is often noisy and hallucinated. Existing data selection metrics, like Instruction-Following Difficulty based on p(A|Q), cannot distinguish between truly difficult tasks and flawed synthetic answers, making them unreliable for filtering low-quality data. The authors seek a more robust signal of data quality that is sensitive to semantic alignment and defect patterns in synthetic QA pairs.
AI.method: The authors propose QAQ, a data selection framework that evaluates p(Q|A) instead of p(A|Q). They define Reverse Mutual Information (RMI) to quantify how much information an answer provides about its question. They analyze behavior at both low and high RMI to diagnose semantic misalignment and over-regular defect patterns. Additionally, they introduce a disagreement-based selection strategy that leverages differences between strong and weak models to identify samples that are valid yet challenging. They then apply stratified sampling based on RMI to curate subsets of the WarriorCoder synthetic code dataset.
AI.result: On the WarriorCoder dataset, selecting only 25% of the synthetic data based on stratified RMI yields model performance comparable to training on the full dataset and surpasses existing selection methods such as IFD. This shows that QAQ-based curation can significantly cut computational cost while preserving or improving model quality.
AI.conclusion: Bidirectional semantic coherence—captured through how well answers predict questions—is a powerful criterion for synthetic data quality. Reverse Mutual Information serves as an effective signal for detecting both misaligned and overly stereotyped samples. The QAQ framework, especially when combined with model disagreement, offers a scalable and more reliable method for curating synthetic datasets for code generation, enabling substantial data and compute reduction without degrading model capability.

title: An Automatic Text Classification Method Based on Hierarchical Taxonomies, Neural Networks and Document Embedding: The NETHIC Tool
summary: This work describes an automatic text classification method implemented in a software tool called NETHIC, which takes advantage of the inner capabilities of highly-scalable neural networks combined with the expressiveness of hierarchical taxonomies. As such, NETHIC succeeds in bringing about a mechanism for text classification that proves to be significantly effective as well as efficient. The tool had undergone an experimentation process against both a generic and a domain-specific corpus, outputting promising results. On the basis of this experimentation, NETHIC has been now further refined and extended by adding a document embedding mechanism, which has shown improvements in terms of performance on the individual networks and on the whole hierarchical model.

AI.tldr: Proposes NETHIC, an automatic text classification tool combining neural networks with hierarchical taxonomies; experiments on generic and domain corpora show promising performance, and adding document embeddings further improves results.
AI.motivation: Text classification needs methods that are both highly effective and scalable, and that can exploit structured category schemes (hierarchical taxonomies). Existing approaches may not simultaneously leverage powerful neural models and the structure of taxonomies, or may not scale well across different domains. The paper aims to design a tool that fills this gap and works on both generic and domain-specific corpora.
AI.method: Implement NETHIC, a text classification system that integrates highly scalable neural networks with hierarchical taxonomies of categories. The system is evaluated experimentally on both generic and domain-specific corpora. After initial experiments, the authors extend NETHIC by incorporating a document embedding mechanism into the neural and hierarchical framework, then re-evaluate performance at both the individual network (node) level and the full hierarchical model level.
AI.result: Initial experiments on generic and domain-specific corpora yield promising classification performance with good efficiency. After adding document embeddings, performance improves further, both for individual neural networks in the hierarchy and for the overall hierarchical classifier.
AI.conclusion: Combining scalable neural networks with hierarchical taxonomies is an effective and efficient strategy for automatic text classification. The NETHIC tool performs well across different corpora, and the integration of document embeddings further enhances its performance, indicating that enriched document representations are beneficial within hierarchical neural classification architectures.

title: Strategic Navigation or Stochastic Search? How Agents and Humans Reason Over Document Collections
summary: Multimodal agents offer a promising path to automating complex document-intensive workflows. Yet, a critical question remains: do these agents demonstrate genuine strategic reasoning, or merely stochastic trial-and-error search? To address this, we introduce MADQA, a benchmark of 2,250 human-authored questions grounded in 800 heterogeneous PDF documents. Guided by Classical Test Theory, we design it to maximize discriminative power across varying levels of agentic abilities. To evaluate agentic behaviour, we introduce a novel evaluation protocol measuring the accuracy-effort trade-off. Using this framework, we show that while the best agents can match human searchers in raw accuracy, they succeed on largely different questions and rely on brute-force search to compensate for weak strategic planning. They fail to close the nearly 20% gap to oracle performance, persisting in unproductive loops. We release the dataset and evaluation harness to help facilitate the transition from brute-force retrieval to calibrated, efficient reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MADQA, a benchmark and evaluation framework to test whether multimodal document agents perform genuine strategic reasoning or just brute-force trial-and-error search.
AI.motivation: Multimodal agents are increasingly used to automate complex, document-heavy workflows, but it is unclear if their performance comes from real planning and strategy or from large-scale random search. Existing benchmarks do not precisely differentiate levels of agentic ability or characterize the trade-off between accuracy and effort, leaving a gap in understanding and improving these systems.
AI.method: The authors construct MADQA, a dataset of 2,250 human-authored questions grounded in 800 diverse PDF documents, designed using principles from Classical Test Theory to maximize discrimination across different ability levels. They then propose a new evaluation protocol that explicitly measures the trade-off between accuracy and effort (e.g., number of steps, retrieval calls, or actions). Agents and human searchers are run on this benchmark, and their behaviours are analyzed in terms of accuracy, efficiency, and patterns of search versus planning, including comparison to an oracle upper bound.
AI.result: Under the new benchmark and protocol, state-of-the-art multimodal agents reach human-level accuracy on some dimensions but succeed on a different subset of questions than humans. Their performance heavily depends on brute-force search, with many redundant or unproductive actions, and they show weak strategic planning. Despite high effort, there remains a roughly 20% performance gap compared to an oracle that has perfect access to relevant information and reasoning paths.
AI.conclusion: MADQA reveals that current multimodal agents compensate for limited strategic reasoning with brute-force search and fail to reach oracle-level performance, often getting stuck in inefficient loops. The released dataset and evaluation harness provide a principled way to measure accuracy-effort trade-offs and are intended to drive development of agents that move beyond brute-force retrieval toward calibrated, efficient reasoning strategies.

title: From Debate to Deliberation: Structured Collective Reasoning with Typed Epistemic Acts
summary: Multi-agent LLM systems increasingly tackle complex reasoning, yet their interaction patterns remain limited to voting, unstructured debate, or pipeline orchestration. None model deliberation: a phased process where differentiated participants exchange typed reasoning moves, preserve disagreements, and converge on accountable outcomes. We introduce Deliberative Collective Intelligence (DCI), specifying four reasoning archetypes, 14 typed epistemic acts, a shared workspace, and DCI-CF, a convergent flow algorithm that guarantees termination with a structured decision packet containing the selected option, residual objections, minority report, and reopen conditions. We evaluate on 45 tasks across seven domains using Gemini 2.5 Flash. On non-routine tasks (n=40), DCI significantly improves over unstructured debate (+0.95, 95% CI [+0.41, +1.54]). DCI excels on hidden-profile tasks requiring perspective integration (9.56, highest of any system on any domain) while failing on routine decisions (5.39), confirming task-dependence. DCI produces 100% structured decision packets and 98% minority reports, artifacts absent from all baselines. However, DCI consumes ~62x single-agent tokens, and single-agent generation outperforms DCI on overall quality. DCI's contribution is not that more agents are better, but that consequential decisions benefit from deliberative structure when process accountability justifies the cost.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Deliberative Collective Intelligence (DCI), a structured multi-agent LLM framework that models deliberation with typed reasoning acts and guarantees a convergent, accountable decision output, improving performance on complex, non-routine tasks at substantial token cost.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-agent LLM systems rely on simple patterns like voting, free-form debate, or fixed pipelines, which do not capture real deliberation. These approaches lack explicit roles, structured reasoning moves, preservation of disagreement, and accountable decision artifacts. The authors aim to design a multi-agent interaction protocol that better mirrors human deliberative processes, especially for consequential, complex decisions requiring integration of diverse perspectives, and to empirically study when such structure helps or hurts.
AI.method: The authors define Deliberative Collective Intelligence (DCI), comprising: (1) four reasoning archetypes (differentiated participant roles); (2) 14 types of epistemic acts (typed reasoning moves); (3) a shared workspace where agents record and reference contributions; and (4) DCI-CF, a convergent flow algorithm that phases the interaction and guarantees termination. DCI-CF outputs a structured decision packet including the chosen option, residual objections, a minority report, and conditions for reopening the decision. They implement DCI with Gemini 2.5 Flash and evaluate it on 45 tasks spanning seven domains, distinguishing between non-routine and routine tasks, and include hidden-profile tasks that require perspective integration. They compare DCI against unstructured multi-agent debate and single-agent generation in terms of decision quality and process artifacts, while also tracking token usage.
AI.result: On 40 non-routine tasks, DCI significantly outperforms unstructured debate, with an improvement of +0.95 points (95% confidence interval [+0.41, +1.54]). On hidden-profile tasks that need integration of distributed information, DCI achieves a score of 9.56, the highest observed across all systems and domains in their study. However, on routine decision tasks, DCI underperforms with a score of 5.39, indicating that its benefits are task-dependent. DCI consistently generates structured decision packets in 100% of cases and produces minority reports in 98% of cases, artifacts not produced by baseline systems. The approach incurs a large computational cost, using about 62 times more tokens than a single-agent setup, and overall single-agent generation still yields higher average decision quality.
AI.conclusion: Structured deliberation protocols for multi-agent LLMs, as instantiated in DCI, can improve performance on complex, non-routine tasks, particularly those requiring perspective integration, while also producing rich artifacts that enhance process accountability. However, the benefits are highly task-dependent, and the method is computationally expensive and not universally superior in outcome quality to strong single-agent baselines. The main value of DCI lies less in raw accuracy gains and more in providing a principled, auditable deliberative process for consequential decisions where accountability can justify the added cost.

title: Long-Context Encoder Models for Polish Language Understanding
summary: While decoder-only Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently dominated the NLP landscape, encoder-only architectures remain a cost-effective and parameter-efficient standard for discriminative tasks. However, classic encoders like BERT are limited by a short context window, which is insufficient for processing long documents. In this paper, we address this limitation for the Polish by introducing a high-quality Polish model capable of processing sequences of up to 8192 tokens. The model was developed by employing a two-stage training procedure that involves positional embedding adaptation and full parameter continuous pre-training. Furthermore, we propose compressed model variants trained via knowledge distillation. The models were evaluated on 25 tasks, including the KLEJ benchmark, a newly introduced financial task suite (FinBench), and other classification and regression tasks, specifically those requiring long-document understanding. The results demonstrate that our model achieves the best average performance among Polish and multilingual models, significantly outperforming competitive solutions in long-context tasks while maintaining comparable quality on short texts.

AI.tldr: They build a long-context Polish encoder model (up to 8192 tokens), plus compressed distilled variants, and show it beats existing Polish and multilingual models, especially on long-document tasks, while staying competitive on short texts.
AI.motivation: Decoder-only LLMs dominate, but for many discriminative tasks encoder-only models are more cost- and parameter-efficient. Existing Polish encoders like BERT are constrained by short context windows, making them unsuitable for long-document understanding common in real applications (e.g., finance, legal, reports). There is a gap for a high-quality, long-context Polish encoder with good performance and efficiency.
AI.method: They extend an encoder-only architecture for Polish to handle sequences up to 8192 tokens via a two-stage training process: (1) positional embedding adaptation to support longer contexts; (2) full-parameter continuous pre-training to refine the model on the extended context. Additionally, they train compressed model variants using knowledge distillation from the long-context teacher model. They evaluate on 25 tasks, including the KLEJ benchmark, a new financial benchmark (FinBench), and other classification/regression tasks emphasizing long-document understanding.
AI.result: The long-context Polish encoder achieves the best average performance across Polish and multilingual baselines. It significantly outperforms competitors on tasks involving long contexts while retaining performance comparable to state-of-the-art on short-text tasks. Distilled variants preserve strong performance with reduced size and cost.
AI.conclusion: A carefully adapted and continuously pre-trained Polish encoder can effectively process much longer sequences (up to 8192 tokens) without sacrificing short-text quality. The proposed model and its distilled versions set a new performance standard, particularly for long-document Polish NLP tasks, confirming that encoder-only architectures remain highly competitive and efficient when equipped with extended context capabilities.

title: DocSage: An Information Structuring Agent for Multi-Doc Multi-Entity Question Answering
summary: Multi-document Multi-entity Question Answering inherently demands models to track implicit logic between multiple entities across scattered documents. However, existing Large Language Models (LLMs) and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) frameworks suffer from critical limitations: standard RAG's vector similarity-based coarse-grained retrieval often omits critical facts, graph-based RAG fails to efficiently integrate fragmented complex relationship networks, and both lack schema awareness, leading to inadequate cross-document evidence chain construction and inaccurate entity relationship deduction. To address these challenges, we propose DocSage, an end-to-end agentic framework that integrates dynamic schema discovery, structured information extraction, and schema-aware relational reasoning with error guarantees. DocSage operates through three core modules: (1) A schema discovery module dynamically infers query-specific minimal joinable schemas to capture essential entities and relationships; (2) An extraction module transforms unstructured text into semantically coherent relational tables, enhanced by error-aware correction mechanisms to reduce extraction errors; (3) A reasoning module performs multi-hop relational reasoning over structured tables, leveraging schema awareness to efficiently align cross-document entities and aggregate evidence. This agentic design offers three key advantages: precise fact localization via SQL-powered indexing, natural support for cross-document entity joins through relational tables, and mitigated LLM attention diffusion via structured representation. Evaluations on two MDMEQA benchmarks demonstrate that DocSage significantly outperforms state-of-the-art long-context LLMs and RAG systems, achieving more than 27% accuracy improvements respectively.

AI.tldr: DocSage is an agentic framework for multi-document, multi-entity QA that turns text into relational tables via dynamic schema discovery and uses schema-aware reasoning to outperform existing LLM and RAG methods.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM and RAG approaches struggle with multi-document, multi-entity QA because coarse vector-based retrieval misses key facts, graph-based RAG cannot efficiently handle complex fragmented relationships, and both lack explicit schema awareness, which is crucial for building cross-document evidence chains and accurately inferring entity relationships.
AI.method: DocSage is an end-to-end agentic pipeline with three components: (1) a schema discovery module that dynamically infers a minimal, query-specific joinable schema capturing essential entities and relations; (2) an extraction module that converts unstructured documents into semantically coherent relational tables and uses error-aware correction mechanisms to mitigate extraction noise; and (3) a schema-aware reasoning module that executes multi-hop relational reasoning over these tables, aligning entities across documents and aggregating supporting evidence, leveraging SQL-style indexing and joins.
AI.result: On two multi-document multi-entity QA benchmarks, DocSage substantially surpasses state-of-the-art long-context LLMs and RAG systems, yielding more than 27% absolute accuracy improvements on each benchmark.
AI.conclusion: By combining dynamic schema discovery, structured information extraction, and schema-aware relational reasoning within an agentic framework, DocSage enables precise fact localization, natural cross-document joins, and reduced attention diffusion, resulting in much stronger performance on complex multi-document, multi-entity QA tasks than existing LLM and RAG baselines.

title: IndexCache: Accelerating Sparse Attention via Cross-Layer Index Reuse
summary: Long-context agentic workflows have emerged as a defining use case for large language models, making attention efficiency critical for both inference speed and serving cost. Sparse attention addresses this challenge effectively, and DeepSeek Sparse Attention (DSA) is a representative production-grade solution: a lightweight lightning indexer selects the top-k most relevant tokens per query, reducing core attention from $O(L^2)$ to $O(Lk)$. However, the indexer itself retains $O(L^2)$ complexity and must run independently at every layer, despite the fact that the resulting top-k selections are highly similar across consecutive layers. We present IndexCache, which exploits this cross-layer redundancy by partitioning layers into a small set of Full layers that run their own indexers and a majority of Shared layers that simply reuse the nearest Full layer's top-k indices. We propose two complementary approaches to determine and optimize this configuration. Training-free IndexCache applies a greedy search algorithm that selects which layers to retain indexers by directly minimizing language modeling loss on a calibration set, requiring no weight updates. Training-aware IndexCache introduces a multi-layer distillation loss that trains each retained indexer against the averaged attention distributions of all layers it serves, enabling even simple interleaved patterns to match full-indexer accuracy. Experimental results on a 30B DSA model show that IndexCache can remove 75% of indexer computations with negligible quality degradation, achieving up to 1.82$\times$ prefill speedup and 1.48$\times$ decode speedup compared to standard DSA. These positive results are further confirmed by our preliminary experiments on the production-scale GLM-5 model (Figure 1).

AI.tldr: The paper proposes IndexCache, a method to reuse sparse-attention indices across transformer layers to cut computation while keeping accuracy, yielding significant speedups over DeepSeek Sparse Attention.
AI.motivation: Long-context LLMs rely on attention over many tokens, which is computationally expensive. Sparse attention methods like DeepSeek Sparse Attention reduce attention cost but still require an expensive indexer to be run independently at every layer, even though the selected top-k tokens are very similar between nearby layers. This redundant per-layer indexing limits the efficiency gains and increases serving cost and latency. The paper aims to remove this redundancy while preserving model quality.
AI.method: They introduce IndexCache, which partitions transformer layers into a small number of Full layers and many Shared layers. Full layers run the usual sparse-attention indexer; Shared layers simply reuse the nearest Full layer’s top-k indices instead of recomputing them. Two strategies determine which layers are Full: (1) Training-free IndexCache uses a greedy search over layer configurations to minimize language modeling loss on a calibration set without updating model weights; (2) Training-aware IndexCache adds a multi-layer distillation loss so each retained indexer learns to approximate the averaged attention distribution of all layers it serves, allowing simple interleaved patterns (e.g., every Nth layer) to perform well. They test this on a 30B-parameter DeepSeek Sparse Attention model and preliminarily on GLM-5.
AI.result: On a 30B DSA model, IndexCache eliminates 75% of indexer computations with negligible degradation in language modeling quality. It yields up to 1.82× speedup in the prefill phase and 1.48× speedup in the decode phase compared to standard DeepSeek Sparse Attention. Preliminary experiments on the production-scale GLM-5 model show similarly positive trends (details referenced in Figure 1).
AI.conclusion: Cross-layer redundancy in sparse-attention index selection can be exploited to substantially reduce computation without retraining the base model heavily. By caching and reusing top-k indices across layers—via either a training-free greedy configuration search or a training-aware distillation scheme—IndexCache significantly speeds up long-context LLM inference while maintaining accuracy, and appears to generalize to large production models like GLM-5.

title: A Semi-Decentralized Approach to Multiagent Control
summary: We introduce an expressive framework and algorithms for the semi-decentralized control of cooperative agents in environments with communication uncertainty. Whereas semi-Markov control admits a distribution over time for agent actions, semi-Markov communication, or what we refer to as semi-decentralization, gives a distribution over time for what actions and observations agents can store in their histories. We extend semi-decentralization to the partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP). The resulting SDec-POMDP unifies decentralized and multiagent POMDPs and several existing explicit communication mechanisms. We present recursive small-step semi-decentralized A* (RS-SDA*), an exact algorithm for generating optimal SDec-POMDP policies. RS-SDA* is evaluated on semi-decentralized versions of several standard benchmarks and a maritime medical evacuation scenario. This paper provides a well-defined theoretical foundation for exploring many classes of multiagent communication problems through the lens of semi-decentralization.

AI.tldr: They propose a new framework (SDec-POMDP) and exact algorithm (RS-SDA*) for controlling cooperative agents when communication is uncertain in time, unifying several prior multiagent models.
AI.motivation: Traditional decentralized and multiagent POMDP models either assume fixed communication structures or do not model the timing and reliability of communication histories explicitly. Many real-world multiagent systems operate under uncertain, delayed, or intermittent communication, which affects what information agents can store and base their decisions on. There is a need for a principled, general framework that can represent such semi-decentralized communication patterns and support optimal control.
AI.method: They generalize the notion of semi-Markov control from actions to communication: instead of just modeling distributions over action durations, they model distributions over when actions and observations become available in an agent’s history. They formalize this as a Semi-Decentralized POMDP (SDec-POMDP) that subsumes decentralized POMDPs, multiagent POMDPs, and explicit communication models. They then design RS-SDA*, a recursive, small-step, semi-decentralized A* search algorithm that exactly computes optimal policies in this framework, and apply it to benchmark problems and a realistic maritime medical evacuation domain.
AI.result: RS-SDA* can generate optimal policies for SDec-POMDPs and is successfully applied to semi-decentralized versions of several standard benchmarks and to a maritime medical evacuation scenario, demonstrating that the framework is expressive enough and the algorithm is practically applicable at least on moderate-sized problems.
AI.conclusion: Semi-decentralization, captured formally by the SDec-POMDP model, offers a unifying theoretical lens for a wide variety of multiagent communication problems. The RS-SDA* algorithm shows that optimal control is tractable for meaningful instances, positioning this framework as a solid foundation for future research on multiagent control under uncertain communication timing.

title: Automating Skill Acquisition through Large-Scale Mining of Open-Source Agentic Repositories: A Framework for Multi-Agent Procedural Knowledge Extraction
