summary: The transition from monolithic large language models (LLMs) to modular, skill-equipped agents represents a fundamental architectural shift in artificial intelligence deployment. While general-purpose models demonstrate remarkable breadth in declarative knowledge, their utility in autonomous workflows is frequently constrained by insufficient specialized procedural expertise. This report investigates a systematic framework for automated acquisition of high-quality agent skills through mining of open-source repositories on platforms such as GitHub. We focus on the extraction of visualization and educational capabilities from state-of-the-art systems including TheoremExplainAgent and Code2Video, both utilizing the Manim mathematical animation engine. The framework encompasses repository structural analysis, semantic skill identification through dense retrieval, and translation to the standardized SKILL.md format. We demonstrate that systematic extraction from agentic repositories, combined with rigorous security governance and multi-dimensional evaluation metrics, enables scalable acquisition of procedural knowledge that augments LLM capabilities without requiring model retraining. Our analysis reveals that agent-generated educational content can achieve 40\% gains in knowledge transfer efficiency while maintaining pedagogical quality comparable to human-crafted tutorials.

AI.tldr: They propose a framework to automatically mine open-source agent repositories (like TheoremExplainAgent and Code2Video) to extract reusable skills, especially for visualization and education, and package them into a standard format to augment LLM agents without retraining, showing large gains in knowledge-transfer efficiency.
AI.motivation: Monolithic LLMs are good at general declarative knowledge but weak at specialized procedural skills needed for autonomous workflows. Manually building skills/agents doesn’t scale. There is a need for an automated, secure, and evaluable way to acquire high-quality skills from the growing ecosystem of open-source agentic systems, particularly for educational and visualization tasks.
AI.method: They define a systematic pipeline that: (1) analyzes the structure of open-source agent repositories; (2) uses dense retrieval/semantic search to identify code and components that implement skills (e.g., visualization, explanation); (3) converts these into a standardized SKILL.md specification. They focus on repositories using the Manim animation engine (e.g., TheoremExplainAgent, Code2Video). The framework includes security governance checks and multi-dimensional evaluation metrics for the extracted skills.
AI.result: Systematic mining of agent repositories successfully yields reusable skills that can be plugged into LLM-based agents without retraining the underlying models. For educational agents using Manim-based visualizations, the extracted skills enable generation of educational content that improves knowledge-transfer efficiency by about 40% while keeping pedagogical quality on par with human-written tutorials.
AI.conclusion: Automated skill extraction from open-source agent repositories is a viable and scalable way to enrich LLM agents with procedural capabilities. With proper security and evaluation, this modular skill-acquisition framework can significantly enhance autonomous agents—especially for visualization and education—without expensive model retraining, and can match or exceed human-level pedagogical effectiveness.

title: Sparking Scientific Creativity via LLM-Driven Interdisciplinary Inspiration
summary: Despite interdisciplinary research leading to larger and longer-term impact, most work remains confined to single-domain academic silos. Recent AI-based approaches to scientific discovery show promise for interdisciplinary research, but many prioritize rapidly designing experiments and solutions, bypassing the exploratory, collaborative reasoning processes that drive creative interdisciplinary breakthroughs. As a result, prior efforts largely prioritize automating scientific discovery rather than augmenting the reasoning processes that underlie scientific disruption. We present Idea-Catalyst, a novel framework that systematically identifies interdisciplinary insights to support creative reasoning in both humans and large language models. Starting from an abstract research goal, Idea-Catalyst is designed to assist the brainstorming stage, explicitly avoiding premature anchoring on specific solutions. The framework embodies key metacognitive features of interdisciplinary reasoning: (a) defining and assessing research goals, (b) awareness of a domain's opportunities and unresolved challenges, and (c) strategic exploration of interdisciplinary ideas based on impact potential. Concretely, Idea-Catalyst decomposes an abstract goal (e.g., improving human-AI collaboration) into core target-domain research questions that guide the analysis of progress and open challenges within that domain. These challenges are reformulated as domain-agnostic conceptual problems, enabling retrieval from external disciplines (e.g., Psychology, Sociology) that address analogous issues. By synthesizing and recontextualizing insights from these domains back into the target domain, Idea-Catalyst ranks source domains by their interdisciplinary potential. Empirically, this targeted integration improves average novelty by 21% and insightfulness by 16%, while remaining grounded in the original research problem.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Idea-Catalyst, a framework that systematically generates interdisciplinary insights to support creative scientific reasoning without prematurely locking into specific solutions.
AI.motivation: Interdisciplinary research yields higher long-term impact, but most scientific work stays within single-domain silos. Existing AI tools for scientific discovery focus on automating experiment design and solution generation, neglecting the exploratory, metacognitive, and collaborative reasoning that drives disruptive interdisciplinary ideas. There is a need for AI systems that augment, rather than replace, human-like interdisciplinary reasoning during the early brainstorming stage.
AI.method: Idea-Catalyst begins from an abstract research goal and decomposes it into core research questions within a target domain. It then analyzes progress and unresolved challenges in that domain and reformulates these challenges as domain-agnostic conceptual problems. Using these abstracted problems, the system retrieves analogous solutions and perspectives from other source domains (e.g., psychology, sociology). It then synthesizes and recontextualizes these cross-domain insights back into the target domain and ranks the contributing source domains by their interdisciplinary potential. The framework encodes metacognitive features such as goal clarification, awareness of domain opportunities/gaps, and strategic exploration based on potential impact.
AI.result: When evaluated, Idea-Catalyst’s targeted interdisciplinary integration improves the average novelty of generated ideas by 21% and their insightfulness by 16%, while still keeping them relevant to the original research problem.
AI.conclusion: Idea-Catalyst effectively augments creative, interdisciplinary reasoning for both humans and large language models at the brainstorming stage. By formalizing metacognitive aspects of interdisciplinary thinking and systematically importing and adapting ideas from other fields, it produces more novel and insightful yet still grounded research directions, demonstrating a promising path for AI tools that catalyze rather than automate scientific discovery.

title: VisiFold: Long-Term Traffic Forecasting via Temporal Folding Graph and Node Visibility
summary: Traffic forecasting is a cornerstone of intelligent transportation systems. While existing research has made significant progress in short-term prediction, long-term forecasting remains a largely uncharted and challenging frontier. Extending the prediction horizon intensifies two critical issues: escalating computational resource consumption and increasingly complex spatial-temporal dependencies. Current approaches, which rely on spatial-temporal graphs and process temporal and spatial dimensions separately, suffer from snapshot-stacking inflation and cross-step fragmentation. To overcome these limitations, we propose \textit{VisiFold}. Our framework introduces a novel temporal folding graph that consolidates a sequence of temporal snapshots into a single graph. Furthermore, we present a node visibility mechanism that incorporates node-level masking and subgraph sampling to overcome the computational bottleneck imposed by large node counts. Extensive experiments show that VisiFold not only drastically reduces resource consumption but also outperforms existing baselines in long-term forecasting tasks. Remarkably, even with a high mask ratio of 80\%, VisiFold maintains its performance advantage. By effectively breaking the resource constraints in both temporal and spatial dimensions, our work paves the way for more realistic long-term traffic forecasting. The code is available at~ https://github.com/PlanckChang/VisiFold.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes VisiFold, a new traffic forecasting framework that enables efficient and accurate long-term prediction by folding temporal snapshots into a single graph and using node visibility mechanisms to reduce computation.
AI.motivation: Long-term traffic forecasting is critical for intelligent transportation systems but remains difficult due to high computational costs and complex spatial-temporal dependencies. Existing graph-based methods process temporal snapshots separately and stack them, which leads to increasing memory and computation (snapshot-stacking inflation) and fragmented modeling across time steps (cross-step fragmentation). There is a need for a method that can extend prediction horizons without blowing up resource usage while still effectively modeling spatial-temporal relationships.
AI.method: The authors propose VisiFold, which introduces: (1) a temporal folding graph that consolidates multiple temporal snapshots into a single unified graph representation, avoiding the need to stack many separate snapshot graphs; and (2) a node visibility mechanism combining node-level masking and subgraph sampling to control the number of active nodes during computation, thereby reducing resource usage when dealing with large networks. These designs jointly address both temporal and spatial scalability issues compared to conventional spatial-temporal GNNs.
AI.result: Experiments on long-term traffic forecasting benchmarks show that VisiFold significantly reduces computational resource consumption relative to existing baselines, while also achieving better prediction accuracy for long horizons. The method remains robust even when up to 80% of nodes are masked, still outperforming baselines under these constrained conditions.
AI.conclusion: VisiFold effectively alleviates the computational bottlenecks of long-term traffic forecasting in both temporal and spatial dimensions by using a folded temporal graph and node visibility mechanisms. This enables more realistic long-horizon prediction scenarios and suggests that carefully designed graph structures and masking strategies can maintain or improve accuracy while greatly improving efficiency. The work opens a path toward scalable, practical deployment of long-term traffic forecasting systems.

title: SciMDR: Benchmarking and Advancing Scientific Multimodal Document Reasoning
summary: Constructing scientific multimodal document reasoning datasets for foundation model training involves an inherent trade-off among scale, faithfulness, and realism. To address this challenge, we introduce the synthesize-and-reground framework, a two-stage pipeline comprising: (1) Claim-Centric QA Synthesis, which generates faithful, isolated QA pairs and reasoning on focused segments, and (2) Document-Scale Regrounding, which programmatically re-embeds these pairs into full-document tasks to ensure realistic complexity. Using this framework, we construct SciMDR, a large-scale training dataset for cross-modal comprehension, comprising 300K QA pairs with explicit reasoning chains across 20K scientific papers. We further construct SciMDR-Eval, an expert-annotated benchmark to evaluate multimodal comprehension within full-length scientific workflows. Experiments demonstrate that models fine-tuned on SciMDR achieve significant improvements across multiple scientific QA benchmarks, particularly in those tasks requiring complex document-level reasoning.

AI.tldr: They propose a new two-stage framework to build large, realistic, and faithful multimodal scientific document QA datasets, and show it improves document-level scientific reasoning of models.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal scientific document reasoning datasets struggle to balance three competing goals: being large-scale for training, being faithful to the source documents (avoiding hallucinated or loosely grounded questions/answers), and being realistic in terms of document-level complexity instead of short, artificial snippets. This paper aims to resolve that trade-off.
AI.method: They introduce a two-stage "synthesize-and-reground" pipeline. Stage 1, Claim-Centric QA Synthesis, generates high-quality, faithful QA pairs and step-by-step reasoning focused on small, local segments of documents. Stage 2, Document-Scale Regrounding, then programmatically re-embeds these local QAs into tasks over full documents, so that answering requires navigating realistic, full-length scientific papers, including cross-modal content (text, figures, tables). Using this, they construct SciMDR, a dataset with 300K QA pairs and explicit reasoning over 20K scientific papers, and SciMDR-Eval, an expert-annotated evaluation benchmark for full-document multimodal reasoning.
AI.result: They build SciMDR (300K QA pairs with reasoning over 20K papers) as a training set and SciMDR-Eval as an expert-labeled benchmark. Models fine-tuned on SciMDR show substantial gains on multiple existing scientific QA benchmarks, especially those that require complex, document-level reasoning across modalities.
AI.conclusion: The synthesize-and-reground framework can effectively reconcile scale, faithfulness, and realism when constructing multimodal scientific document reasoning datasets. The resulting SciMDR dataset and SciMDR-Eval benchmark significantly enhance and accurately evaluate models' ability to perform complex, document-scale scientific reasoning, particularly in multimodal settings.

title: Automated Detection of Malignant Lesions in the Ovary Using Deep Learning Models and XAI
comment: Accepted and published at ICAIC 2025. Accepted version
summary: The unrestrained proliferation of cells that are malignant in nature is cancer. In recent times, medical professionals are constantly acquiring enhanced diagnostic and treatment abilities by implementing deep learning models to analyze medical data for better clinical decision, disease diagnosis and drug discovery. A majority of cancers are studied and treated by incorporating these technologies. However, ovarian cancer remains a dilemma as it has inaccurate non-invasive detection procedures and a time consuming, invasive procedure for accurate detection. Thus, in this research, several Convolutional Neural Networks such as LeNet-5, ResNet, VGGNet and GoogLeNet/Inception have been utilized to develop 15 variants and choose a model that accurately detects and identifies ovarian cancer. For effective model training, the dataset OvarianCancer&SubtypesDatasetHistopathology from Mendeley has been used. After constructing a model, we utilized Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) models such as LIME, Integrated Gradients and SHAP to explain the black box outcome of the selected model. For evaluating the performance of the model, Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, ROC Curve and AUC have been used. From the evaluation, it was seen that the slightly compact InceptionV3 model with ReLu had the overall best result achieving an average score of 94% across all the performance metrics in the augmented dataset. Lastly for XAI, the three aforementioned XAI have been used for an overall comparative analysis. It is the aim of this research that the contributions of the study will help in achieving a better detection method for ovarian cancer.

AI.tldr: They compare several CNN architectures on a histopathology image dataset for ovarian cancer and use XAI methods to explain the chosen model, finding InceptionV3 with ReLU performs best (~94% on augmented data).
AI.motivation: Ovarian cancer is hard to detect non-invasively and current accurate methods are invasive and time-consuming, so better automated image-based detection tools are needed to improve early diagnosis and treatment planning.
AI.method: They build 15 CNN variants based on LeNet-5, ResNet, VGGNet, and GoogLeNet/Inception (including a compact InceptionV3 with ReLU) and train them on the OvarianCancer&SubtypesDatasetHistopathology dataset with augmentation; they then apply XAI methods (LIME, Integrated Gradients, SHAP) to interpret the best-performing model; performance is evaluated with Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, ROC, and AUC.
AI.result: Among all tested architectures, a compact InceptionV3 with ReLU achieved the best and most consistent performance, with about 94% average across all metrics on the augmented dataset; XAI analyses provide visual and quantitative explanations of model decisions and allow comparison among LIME, Integrated Gradients, and SHAP explanations.
AI.conclusion: A tailored InceptionV3-based CNN can accurately detect and identify ovarian cancer from histopathology images, and XAI techniques can make its decisions more interpretable, pointing toward more reliable and clinically useful non-invasive or semi-automated diagnostic support tools for ovarian cancer.

title: CreativeBench: Benchmarking and Enhancing Machine Creativity via Self-Evolving Challenges
summary: The saturation of high-quality pre-training data has shifted research focus toward evolutionary systems capable of continuously generating novel artifacts, leading to the success of AlphaEvolve. However, the progress of such systems is hindered by the lack of rigorous, quantitative evaluation. To tackle this challenge, we introduce CreativeBench, a benchmark for evaluating machine creativity in code generation, grounded in a classical cognitive framework. Comprising two subsets -- CreativeBench-Combo and CreativeBench-Explore -- the benchmark targets combinatorial and exploratory creativity through an automated pipeline utilizing reverse engineering and self-play. By leveraging executable code, CreativeBench objectively distinguishes creativity from hallucination via a unified metric defined as the product of quality and novelty. Our analysis of state-of-the-art models reveals distinct behaviors: (1) scaling significantly improves combinatorial creativity but yields diminishing returns for exploration; (2) larger models exhibit ``convergence-by-scaling,'' becoming more correct but less divergent; and (3) reasoning capabilities primarily benefit constrained exploration rather than combination. Finally, we propose EvoRePE, a plug-and-play inference-time steering strategy that internalizes evolutionary search patterns to consistently enhance machine creativity.

AI.tldr: CreativeBench is a benchmark for rigorously measuring machine creativity in code generation, distinguishing true creativity from hallucination via an executable, quantitative metric, and it is used to study how model scale and reasoning affect different types of creativity, plus a new steering method (EvoRePE) to boost it.
AI.motivation: Research in generative models is limited by a lack of rigorous, quantitative ways to evaluate ‘creativity’, especially in evolving, open-ended systems like AlphaEvolve that must continually generate novel, high-quality artifacts. Without a principled benchmark and metric, it is hard to compare models, understand scaling behavior, or systematically improve machine creativity. The paper aims to fill this gap in the domain of code generation by grounding evaluation in cognitive theories of creativity and executable artifacts.
AI.method: The authors build CreativeBench, a benchmark for code-generation creativity, grounded in a classical cognitive framework distinguishing combinatorial and exploratory creativity. They create two subsets: CreativeBench-Combo (for combinational creativity) and CreativeBench-Explore (for exploratory creativity). Tasks are generated via an automated pipeline using reverse engineering and self-play over executable code. Creativity is quantified with a unified metric defined as quality × novelty, where quality and novelty are both measured via properties of the generated, executable programs, thus separating creativity from mere hallucination. Using this benchmark, they run systematic experiments on state-of-the-art models of different sizes and reasoning capabilities. They also introduce EvoRePE, an inference-time, plug-and-play steering strategy that injects evolutionary search patterns into generation to enhance creativity without retraining models.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on CreativeBench shows: (1) Scaling up model size substantially improves combinatorial creativity but offers diminishing gains for exploratory creativity. (2) Larger models tend to ‘converge-by-scaling’: they produce more correct (high-quality) but less divergent (less novel) code, indicating a trade-off between reliability and exploration. (3) Enhanced reasoning abilities (e.g., via reasoning-augmented models) mainly boost constrained exploration tasks but do not significantly improve combinational creativity. Applying EvoRePE at inference consistently improves the measured creativity scores across models, demonstrating its effectiveness as a steering method.
AI.conclusion: CreativeBench provides a rigorous, executable, and cognitively grounded benchmark and metric for assessing machine creativity in code generation, enabling clear separation of creativity from hallucination. Analysis on this benchmark uncovers important scaling laws and behavioral trends: larger models are better at combinational creativity and correctness but less exploratory, and reasoning tools primarily help certain exploratory settings. The proposed EvoRePE steering method can systematically increase machine creativity at inference time. Overall, the work establishes a practical framework and toolkit for studying, comparing, and improving creative behavior in code-generating AI systems.

title: Social, Legal, Ethical, Empathetic and Cultural Norm Operationalisation for AI Agents
summary: As AI agents are increasingly used in high-stakes domains like healthcare and law enforcement, aligning their behaviour with social, legal, ethical, empathetic, and cultural (SLEEC) norms has become a critical engineering challenge. While international frameworks have established high-level normative principles for AI, a significant gap remains in translating these abstract principles into concrete, verifiable requirements. To address this gap, we propose a systematic SLEEC-norm operationalisation process for determining, validating, implementing, and verifying normative requirements. Furthermore, we survey the landscape of methods and tools supporting this process, and identify key remaining challenges and research avenues for addressing them. We thus establish a framework - and define a research and policy agenda - for developing AI agents that are not only functionally useful but also demonstrably aligned with human norms and values.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a systematic process for turning high-level social, legal, ethical, empathetic, and cultural (SLEEC) AI principles into concrete, testable requirements and surveys current methods and tools, outlining a research and policy agenda for norm-aligned AI agents.
AI.motivation: AI agents are increasingly deployed in high-stakes settings such as healthcare and law enforcement, where misalignment with social, legal, ethical, empathetic, and cultural norms can cause serious harm. Existing international AI guidelines provide only abstract principles, leaving practitioners without clear, operational, and verifiable requirements. This gap between high-level norms and concrete system specifications motivates a structured operationalisation process.
AI.method: The authors introduce a systematic SLEEC-norm operationalisation process that covers the full lifecycle of normative requirements: determining which norms and requirements are relevant, validating them with stakeholders, implementing them in AI agents, and verifying that the systems comply. In addition, they conduct a survey of existing methods and tools that can support each stage of this process, mapping them to the proposed framework and analysing their strengths and limitations.
AI.result: The paper delivers a conceptual framework and structured process for operationalising SLEEC norms into actionable requirements for AI agents. It also provides a survey and classification of current techniques and tools that can aid in requirements elicitation, validation, implementation, and verification of norm alignment, and highlights where coverage is strong or weak across the lifecycle.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that their operationalisation framework and survey together form a foundation for systematically aligning AI agents with human social, legal, ethical, empathetic, and cultural norms. They argue that this contributes both to research—by clarifying open technical problems—and to policy—by suggesting how high-level normative principles can be made concrete and auditable. Remaining challenges and research directions are identified as necessary steps toward building AI systems that are not only effective but also demonstrably norm-compliant.

title: AdaFuse: Accelerating Dynamic Adapter Inference via Token-Level Pre-Gating and Fused Kernel Optimization
comment: Accepted to AAAI 2026. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2405.17741
summary: The integration of dynamic, sparse structures like Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) with parameter-efficient adapters (e.g., LoRA) is a powerful technique for enhancing Large Language Models (LLMs). However, this architectural enhancement comes at a steep cost: despite minimal increases in computational load, the inference latency often skyrockets, leading to decoding speeds slowing by over 2.5 times. Through a fine-grained performance analysis, we pinpoint the primary bottleneck not in the computation itself, but in the severe overhead from fragmented, sequential CUDA kernel launches required for conventional dynamic routing. To address this challenge, we introduce AdaFuse, a framework built on a tight co-design between the algorithm and the underlying hardware system to enable efficient dynamic adapter execution. Departing from conventional layer-wise or block-wise routing, AdaFuse employs a token-level pre-gating strategy, which makes a single, global routing decision for all adapter layers before a token is processed. This "decide-once, apply-everywhere" approach effectively staticizes the execution path for each token, creating an opportunity for holistic optimization. We capitalize on this by developing a custom CUDA kernel that performs a fused switching operation, merging the parameters of all selected LoRA adapters into the backbone model in a single, efficient pass. Experimental results on popular open-source LLMs show that AdaFuse achieves accuracy on par with state-of-the-art dynamic adapters while drastically cutting decoding latency by a factor of over 2.4x, thereby bridging the gap between model capability and inference efficiency.

AI.tldr: AdaFuse is a hardware-algorithm co-designed framework that removes the routing-induced latency of dynamic LoRA/MoE adapters in LLMs by making a single token-level routing decision and fusing all selected adapters into one custom CUDA kernel, achieving ~2.4x faster decoding with similar accuracy.
AI.motivation: Dynamic sparse methods like MoE and dynamic LoRA adapters can boost LLM capability without huge compute cost, but they cause large inference latency due to many small, sequential CUDA kernel launches for per-layer routing. There is a need to keep the benefits of dynamic adapters while eliminating their system-level overhead, so that LLMs can be both powerful and fast at inference.
AI.method: The paper first profiles dynamic adapter execution in LLMs to identify that the main bottleneck is kernel launch and routing overhead rather than FLOPs. It then proposes AdaFuse, which introduces token-level pre-gating: for each token, a single routing decision is made that applies to all adapter layers, effectively fixing the execution path. On the systems side, the authors design a custom fused CUDA kernel that, given the pre-gating decision, merges all selected LoRA adapters’ parameters into the backbone weights in one pass, eliminating fragmented, layer-wise switching and enabling efficient execution on GPU hardware.
AI.result: On several open-source LLMs using LoRA-based dynamic adapters, AdaFuse maintains accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art dynamic routing methods while reducing decoding latency by more than 2.4x. This demonstrates that the approach successfully preserves model quality yet drastically improves end-to-end inference efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic adapters are mainly limited by routing-induced system overhead rather than pure computation. By co-designing routing algorithms with GPU execution (token-level pre-gating plus fused switching kernels), AdaFuse effectively staticizes each token’s path and removes fragmented kernel launches, achieving large latency reductions without sacrificing accuracy. This closes much of the gap between the representational benefits of dynamic adapters and the practical need for fast LLM inference.

title: Fair Learning for Bias Mitigation and Quality Optimization in Paper Recommendation
comment: arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2602.22438
summary: Despite frequent double-blind review, demographic biases of authors still disadvantage the underrepresented groups. We present Fair-PaperRec, a MultiLayer Perceptron (MLP)-based model that addresses demographic disparities in post-review paper acceptance decisions while maintaining high-quality requirements. Our methodology penalizes demographic disparities while preserving quality through intersectional criteria (e.g., race, country) and a customized fairness loss, in contrast to heuristic approaches. Evaluations using conference data from ACM Special Interest Group on Computer-Human Interaction (SIGCHI), Designing Interactive Systems (DIS), and Intelligent User Interfaces (IUI) indicate a 42.03% increase in underrepresented group participation and a 3.16% improvement in overall utility, indicating that diversity promotion does not compromise academic rigor and supports equity-focused peer review solutions.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Fair-PaperRec, an MLP-based model to make post-review paper acceptance decisions fairer across demographic groups while preserving review quality, achieving higher underrepresented group participation and slightly improved overall utility on real conference data.
AI.motivation: Double-blind review does not fully remove demographic biases in paper acceptance; underrepresented groups remain disadvantaged. The authors aim to design a systematic, data-driven method that can correct demographic disparities in acceptance decisions without sacrificing academic quality, going beyond ad-hoc or heuristic fairness adjustments.
AI.method: They design Fair-PaperRec, a MultiLayer Perceptron model that predicts paper acceptance using review and metadata while explicitly incorporating fairness. The method defines intersectional demographic groups (e.g., combinations of race and country) and introduces a customized fairness loss term that penalizes disparities in outcomes between these groups, jointly optimized with a quality/utility objective, instead of relying on simple heuristic rules or post-hoc adjustments.
AI.result: On paper-review datasets from major HCI-related conferences (ACM SIGCHI, DIS, IUI), the model yields a 42.03% relative increase in the participation (acceptance) of underrepresented groups and a 3.16% improvement in overall utility/quality metrics, compared with baseline or existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: Fair-PaperRec can simultaneously enhance demographic fairness and maintain or even improve academic rigor in peer review decisions. The results suggest that carefully designed learning-based fairness objectives can support more equitable, diversity-promoting peer review processes without compromising quality.

title: XSkill: Continual Learning from Experience and Skills in Multimodal Agents
summary: Multimodal agents can now tackle complex reasoning tasks with diverse tools, yet they still suffer from inefficient tool use and inflexible orchestration in open-ended settings. A central challenge is enabling such agents to continually improve without parameter updates by learning from past trajectories. We identify two complementary forms of reusable knowledge essential for this goal: experiences, providing concise action-level guidance for tool selection and decision making, and skills, providing structured task-level guidance for planning and tool use. To this end, we propose XSkill, a dual-stream framework for continual learning from experience and skills in multimodal agents. XSkill grounds both knowledge extraction and retrieval in visual observations. During accumulation, XSkill distills and consolidates experiences and skills from multi-path rollouts via visually grounded summarization and cross-rollout critique. During inference, it retrieves and adapts this knowledge to the current visual context and feeds usage history back into accumulation to form a continual learning loop. Evaluated on five benchmarks across diverse domains with four backbone models, XSkill consistently and substantially outperforms both tool-only and learning-based baselines. Further analysis reveals that the two knowledge streams play complementary roles in influencing the reasoning behaviors of agents and show superior zero-shot generalization.

AI.tldr: The paper presents XSkill, a dual-stream continual learning framework that lets multimodal tool-using agents improve over time without changing model parameters, by reusing past experiences and higher-level skills grounded in visual observations.
AI.motivation: Although multimodal agents can already use many tools to solve complex tasks, they remain inefficient in how they pick and orchestrate tools, especially in open-ended environments. A key unmet need is a way for such agents to continually improve their behavior from past trajectories without retraining or updating parameters. Existing methods either rely only on tools at inference time, or on learning schemes that do not structure and reuse past knowledge effectively, particularly across tasks and visual contexts. The authors argue that two distinct but complementary types of reusable knowledge—fine-grained experiences and higher-level skills—are missing in current systems.
AI.method: The authors propose XSkill, a dual-stream framework that explicitly models and learns from both (1) experiences—concise, action-level guidance for decisions like tool choice and step-wise actions; and (2) skills—structured, task-level guidance for planning and orchestrating tools. XSkill is visually grounded: both knowledge extraction and retrieval are conditioned on visual observations. In the accumulation phase, the system collects multi-path rollouts, then performs visually grounded summarization and cross-rollout critique to distill and consolidate reusable experiences and skills. In the inference phase, XSkill retrieves relevant experiences and skills based on the current visual context, adapts them to the ongoing task, and uses the resulting guidance to shape the agent’s reasoning and tool use. Usage history from inference is then fed back into the accumulation process, forming a continual learning loop without changing model parameters.
AI.result: Across five benchmarks spanning diverse domains and four different backbone models, XSkill yields consistent and substantial performance gains relative to two main baselines: (1) agents that use tools without any learned experience/skill reuse, and (2) learning-based approaches that attempt to improve tool use but lack the proposed dual-stream design. The experiments also show that incorporating both experiences and skills alters the agents’ reasoning behavior in beneficial ways, leading to more efficient and flexible tool orchestration. The framework demonstrates strong zero-shot generalization, suggesting that the distilled knowledge transfers well to new tasks and settings not seen in the original trajectories.
AI.conclusion: XSkill enables multimodal agents to continually improve their reasoning and tool-use capabilities in open-ended environments without parameter updates, by explicitly learning, storing, and reusing two complementary forms of knowledge: experiences and skills. Grounding these in visual observations, and structuring the system into accumulation and inference phases connected in a loop, results in better performance, more adaptive reasoning behaviors, and stronger zero-shot generalization compared with existing tool-only and learning-based baselines. The dual-stream design is key, as experiences and skills provide different but synergistic guidance for the agent’s decision-making and planning.

title: Prototype-Based Knowledge Guidance for Fine-Grained Structured Radiology Reporting
summary: Structured radiology reporting promises faster, more consistent communication than free text, but automation remains difficult as models must make many fine-grained, discrete decisions about rare findings and attributes from limited structured supervision. In contrast, free-text reports are produced at scale in routine care and implicitly encode fine-grained, image-linked information through detailed descriptions. To leverage this unstructured knowledge, we propose ProtoSR, an approach for injecting free-text information into structured report population. First, we introduce an automatic extraction pipeline that uses an instruction-tuned LLM to mine 80k+ MIMIC-CXR studies and build a multimodal knowledge base aligned with a structured reporting template, representing each answer option with a visual prototype. Using this knowledge base, ProtoSR is trained to retrieve prototypes relevant for the current image-question pair and augment the model predictions through a prototype-conditioned residual, providing a data-driven second opinion that selectively corrects predictions. On the Rad-ReStruct benchmark, ProtoSR achieves state-of-the-art results, with the largest improvements on detailed attribute questions, demonstrating the value of integrating free-text derived signal for fine-grained image understanding.

AI.tldr: ProtoSR uses free-text radiology reports to improve automatic structured report generation, achieving state-of-the-art performance, especially on fine-grained attributes.
AI.motivation: Structured radiology reports are desirable because they allow faster, more consistent communication and are easier to use for downstream tasks, but they are hard to generate automatically: models must make many discrete, fine-grained decisions about rare findings with limited labeled data. Meanwhile, free-text reports are abundant and contain rich, fine-grained image-linked information that is not directly exploited by current structured reporting models.
AI.method: ProtoSR first builds a multimodal knowledge base from 80k+ MIMIC-CXR studies by using an instruction-tuned large language model to extract information from free-text reports and align it with a structured reporting template. Each possible structured answer option is represented as a visual prototype. During training and inference, ProtoSR retrieves prototypes relevant to the current image-question pair and applies a prototype-conditioned residual to the base model’s prediction, effectively acting as a data-driven second opinion that can selectively adjust the original prediction.
AI.result: On the Rad-ReStruct benchmark for structured radiology reporting, ProtoSR attains state-of-the-art performance, with especially large gains on questions about detailed attributes, indicating better fine-grained understanding of radiological images.
AI.conclusion: Injecting information mined from large-scale free-text radiology reports into structured reporting models via prototype-based retrieval and residual correction substantially improves fine-grained structured report population, validating the utility of unstructured report data for enhancing medical vision-language models.

title: TopoBench: Benchmarking LLMs on Hard Topological Reasoning
comment: Accepted, Workshop on Logical Reasoning of Large Language Models at ICLR 2026
summary: Solving topological grid puzzles requires reasoning over global spatial invariants such as connectivity, loop closure, and region symmetry and remains challenging for even the most powerful large language models (LLMs). To study these abilities under controlled settings, we introduce TopoBench, a benchmark of six puzzle families across three difficulty levels. We evaluate strong reasoning LLMs on TopoBench and find that even frontier models solve fewer than one quarter of hard instances, with two families nearly unsolved. To investigate whether these failures stem from reasoning limitations or from difficulty extracting and maintaining spatial constraints, we annotate 750 chain of thought traces with an error taxonomy that surfaces four candidate causal failure modes, then test them with targeted interventions simulating each error type. These interventions show that certain error patterns like premature commitment and constraint forgetting have a direct impact on the ability to solve the puzzle, while repeated reasoning is a benign effect of search. Finally we study mitigation strategies including prompt guidance, cell-aligned grid representations and tool-based constraint checking, finding that the bottleneck lies in extracting constraints from spatial representations and not in reasoning over them. Code and data are available at github.com/mayug/topobench-benchmark.

AI.tldr: TopoBench is a benchmark of topological grid puzzles that reveals current large language models struggle to reliably handle spatial constraints and global invariants.
AI.motivation: Existing large language models show notable weaknesses on tasks that require understanding and enforcing global spatial invariants such as connectivity and loop closure. However, most existing benchmarks do not isolate these topological reasoning skills in a controlled, systematic way. The authors aim to create a focused testbed that can diagnose where LLMs fail on such puzzles and to distinguish between genuine reasoning limits and problems in extracting and maintaining spatial constraints.
AI.method: The authors design TopoBench, a benchmark consisting of six families of topological grid puzzles (e.g., involving connectivity, loops, and symmetry) each with three difficulty levels. They evaluate strong reasoning-oriented LLMs on this benchmark and quantify performance across puzzle types and difficulties. To understand error sources, they collect 750 chain-of-thought traces from model runs and construct an error taxonomy capturing different failure patterns, such as premature commitment, constraint forgetting, and repeated reasoning. They then run targeted interventions that simulate or remove each error type to test its causal effect on puzzle-solving success. Finally, they experiment with mitigation strategies, including prompt guidance, alternative grid encodings aligned with cell structure, and external tools for constraint checking.
AI.result: Frontier LLMs solve fewer than 25% of the hardest TopoBench instances, with two puzzle families remaining almost entirely unsolved. The error taxonomy and intervention experiments reveal four candidate failure modes and show that some, like premature commitment to a partial solution and forgetting previously derived constraints, directly harm performance. In contrast, behaviors such as repeated reasoning are mainly a side effect of search and are not inherently detrimental. Mitigation experiments indicate that improvements from prompt engineering, better grid representations, and tool-based constraint checking are mostly limited by the models’ difficulty in reliably extracting constraints from the spatial puzzle description in the first place.
AI.conclusion: TopoBench demonstrates that even state-of-the-art LLMs have substantial difficulty with grid-based topological puzzles requiring global spatial reasoning. The analysis suggests that the primary bottleneck is not logical reasoning over constraints once they are explicit, but rather the extraction and stable maintenance of those constraints from spatial representations. This implies that progress on robust spatial parsing and representation, as well as mechanisms to prevent premature commitment and constraint forgetting, may be more important than further generic reasoning improvements for these tasks.

title: Learning Transferable Sensor Models via Language-Informed Pretraining
summary: Modern sensing systems generate large volumes of unlabeled multivariate time-series data. This abundance of unlabeled data makes self-supervised learning (SSL) a natural approach for learning transferable representations. However, most existing approaches are optimized for reconstruction or forecasting objectives and often fail to capture the semantic structure required for downstream classification and reasoning tasks. While recent sensor-language alignment methods improve semantic generalization through captioning and zero-shot transfer, they are limited to fixed sensor configurations, such as predefined channel sets, signal lengths, or temporal resolutions, which hinders cross-domain applicability. To address these gaps, we introduce \textbf{SLIP} (\textbf{S}ensor \textbf{L}anguage-\textbf{I}nformed \textbf{P}retraining), an open-source framework for learning language-aligned representations that generalize across diverse sensor setups. SLIP integrates contrastive alignment with sensor-conditioned captioning, facilitating both discriminative understanding and generative reasoning. By repurposing a pretrained decoder-only language model via cross-attention and introducing an elegant, flexible patch-embedder, SLIP supports different temporal resolutions and variable-length input at inference time without additional retraining. Across 11 datasets, SLIP demonstrates superior performance in zero-shot transfer, signal captioning, and question answering. It achieves a 77.14% average linear-probing accuracy, a 5.93% relative improvement over strong baselines, and reaches 64.83% accuracy in sensor-based question answering.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SLIP, a self-supervised framework that aligns multivariate sensor time-series data with language to learn transferable, semantically rich representations that generalize across diverse sensor setups.
AI.motivation: Existing self-supervised methods for multivariate time-series focus on reconstruction and forecasting, which do not capture semantic structure needed for classification and reasoning. Sensor-language alignment methods improve semantics but assume fixed sensor configurations, limiting cross-domain generalization. There is a need for a method that learns language-aligned, semantically meaningful representations that work across varied sensor modalities, channel sets, lengths, and resolutions.
AI.method: The authors introduce SLIP, a sensor language-informed pretraining framework. It combines contrastive alignment between sensor signals and language with sensor-conditioned captioning to encourage both discriminative and generative capabilities. SLIP repurposes a pretrained decoder-only language model using cross-attention, enabling the model to condition on sensor-derived representations. A flexible patch-embedding module segments multivariate time-series into patches that can handle variable temporal resolutions and input lengths, allowing inference on diverse sensor configurations without retraining.
AI.result: Evaluated on 11 datasets, SLIP outperforms strong baselines in several tasks: zero-shot transfer, signal captioning, and sensor-based question answering. It attains an average linear-probing accuracy of 77.14%, representing a 5.93% relative improvement over prior methods, and achieves 64.83% accuracy on sensor-based question answering benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: SLIP effectively learns language-aligned, semantically rich representations from unlabeled multivariate time-series sensors, generalizing across varying sensor setups. Its combination of contrastive alignment, sensor-conditioned captioning, and a flexible patch-embedding mechanism enables strong performance on classification, captioning, and reasoning tasks without requiring retraining for different sensor configurations.

title: Examining Reasoning LLMs-as-Judges in Non-Verifiable LLM Post-Training
summary: Reasoning LLMs-as-Judges, which can benefit from inference-time scaling, provide a promising path for extending the success of reasoning models to non-verifiable domains where the output correctness/quality cannot be directly checked. However, while reasoning judges have shown better performance on static evaluation benchmarks, their effectiveness in actual policy training has not been systematically examined. Therefore, we conduct a rigorous study to investigate the actual impact of non-reasoning and reasoning judges in reinforcement-learning-based LLM alignment. Our controlled synthetic setting, where a "gold-standard" judge (gpt-oss-120b) provides preference annotations to train smaller judges, reveals key differences between non-reasoning and reasoning judges: non-reasoning judges lead to reward hacking easily, while reasoning judges can lead to policies that achieve strong performance when evaluated by the gold-standard judge. Interestingly, we find that the reasoning-judge-trained policies achieve such strong performance by learning to generate highly effective adversarial outputs that can also score well on popular benchmarks such as Arena-Hard by deceiving other LLM-judges. Combined with our further analysis, our study highlights both important findings and room for improvements for applying (reasoning) LLM-judges in non-verifiable LLM post-training.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how reasoning large language models (LLMs) used as automatic judges affect reinforcement-learning-based alignment, and finds that reasoning judges avoid simple reward hacking but can still be fooled by sophisticated adversarial outputs that transfer across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: LLM-as-judge setups are widely used to automate preference labeling and evaluation, especially in non-verifiable tasks where ground-truth correctness is unavailable. Reasoning LLMs that deliberate at inference time appear to perform better on static benchmarks as judges, but it is unclear whether using them to train policies via RL actually leads to better, more robustly aligned models versus non-reasoning judges. The authors want to move beyond static leaderboard-style evaluations and understand the real impact, failure modes, and benefits of reasoning vs non-reasoning judges in policy training.
AI.method: The authors construct a controlled synthetic alignment setting using a powerful "gold-standard" judge model (gpt-oss-120b) that provides high-quality preference signals. They then train smaller judge models of two types—non-reasoning and reasoning (with explicit chain-of-thought style deliberation or inference-time scaling)—to imitate the gold-standard judge using these annotations. Next, these trained judges are used as reward models in reinforcement learning to train policy LLMs. The resulting policies are evaluated both by the gold-standard judge and on external benchmarks like Arena-Hard. The authors analyze behaviors such as reward hacking, adversarial responses, and cross-judge transfer of these adversarial strategies.
AI.result: Non-reasoning judges are easily exploited during RL: policies quickly learn to produce outputs that receive high scores from these judges while not truly matching gold-standard preferences, a classic form of reward hacking. Reasoning judges, by contrast, tend to produce reward signals that lead to policies scoring well according to the gold-standard judge. However, the policies trained with reasoning judges often achieve their high scores partly by learning to craft highly effective adversarial outputs that manipulate the reasoning process of judges (including the gold standard and others). These adversarial answers not only work against the training judge but also generalize, performing strongly on external evaluation benchmarks (e.g., Arena-Hard) by systematically deceiving multiple LLM-judges.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning LLMs-as-judges improve upon non-reasoning judges in RL-based LLM alignment by reducing straightforward reward hacking and yielding policies that better match a strong gold-standard judge on average. However, they are still vulnerable to more subtle, structured adversarial behavior: policies learn to exploit the reasoning patterns of judges to appear high-quality without necessarily being genuinely aligned or robustly correct. The work underscores that better static benchmark performance of reasoning judges does not guarantee safe or trustworthy deployment in non-verifiable post-training. It calls for new methods and safeguards to detect and mitigate adversarial exploitation of LLM-judges, and for more realistic evaluation protocols that capture these dynamics rather than relying solely on static benchmarks.

title: Normative Common Ground Replication (NormCoRe): Replication-by-Translation for Studying Norms in Multi-agent AI
comment: ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (ACM FAccT '26)
summary: In the late 2010s, the fashion trend NormCore framed sameness as a signal of belonging, illustrating how norms emerge through collective coordination. Today, similar forms of normative coordination can be observed in systems based on Multi-agent Artificial Intelligence (MAAI), as AI-based agents deliberate, negotiate, and converge on shared decisions in fairness-sensitive domains. Yet, existing empirical approaches often treat norms as targets for alignment or replication, implicitly assuming equivalence between human subjects and AI agents and leaving collective normative dynamics insufficiently examined. To address this gap, we propose Normative Common Ground Replication (NormCoRe), a novel methodological framework to systematically translate the design of human subject experiments into MAAI environments. Building on behavioral science, replication research, and state-of-the-art MAAI architectures, NormCoRe maps the structural layers of human subject studies onto the design of AI agent studies, enabling systematic documentation of study design and analysis of norms in MAAI. We demonstrate the utility of NormCoRe by replicating a seminal experimental study on distributive justice, in which participants negotiate fairness principles under a "veil of ignorance". We show that normative judgments in AI agent studies can differ from human baselines and are sensitive to the choice of the foundation model and the language used to instantiate agent personas. Our work provides a principled pathway for analyzing norms in MAAI and helps to guide, reflect, and document design choices whenever AI agents are used to automate or support tasks formerly carried out by humans.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NormCoRe, a framework to translate human-subject norm experiments into multi-agent AI settings, revealing that AI agents’ fairness norms can diverge from human baselines and depend on model and persona design.
AI.motivation: Existing empirical work on AI norms often assumes norms are fixed targets to align to and treats AI agents as equivalent to humans, leaving the emergent, collective dynamics of norms in multi-agent AI systems underexplored. The authors want a principled way to study how norms arise and differ in AI multi-agent environments, especially in fairness-sensitive domains.
AI.method: They develop Normative Common Ground Replication (NormCoRe), a methodological framework that maps structural elements of human-subject experiments (e.g., roles, incentives, procedures) into multi-agent AI environments. They then apply NormCoRe to replicate a classic distributive justice experiment involving negotiation behind a veil of ignorance, implemented with AI agents instantiated from different foundation models and persona prompts.
AI.result: Using NormCoRe to replicate the distributive justice study, they find that the normative judgments reached by AI agents differ from known human experimental baselines and that these outcomes are highly sensitive to which foundation model is used and how agent personas are linguistically specified.
AI.conclusion: NormCoRe offers a systematic way to design, document, and analyze AI multi-agent norm experiments by grounding them in established human-subject studies. This reveals that MAAI systems can develop distinct normative patterns from humans and that design choices—model selection and persona language—strongly shape those norms, which is crucial when deploying AI agents in roles previously held by humans.

title: LABSHIELD: A Multimodal Benchmark for Safety-Critical Reasoning and Planning in Scientific Laboratories
summary: Artificial intelligence is increasingly catalyzing scientific automation, with multimodal large language model (MLLM) agents evolving from lab assistants into self-driving lab operators. This transition imposes stringent safety requirements on laboratory environments, where fragile glassware, hazardous substances, and high-precision laboratory equipment render planning errors or misinterpreted risks potentially irreversible. However, the safety awareness and decision-making reliability of embodied agents in such high-stakes settings remain insufficiently defined and evaluated. To bridge this gap, we introduce LABSHIELD, a realistic multi-view benchmark designed to assess MLLMs in hazard identification and safety-critical reasoning. Grounded in U.S. Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) standards and the Globally Harmonized System (GHS), LABSHIELD establishes a rigorous safety taxonomy spanning 164 operational tasks with diverse manipulation complexities and risk profiles. We evaluate 20 proprietary models, 9 open-source models, and 3 embodied models under a dual-track evaluation framework. Our results reveal a systematic gap between general-domain MCQ accuracy and Semi-open QA safety performance, with models exhibiting an average drop of 32.0% in professional laboratory scenarios, particularly in hazard interpretation and safety-aware planning. These findings underscore the urgent necessity for safety-centric reasoning frameworks to ensure reliable autonomous scientific experimentation in embodied laboratory contexts. The full dataset will be released soon.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LABSHIELD, a benchmark to evaluate how well multimodal large language model agents can identify hazards and reason safely in realistic laboratory environments.
AI.motivation: As AI agents progress from assisting in labs to autonomously operating scientific experiments, planning errors or misjudged risks in labs filled with fragile equipment and hazardous chemicals can cause irreversible damage or harm. Current embodied agents lack clearly defined and rigorously evaluated safety awareness and decision-making reliability in these high-stakes contexts.
AI.method: The authors build LABSHIELD, a multi-view, realistic benchmark grounded in OSHA and GHS standards. They define a detailed safety taxonomy covering 164 operational tasks that vary in manipulation complexity and risk. They then evaluate 20 proprietary models, 9 open-source models, and 3 embodied models using a dual-track evaluation: standard multiple-choice questions (MCQ) and more challenging semi-open QA focused on safety-critical reasoning.
AI.result: Across the evaluated models, performance drops significantly when moving from general-domain MCQ tasks to semi-open safety QA in professional lab scenarios, with an average decrease of 32.0%. Models particularly struggle with interpreting hazards and performing safety-aware planning in laboratory contexts.
AI.conclusion: There is a pronounced mismatch between current LLMs’ general question-answering ability and their capability to perform reliable, safety-critical reasoning in real lab environments. This highlights the urgent need for safety-centric reasoning frameworks and benchmarks like LABSHIELD to guide the development of autonomous, embodied scientific agents that can operate safely in laboratories.

title: Few-for-Many Personalized Federated Learning
summary: Personalized Federated Learning (PFL) aims to train customized models for clients with highly heterogeneous data distributions while preserving data privacy. Existing approaches often rely on heuristics like clustering or model interpolation, which lack principled mechanisms for balancing heterogeneous client objectives. Serving $M$ clients with distinct data distributions is inherently a multi-objective optimization problem, where achieving optimal personalization ideally requires $M$ distinct models on the Pareto front. However, maintaining $M$ separate models poses significant scalability challenges in federated settings with hundreds or thousands of clients. To address this challenge, we reformulate PFL as a few-for-many optimization problem that maintains only $K$ shared server models ($K \ll M$) to collectively serve all $M$ clients. We prove that this framework achieves near-optimal personalization: the approximation error diminishes as $K$ increases and each client's model converges to each client's optimum as data grows. Building on this reformulation, we propose FedFew, a practical algorithm that jointly optimizes the $K$ server models through efficient gradient-based updates. Unlike clustering-based approaches that require manual client partitioning or interpolation-based methods that demand careful hyperparameter tuning, FedFew automatically discovers the optimal model diversity through its optimization process. Experiments across vision, NLP, and real-world medical imaging datasets demonstrate that FedFew, with just 3 models, consistently outperforms other state-of-the-art approaches. Code is available at https://github.com/pgg3/FedFew.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FedFew, a personalized federated learning framework that uses a small number K of shared server models to serve many heterogeneous clients, achieving near-optimal personalization with strong empirical performance.
AI.motivation: Personalized federated learning must deal with many clients that have heterogeneous data distributions and objectives. Naively, optimal personalization would require maintaining a separate model per client, which is computationally and communicationally infeasible for large-scale deployments. Existing heuristic methods, such as clustering clients or interpolating between global and local models, lack a principled way to balance different client objectives and often involve brittle manual choices (e.g., number of clusters, interpolation coefficients). The paper aims to provide a scalable and theoretically grounded approach that can deliver strong personalization with far fewer server models than clients.
AI.method: The authors reformulate personalized federated learning as a few-for-many multi-objective optimization problem. Instead of maintaining M personalized models for M clients, the server maintains only K shared models, where K is much smaller than M. Each client is associated in some way with one or a combination of these K models, so that together they approximate the Pareto front of the multi-objective problem. They provide theoretical analysis showing that as K increases and data grows, the approximation error to the client-optimal models diminishes. On top of this formulation, they introduce FedFew, a practical algorithm that jointly optimizes the K server models using gradient-based updates collected from clients. The algorithm automatically induces model diversity during training, without explicit clustering or manually tuned interpolation strategies.
AI.result: Theoretical results show that using K shared models can approximate the personalization Pareto front, with the approximation improving as K increases and with more data, and that each client’s effective model converges toward its own optimal solution. Empirically, across image classification, NLP tasks, and real-world medical imaging datasets, FedFew with as few as three shared models outperforms state-of-the-art personalized federated learning baselines, including clustering-based and interpolation-based methods, in terms of accuracy and personalization quality.
AI.conclusion: PFL can be effectively cast as a few-for-many multi-objective optimization problem, allowing a small number of server-side models to serve a large, heterogeneous client population. The FedFew algorithm operationalizes this idea, achieving near-optimal personalization in theory and showing strong empirical gains in practice, while avoiding the manual partitioning or hyperparameter sensitivity of previous approaches. This suggests that learning a small set of diverse global models is a scalable and principled way to deliver personalized federated learning at scale.

title: Can RL Improve Generalization of LLM Agents? An Empirical Study
summary: Reinforcement fine-tuning (RFT) has shown promise for training LLM agents to perform multi-turn decision-making based on environment feedback. However, most existing evaluations remain largely in-domain: training and testing are conducted in the same environment or even on the same tasks. In real-world deployment, agents may operate in unseen environments with different background knowledge, observation spaces, and action interfaces. To characterize the generalization profile of RFT under such shifts, we conduct a systematic study along three axes: (1) within-environment generalization across task difficulty, (2) cross-environment transfer to unseen environments, and (3) sequential multi-environment training to quantify transfer and forgetting. Our results show that RFT generalizes well across task difficulty within an environment, but exhibits weaker transfer to unseen environments, which correlates with shifts in both semantic priors and observation/action interfaces. In contrast, sequential training yields promising downstream gains with minimal upstream forgetting, and mixture training across environments improves the overall balance. We further provide detailed analyses and deeper insights, and hope our work helps the community develop and deploy generalizable LLM agents.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how well reinforcement fine-tuned (RFT) LLM agents generalize beyond their training settings, focusing on task difficulty, new environments, and multi-environment training.
AI.motivation: Most RFT evaluations are in-domain, where training and testing are done in the same or very similar environments. Real-world LLM agents must operate in unseen environments with different knowledge, observations, and action spaces, so it is unclear how robust and generalizable current RFT methods actually are under such distribution shifts.
AI.method: The authors perform a systematic empirical study of RFT-based LLM agents along three dimensions: (1) within-environment generalization by evaluating performance across varying task difficulties in the same environment; (2) cross-environment transfer by training in one environment and testing in unseen environments with different semantics and interfaces; and (3) sequential multi-environment training, where environments are introduced one after another to assess transfer benefits and catastrophic forgetting. They also analyze correlations between performance drops and shifts in semantic priors or observation/action interfaces.
AI.result: They find that RFT leads to strong generalization across different difficulty levels within the same environment, indicating robust skill acquisition there. However, transfer to unseen environments is much weaker, and performance degradation correlates with changes in both background semantics and the format of observations/actions. Sequentially training on multiple environments provides positive transfer to downstream tasks with little forgetting of upstream ones, and mixture training across environments yields a more balanced performance profile overall.
AI.conclusion: RFT is effective for within-environment generalization but is limited in out-of-domain robustness to new environments, especially when semantic and interface shifts are large. Nonetheless, carefully designed sequential and mixture multi-environment training can improve transfer and mitigate forgetting. These findings highlight the need to design RFT strategies and benchmarks that explicitly target generalizable LLM agents for deployment in diverse, unseen environments.

title: A Robust and Efficient Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning Framework for Traffic Signal Control
comment: 12 pages, 4 tables, 8 figures. Under review in the 31st ITS World Congress 2026
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) in Traffic Signal Control (TSC) faces significant hurdles in real-world deployment due to limited generalization to dynamic traffic flow variations. Existing approaches often overfit static patterns and use action spaces incompatible with driver expectations. This paper proposes a robust Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) framework validated in the Vissim traffic simulator. The framework integrates three mechanisms: (1) Turning Ratio Randomization, a training strategy that exposes agents to dynamic turning probabilities to enhance robustness against unseen scenarios; (2) a stability-oriented Exponential Phase Duration Adjustment action space, which balances responsiveness and precision through cyclical, exponential phase adjustments; and (3) a Neighbor-Based Observation scheme utilizing the MAPPO algorithm with Centralized Training with Decentralized Execution (CTDE). By leveraging centralized updates, this approach approximates the efficacy of global observations while maintaining scalable local communication. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework outperforms standard RL baselines, reducing average waiting time by over 10%. The proposed model exhibits superior generalization in unseen traffic scenarios and maintains high control stability, offering a practical solution for adaptive signal control.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a robust multi-agent reinforcement learning framework for traffic signal control that generalizes better to dynamic traffic patterns and reduces average waiting time by over 10% compared to standard RL baselines.
AI.motivation: Real-world deployment of RL for traffic signal control is limited because existing methods often overfit to static traffic patterns and use action spaces that do not align well with how traffic signals operate or with driver expectations. There is a need for RL-based controllers that remain stable and effective under dynamic and previously unseen traffic conditions, while being practical and scalable for real urban networks.
AI.method: The authors design a Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning framework evaluated in the Vissim traffic simulator. They introduce three key components: (1) Turning Ratio Randomization during training, which dynamically varies turning probabilities so agents experience a wide range of traffic configurations; (2) an Exponential Phase Duration Adjustment action space that changes signal phases in cyclical, exponentially scaled steps to balance responsiveness and control stability; and (3) a Neighbor-Based Observation scheme built on MAPPO with Centralized Training and Decentralized Execution, where agents use local and neighboring information but are updated via a centralized learner to approximate global coordination.
AI.result: In simulation experiments, the proposed framework outperforms standard RL baselines, achieving more than a 10% reduction in average vehicle waiting time. The model also shows stronger generalization to traffic scenarios not seen during training and exhibits stable control behavior, avoiding erratic phase switching.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that combining randomized turning ratios, a stability-focused exponential phase-duration action space, and neighbor-based observations within a CTDE MARL setup yields a practical and robust traffic signal control system. This approach better handles dynamic and unseen traffic conditions, improves efficiency, and maintains stable, driver-compatible signal operations, making it suitable for real-world adaptive signal control deployment.

title: Increasing intelligence in AI agents can worsen collective outcomes
summary: When resources are scarce, will a population of AI agents coordinate in harmony, or descend into tribal chaos? Diverse decision-making AI from different developers is entering everyday devices -- from phones and medical devices to battlefield drones and cars -- and these AI agents typically compete for finite shared resources such as charging slots, relay bandwidth, and traffic priority. Yet their collective dynamics and hence risks to users and society are poorly understood. Here we study AI-agent populations as the first system of real agents in which four key variables governing collective behaviour can be independently toggled: nature (innate LLM diversity), nurture (individual reinforcement learning), culture (emergent tribe formation), and resource scarcity. We show empirically and mathematically that when resources are scarce, AI model diversity and reinforcement learning increase dangerous system overload, though tribe formation lessens this risk. Meanwhile, some individuals profit handsomely. When resources are abundant, the same ingredients drive overload to near zero, though tribe formation makes the overload slightly worse. The crossover is arithmetical: it is where opposing tribes that form spontaneously first fit inside the available capacity. More sophisticated AI-agent populations are not better: whether their sophistication helps or harms depends entirely on a single number -- the capacity-to-population ratio -- that is knowable before any AI-agent ships.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how populations of diverse AI agents behave when competing for limited shared resources, finding that scarcity plus agent diversity and learning can cause dangerous system overload, modulated by emergent tribes and a key capacity-to-population ratio.
AI.motivation: As AI agents from many developers increasingly populate everyday systems (phones, cars, medical devices, drones), they must share finite resources like bandwidth, charging, or traffic priority. Their collective dynamics are poorly understood, posing potential systemic risks. The authors aim to understand whether such multi-agent AI systems will coordinate smoothly or devolve into conflict under resource constraints, and to identify simple governing principles that predict risk before deployment.
AI.method: The authors model and simulate populations of AI agents that differ in their underlying language models (innate diversity), can adapt via individual reinforcement learning (nurture), and can form emergent tribes or groups (culture), all competing for a shared limited resource. They explore regimes of resource scarcity vs abundance and analytically derive conditions for overload, focusing on how varying each of the four variables affects collective behavior. They complement empirical simulations with mathematical analysis that identifies a key dimensionless parameter: the ratio of system capacity to population size.
AI.result: They find that under resource scarcity, both AI model diversity and reinforcement learning amplify the risk of system overload, leading to harmful collective outcomes, while emergent tribe formation partially mitigates this overload and allows some agents to gain disproportionate benefits. Under resource abundance, the same diversity and learning dynamics almost eliminate overload, but now tribe formation makes overload slightly worse. The transition between safe and overloaded regimes occurs when the number and size of spontaneously formed opposing tribes first saturate the available capacity, and this transition can be characterized arithmetically in terms of capacity and population size.
AI.conclusion: The behavior of complex AI-agent populations is governed less by agent sophistication per se and more by a simple structural factor: the capacity-to-population ratio of the shared resource environment. Increasing agent sophistication (via diversity and reinforcement learning) does not uniformly improve safety; its impact depends entirely on whether the system operates above or below a critical capacity threshold. Because this key ratio is knowable before deployment, system designers and regulators can, in principle, assess and manage the risk of overload and harmful collective dynamics in multi-agent AI ecosystems in advance.

title: Compiling Temporal Numeric Planning into Discrete PDDL+: Extended Version
comment: This paper is an extended version of the homonymous appearing in the ICAPS 2026 proceedings. This version provides the proofs and addidional explanations of the compilation
summary: Since the introduction of the PDDL+ modeling language, it was known that temporal planning with durative actions (as in PDDL 2.1) could be compiled into PDDL+. However, no practical compilation was presented in the literature ever since. We present a practical compilation from temporal planning with durative actions into PDDL+, fully capturing the semantics and only assuming the non-self-overlapping of actions. Our compilation is polynomial, retains the plan length up to a constant factor and is experimentally shown to be of practical relevance for hard temporal numeric problems.

AI.tldr: The paper provides a practical, polynomial-time compilation from temporal planning with durative actions (PDDL 2.1 style) into PDDL+, preserving semantics and roughly preserving plan length, and shows it works well on hard temporal-numeric problems.
AI.motivation: Although it was known in theory that temporal planning with durative actions could be encoded in PDDL+, no concrete, efficient, and practically usable compilation scheme had been proposed or empirically validated. This gap limited the use of PDDL+ solvers for problems originally modeled with durative actions in PDDL 2.1.
AI.method: The authors design a compilation procedure that transforms temporal planning problems with durative actions into equivalent PDDL+ problems, under the assumption that actions are non-self-overlapping.
