The compilation preserves the semantics of durative actions, operates in polynomial time, and keeps plan length within a constant factor. They then implement this compilation and run experiments on challenging temporal-numeric benchmarks to evaluate practical performance.
AI.result: The resulting PDDL+ encodings are solvable in practice by existing PDDL+ planners, and the performance on hard temporal-numeric problems demonstrates that the compilation is feasible and competitive. The polynomial overhead and near-preservation of plan length are confirmed empirically to be manageable.
AI.conclusion: A fully semantic-preserving and practical compilation from temporal planning with durative actions into PDDL+ is possible and useful. This allows leveraging PDDL+ planners for a broader class of temporal planning problems and supports the view that PDDL+ is a sufficiently expressive target language for temporal planning, under mild assumptions like non-self-overlapping actions.

title: Portfolio of Solving Strategies in CEGAR-based Object Packing and Scheduling for Sequential 3D Printing
comment: arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2503.05071
summary: Computing power that used to be available only in supercomputers decades ago especially their parallelism is currently available in standard personal computer CPUs even in CPUs for mobile telephones. We show how to effectively utilize the computing power of modern multi-core personal computer CPU to solve the complex combinatorial problem of object arrangement and scheduling for sequential 3D printing. We achieved this by parallelizing the existing CEGAR-SEQ algorithm that solves the sequential object arrangement and scheduling by expressing it as a linear arithmetic formula which is then solved by a technique inspired by counterexample guided abstraction refinement (CEGAR). The original CEGAR-SEQ algorithm uses an object arrangement strategy that places objects towards the center of the printing plate. We propose alternative object arrangement strategies such as placing objects towards a corner of the printing plate and scheduling objects according to their height. Our parallelization is done at the high-level where we execute the CEGAR-SEQ algorithm in parallel with a portfolio of object arrangement strategies, an algorithm is called Porfolio-CEGAR-SEQ. Our experimental evaluation indicates that Porfolio-CEGAR-SEQ outperforms the original CEGAR-SEQ. When a batch of objects for multiple printing plates is scheduled, Portfolio-CEGAR-SEQ often uses fewer printing plates than CEGAR-SEQ.

AI.tldr: The paper parallelizes and enhances the CEGAR-SEQ algorithm for arranging and scheduling objects in sequential 3D printing, using multi-core CPUs and a portfolio of placement strategies to reduce the number of required printing plates and improve performance.
AI.motivation: Modern consumer and mobile CPUs now offer supercomputer-like parallelism, but many complex combinatorial problems in 3D printing, such as optimizing object arrangement and scheduling on printing plates, still struggle to exploit this computing power efficiently. Existing algorithms like CEGAR-SEQ solve these problems but use a single, fixed placement strategy and run sequentially, which can limit scalability and solution quality, especially when scheduling many objects across multiple plates. There is a need to both better leverage multi-core architectures and explore diverse arrangement heuristics to improve throughput and resource usage in 3D printing workflows.
AI.method: The authors parallelize the existing CEGAR-SEQ algorithm, which formulates the sequential object arrangement and scheduling problem as a linear arithmetic formula and solves it using a CEGAR-inspired refinement approach. They introduce alternative object arrangement strategies (e.g., placing objects toward a corner instead of the center, and scheduling based on object height) and execute multiple instances of CEGAR-SEQ in parallel, each with a different strategy, forming a portfolio approach called Portfolio-CEGAR-SEQ. Parallelization is done at a high algorithmic level rather than low-level thread management, using multi-core CPUs to run these diverse strategies concurrently and compete/cooperate to find better solutions faster.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation shows that Portfolio-CEGAR-SEQ consistently outperforms the original, single-strategy CEGAR-SEQ in solving the object arrangement and scheduling problem for sequential 3D printing. In particular, when scheduling batches of objects across multiple printing plates, the portfolio approach often reduces the number of plates required compared to the baseline, demonstrating both computational and practical efficiency gains. The results confirm that leveraging multi-core CPU parallelism together with a diverse set of arrangement heuristics yields better optimization outcomes than a single, centrally-biased placement strategy.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that high-level parallelization of the CEGAR-SEQ algorithm via a portfolio of object arrangement strategies is an effective way to exploit modern multi-core CPU capabilities for complex 3D printing scheduling tasks. The Portfolio-CEGAR-SEQ approach not only accelerates solving but also improves solution quality, frequently using fewer printing plates for object batches. The work underscores the benefits of combining CEGAR-style formal optimization with heuristic diversity and parallel execution, and suggests that similar portfolio-based parallelization could be beneficial for other combinatorial optimization problems in manufacturing and beyond.
